speeches.speech: Sir Hugh, persuade me not; I will make a Star- chamber matter of it: if he were twenty Sir John Falstaffs, he shall not abuse Robert Shallow, esquire.
lines: Sir Hugh, persuade me not; I will make a Star-
chamber matter of it: if he were twenty Sir John
Falstaffs, he shall not abuse Robert Shallow, esquire.

speeches.speech: In the county of Gloucester, justice of peace and 'Coram.'
lines: In the county of Gloucester, justice of peace and
'Coram.'

speeches.speech: Ay, cousin Slender, and 'Custalourum.
lines: Ay, cousin Slender, and 'Custalourum.

speeches.speech: Ay, and 'Rato-lorum' too; and a gentleman born, master parson; who writes himself 'Armigero,' in any bill, warrant, quittance, or obligation, 'Armigero.'
lines: Ay, and 'Rato-lorum' too; and a gentleman born,
master parson; who writes himself 'Armigero,' in any
bill, warrant, quittance, or obligation, 'Armigero.'

speeches.speech: Ay, that I do; and have done any time these three hundred years.
lines: Ay, that I do; and have done any time these three
hundred years.

speeches.speech: All his successors gone before him hath done't; and all his ancestors that come after him may: they may give the dozen white luces in their coat.
lines: All his successors gone before him hath done't; and
all his ancestors that come after him may: they may
give the dozen white luces in their coat.

speeches.speech: It is an old coat.
lines: It is an old coat.

speeches.speech: The dozen white louses do become an old coat well; it agrees well, passant; it is a familiar beast to man, and signifies love.
lines: The dozen white louses do become an old coat well;
it agrees well, passant; it is a familiar beast to
man, and signifies love.

speeches.speech: The luce is the fresh fish; the salt fish is an old coat.
lines: The luce is the fresh fish; the salt fish is an old coat.

speeches.speech: I may quarter, coz.
lines: I may quarter, coz.

speeches.speech: You may, by marrying.
lines: You may, by marrying.

speeches.speech: It is marring indeed, if he quarter it.
lines: It is marring indeed, if he quarter it.

speeches.speech: Not a whit.
lines: Not a whit.

speeches.speech: Yes, py'r lady; if he has a quarter of your coat, there is but three skirts for yourself, in my simple conjectures: but that is all one. If Sir John Falstaff have committed disparagements unto you, I am of the church, and will be glad to do my benevolence to make atonements and compremises between you.
lines: Yes, py'r lady; if he has a quarter of your coat,
there is but three skirts for yourself, in my
simple conjectures: but that is all one. If Sir
John Falstaff have committed disparagements unto
you, I am of the church, and will be glad to do my
benevolence to make atonements and compremises
between you.

speeches.speech: The council shall bear it; it is a riot.
lines: The council shall bear it; it is a riot.

speeches.speech: It is not meet the council hear a riot; there is no fear of Got in a riot: the council, look you, shall desire to hear the fear of Got, and not to hear a riot; take your vizaments in that.
lines: It is not meet the council hear a riot; there is no
fear of Got in a riot: the council, look you, shall
desire to hear the fear of Got, and not to hear a
riot; take your vizaments in that.

speeches.speech: Ha! o' my life, if I were young again, the sword should end it.
lines: Ha! o' my life, if I were young again, the sword
should end it.

speeches.speech: It is petter that friends is the sword, and end it: and there is also another device in my prain, which peradventure prings goot discretions with it: there is Anne Page, which is daughter to Master Thomas Page, which is pretty virginity.
lines: It is petter that friends is the sword, and end it:
and there is also another device in my prain, which
peradventure prings goot discretions with it: there
is Anne Page, which is daughter to Master Thomas
Page, which is pretty virginity.

speeches.speech: Mistress Anne Page? She has brown hair, and speaks small like a woman.
lines: Mistress Anne Page? She has brown hair, and speaks
small like a woman.

speeches.speech: It is that fery person for all the orld, as just as you will desire; and seven hundred pounds of moneys, and gold and silver, is her grandsire upon his death's-bed--Got deliver to a joyful resurrections! --give, when she is able to overtake seventeen years old: it were a goot motion if we leave our pribbles and prabbles, and desire a marriage between Master Abraham and Mistress Anne Page.
lines: It is that fery person for all the orld, as just as
you will desire; and seven hundred pounds of moneys,
and gold and silver, is her grandsire upon his
death's-bed--Got deliver to a joyful resurrections!
--give, when she is able to overtake seventeen years
old: it were a goot motion if we leave our pribbles
and prabbles, and desire a marriage between Master
Abraham and Mistress Anne Page.

speeches.speech: Did her grandsire leave her seven hundred pound?
lines: Did her grandsire leave her seven hundred pound?

speeches.speech: Ay, and her father is make her a petter penny.
lines: Ay, and her father is make her a petter penny.

speeches.speech: I know the young gentlewoman; she has good gifts.
lines: I know the young gentlewoman; she has good gifts.

speeches.speech: Seven hundred pounds and possibilities is goot gifts.
lines: Seven hundred pounds and possibilities is goot gifts.

speeches.speech: Well, let us see honest Master Page. Is Falstaff there?
lines: Well, let us see honest Master Page. Is Falstaff there?

speeches.speech: Shall I tell you a lie? I do despise a liar as I do despise one that is false, or as I despise one that is not true. The knight, Sir John, is there; and, I beseech you, be ruled by your well-willers. I will peat the door for Master Page. [Knocks] What, hoa! Got pless your house here!
lines: Shall I tell you a lie? I do despise a liar as I do
despise one that is false, or as I despise one that
is not true. The knight, Sir John, is there; and, I
beseech you, be ruled by your well-willers. I will
peat the door for Master Page.
[Knocks]
What, hoa! Got pless your house here!

speeches.speech: [Within] Who's there?
lines: [Within] Who's there?

speeches.speech: Here is Got's plessing, and your friend, and Justice Shallow; and here young Master Slender, that peradventures shall tell you another tale, if matters grow to your likings.
lines: Here is Got's plessing, and your friend, and Justice
Shallow; and here young Master Slender, that
peradventures shall tell you another tale, if
matters grow to your likings.

speeches.speech: I am glad to see your worships well. I thank you for my venison, Master Shallow.
lines: I am glad to see your worships well.
I thank you for my venison, Master Shallow.

speeches.speech: Master Page, I am glad to see you: much good do it your good heart! I wished your venison better; it was ill killed. How doth good Mistress Page?--and I thank you always with my heart, la! with my heart.
lines: Master Page, I am glad to see you: much good do it
your good heart! I wished your venison better; it
was ill killed. How doth good Mistress Page?--and I
thank you always with my heart, la! with my heart.

speeches.speech: Sir, I thank you.
lines: Sir, I thank you.

speeches.speech: Sir, I thank you; by yea and no, I do.
lines: Sir, I thank you; by yea and no, I do.

speeches.speech: I am glad to see you, good Master Slender.
lines: I am glad to see you, good Master Slender.

speeches.speech: How does your fallow greyhound, sir? I heard say he was outrun on Cotsall.
lines: How does your fallow greyhound, sir? I heard say he
was outrun on Cotsall.

speeches.speech: It could not be judged, sir.
lines: It could not be judged, sir.

speeches.speech: You'll not confess, you'll not confess.
lines: You'll not confess, you'll not confess.

speeches.speech: That he will not. 'Tis your fault, 'tis your fault; 'tis a good dog.
lines: That he will not. 'Tis your fault, 'tis your fault;
'tis a good dog.

speeches.speech: A cur, sir.
lines: A cur, sir.

speeches.speech: Sir, he's a good dog, and a fair dog: can there be more said? he is good and fair. Is Sir John Falstaff here?
lines: Sir, he's a good dog, and a fair dog: can there be
more said? he is good and fair. Is Sir John
Falstaff here?

speeches.speech: Sir, he is within; and I would I could do a good office between you.
lines: Sir, he is within; and I would I could do a good
office between you.

speeches.speech: It is spoke as a Christians ought to speak.
lines: It is spoke as a Christians ought to speak.

speeches.speech: He hath wronged me, Master Page.
lines: He hath wronged me, Master Page.

speeches.speech: Sir, he doth in some sort confess it.
lines: Sir, he doth in some sort confess it.

speeches.speech: If it be confessed, it is not redress'd: is not that so, Master Page? He hath wronged me; indeed he hath, at a word, he hath, believe me: Robert Shallow, esquire, saith, he is wronged.
lines: If it be confessed, it is not redress'd: is not that
so, Master Page? He hath wronged me; indeed he
hath, at a word, he hath, believe me: Robert
Shallow, esquire, saith, he is wronged.

speeches.speech: Here comes Sir John.
lines: Here comes Sir John.

speeches.speech: Now, Master Shallow, you'll complain of me to the king?
lines: Now, Master Shallow, you'll complain of me to the king?

speeches.speech: Knight, you have beaten my men, killed my deer, and broke open my lodge.
lines: Knight, you have beaten my men, killed my deer, and
broke open my lodge.

speeches.speech: But not kissed your keeper's daughter?
lines: But not kissed your keeper's daughter?

speeches.speech: Tut, a pin! this shall be answered.
lines: Tut, a pin! this shall be answered.

speeches.speech: I will answer it straight; I have done all this. That is now answered.
lines: I will answer it straight; I have done all this.
That is now answered.

speeches.speech: The council shall know this.
lines: The council shall know this.

speeches.speech: 'Twere better for you if it were known in counsel: you'll be laughed at.
lines: 'Twere better for you if it were known in counsel:
you'll be laughed at.

speeches.speech: Pauca verba, Sir John; goot worts.
lines: Pauca verba, Sir John; goot worts.

speeches.speech: Good worts! good cabbage. Slender, I broke your head: what matter have you against me?
lines: Good worts! good cabbage. Slender, I broke your
head: what matter have you against me?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, I have matter in my head against you; and against your cony-catching rascals, Bardolph, Nym, and Pistol.
lines: Marry, sir, I have matter in my head against you;
and against your cony-catching rascals, Bardolph,
Nym, and Pistol.

speeches.speech: You Banbury cheese!
lines: You Banbury cheese!

speeches.speech: Ay, it is no matter.
lines: Ay, it is no matter.

speeches.speech: How now, Mephostophilus!
lines: How now, Mephostophilus!

speeches.speech: Ay, it is no matter.
lines: Ay, it is no matter.

speeches.speech: Slice, I say! pauca, pauca: slice! that's my humour.
lines: Slice, I say! pauca, pauca: slice! that's my humour.

speeches.speech: Where's Simple, my man? Can you tell, cousin?
lines: Where's Simple, my man? Can you tell, cousin?

speeches.speech: Peace, I pray you. Now let us understand. There is three umpires in this matter, as I understand; that is, Master Page, fidelicet Master Page; and there is myself, fidelicet myself; and the three party is, lastly and finally, mine host of the Garter.
lines: Peace, I pray you. Now let us understand. There is
three umpires in this matter, as I understand; that
is, Master Page, fidelicet Master Page; and there is
myself, fidelicet myself; and the three party is,
lastly and finally, mine host of the Garter.

speeches.speech: We three, to hear it and end it between them.
lines: We three, to hear it and end it between them.

speeches.speech: Fery goot: I will make a prief of it in my note- book; and we will afterwards ork upon the cause with as great discreetly as we can.
lines: Fery goot: I will make a prief of it in my note-
book; and we will afterwards ork upon the cause with
as great discreetly as we can.

speeches.speech: Pistol!
lines: Pistol!

speeches.speech: He hears with ears.
lines: He hears with ears.

speeches.speech: The tevil and his tam! what phrase is this, 'He hears with ear'? why, it is affectations.
lines: The tevil and his tam! what phrase is this, 'He
hears with ear'? why, it is affectations.

speeches.speech: Pistol, did you pick Master Slender's purse?
lines: Pistol, did you pick Master Slender's purse?

speeches.speech: Ay, by these gloves, did he, or I would I might never come in mine own great chamber again else, of seven groats in mill-sixpences, and two Edward shovel-boards, that cost me two shilling and two pence apiece of Yead Miller, by these gloves.
lines: Ay, by these gloves, did he, or I would I might
never come in mine own great chamber again else, of
seven groats in mill-sixpences, and two Edward
shovel-boards, that cost me two shilling and two
pence apiece of Yead Miller, by these gloves.

speeches.speech: Is this true, Pistol?
lines: Is this true, Pistol?

speeches.speech: No; it is false, if it is a pick-purse.
lines: No; it is false, if it is a pick-purse.

speeches.speech: Ha, thou mountain-foreigner! Sir John and Master mine, I combat challenge of this latten bilbo. Word of denial in thy labras here! Word of denial: froth and scum, thou liest!
lines: Ha, thou mountain-foreigner! Sir John and Master mine,
I combat challenge of this latten bilbo.
Word of denial in thy labras here!
Word of denial: froth and scum, thou liest!

speeches.speech: By these gloves, then, 'twas he.
lines: By these gloves, then, 'twas he.

speeches.speech: Be avised, sir, and pass good humours: I will say 'marry trap' with you, if you run the nuthook's humour on me; that is the very note of it.
lines: Be avised, sir, and pass good humours: I will say
'marry trap' with you, if you run the nuthook's
humour on me; that is the very note of it.

speeches.speech: By this hat, then, he in the red face had it; for though I cannot remember what I did when you made me drunk, yet I am not altogether an ass.
lines: By this hat, then, he in the red face had it; for
though I cannot remember what I did when you made me
drunk, yet I am not altogether an ass.

speeches.speech: What say you, Scarlet and John?
lines: What say you, Scarlet and John?

speeches.speech: Why, sir, for my part I say the gentleman had drunk himself out of his five sentences.
lines: Why, sir, for my part I say the gentleman had drunk
himself out of his five sentences.

speeches.speech: It is his five senses: fie, what the ignorance is!
lines: It is his five senses: fie, what the ignorance is!

speeches.speech: And being fap, sir, was, as they say, cashiered; and so conclusions passed the careires.
lines: And being fap, sir, was, as they say, cashiered; and
so conclusions passed the careires.

speeches.speech: Ay, you spake in Latin then too; but 'tis no matter: I'll ne'er be drunk whilst I live again, but in honest, civil, godly company, for this trick: if I be drunk, I'll be drunk with those that have the fear of God, and not with drunken knaves.
lines: Ay, you spake in Latin then too; but 'tis no
matter: I'll ne'er be drunk whilst I live again,
but in honest, civil, godly company, for this trick:
if I be drunk, I'll be drunk with those that have
the fear of God, and not with drunken knaves.

speeches.speech: So Got udge me, that is a virtuous mind.
lines: So Got udge me, that is a virtuous mind.

speeches.speech: You hear all these matters denied, gentlemen; you hear it. [Enter ANNE PAGE, with wine; MISTRESS FORD] and MISTRESS PAGE, following]
lines: You hear all these matters denied, gentlemen; you hear it.
[Enter ANNE PAGE, with wine; MISTRESS FORD]
and MISTRESS PAGE, following]

speeches.speech: Nay, daughter, carry the wine in; we'll drink within.
lines: Nay, daughter, carry the wine in; we'll drink within.

speeches.speech: O heaven! this is Mistress Anne Page.
lines: O heaven! this is Mistress Anne Page.

speeches.speech: How now, Mistress Ford!
lines: How now, Mistress Ford!

speeches.speech: Mistress Ford, by my troth, you are very well met: by your leave, good mistress.
lines: Mistress Ford, by my troth, you are very well met:
by your leave, good mistress.

speeches.speech: Wife, bid these gentlemen welcome. Come, we have a hot venison pasty to dinner: come, gentlemen, I hope we shall drink down all unkindness.
lines: Wife, bid these gentlemen welcome. Come, we have a
hot venison pasty to dinner: come, gentlemen, I hope
we shall drink down all unkindness.

speeches.speech: I had rather than forty shillings I had my Book of Songs and Sonnets here. [Enter SIMPLE] How now, Simple! where have you been? I must wait on myself, must I? You have not the Book of Riddles about you, have you?
lines: I had rather than forty shillings I had my Book of
Songs and Sonnets here.
[Enter SIMPLE]
How now, Simple! where have you been? I must wait
on myself, must I? You have not the Book of Riddles
about you, have you?

speeches.speech: Book of Riddles! why, did you not lend it to Alice Shortcake upon All-hallowmas last, a fortnight afore Michaelmas?
lines: Book of Riddles! why, did you not lend it to Alice
Shortcake upon All-hallowmas last, a fortnight
afore Michaelmas?

speeches.speech: Come, coz; come, coz; we stay for you. A word with you, coz; marry, this, coz: there is, as 'twere, a tender, a kind of tender, made afar off by Sir Hugh here. Do you understand me?
lines: Come, coz; come, coz; we stay for you. A word with
you, coz; marry, this, coz: there is, as 'twere, a
tender, a kind of tender, made afar off by Sir Hugh
here. Do you understand me?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, you shall find me reasonable; if it be so, I shall do that that is reason.
lines: Ay, sir, you shall find me reasonable; if it be so,
I shall do that that is reason.

speeches.speech: Nay, but understand me.
lines: Nay, but understand me.

speeches.speech: So I do, sir.
lines: So I do, sir.

speeches.speech: Give ear to his motions, Master Slender: I will description the matter to you, if you be capacity of it.
lines: Give ear to his motions, Master Slender: I will
description the matter to you, if you be capacity of it.

speeches.speech: Nay, I will do as my cousin Shallow says: I pray you, pardon me; he's a justice of peace in his country, simple though I stand here.
lines: Nay, I will do as my cousin Shallow says: I pray
you, pardon me; he's a justice of peace in his
country, simple though I stand here.

speeches.speech: But that is not the question: the question is concerning your marriage.
lines: But that is not the question: the question is
concerning your marriage.

speeches.speech: Ay, there's the point, sir.
lines: Ay, there's the point, sir.

speeches.speech: Marry, is it; the very point of it; to Mistress Anne Page.
lines: Marry, is it; the very point of it; to Mistress Anne Page.

speeches.speech: Why, if it be so, I will marry her upon any reasonable demands.
lines: Why, if it be so, I will marry her upon any
reasonable demands.

speeches.speech: But can you affection the 'oman? Let us command to know that of your mouth or of your lips; for divers philosophers hold that the lips is parcel of the mouth. Therefore, precisely, can you carry your good will to the maid?
lines: But can you affection the 'oman? Let us command to
know that of your mouth or of your lips; for divers
philosophers hold that the lips is parcel of the
mouth. Therefore, precisely, can you carry your
good will to the maid?

speeches.speech: Cousin Abraham Slender, can you love her?
lines: Cousin Abraham Slender, can you love her?

speeches.speech: I hope, sir, I will do as it shall become one that would do reason.
lines: I hope, sir, I will do as it shall become one that
would do reason.

speeches.speech: Nay, Got's lords and his ladies! you must speak possitable, if you can carry her your desires towards her.
lines: Nay, Got's lords and his ladies! you must speak
possitable, if you can carry her your desires
towards her.

speeches.speech: That you must. Will you, upon good dowry, marry her?
lines: That you must. Will you, upon good dowry, marry her?

speeches.speech: I will do a greater thing than that, upon your request, cousin, in any reason.
lines: I will do a greater thing than that, upon your
request, cousin, in any reason.

speeches.speech: Nay, conceive me, conceive me, sweet coz: what I do is to pleasure you, coz. Can you love the maid?
lines: Nay, conceive me, conceive me, sweet coz: what I do
is to pleasure you, coz. Can you love the maid?

speeches.speech: I will marry her, sir, at your request: but if there be no great love in the beginning, yet heaven may decrease it upon better acquaintance, when we are married and have more occasion to know one another; I hope, upon familiarity will grow more contempt: but if you say, 'Marry her,' I will marry her; that I am freely dissolved, and dissolutely.
lines: I will marry her, sir, at your request: but if there
be no great love in the beginning, yet heaven may
decrease it upon better acquaintance, when we are
married and have more occasion to know one another;
I hope, upon familiarity will grow more contempt:
but if you say, 'Marry her,' I will marry her; that
I am freely dissolved, and dissolutely.

speeches.speech: It is a fery discretion answer; save the fall is in the ort 'dissolutely:' the ort is, according to our meaning, 'resolutely:' his meaning is good.
lines: It is a fery discretion answer; save the fall is in
the ort 'dissolutely:' the ort is, according to our
meaning, 'resolutely:' his meaning is good.

speeches.speech: Ay, I think my cousin meant well.
lines: Ay, I think my cousin meant well.

speeches.speech: Ay, or else I would I might be hanged, la!
lines: Ay, or else I would I might be hanged, la!

speeches.speech: Here comes fair Mistress Anne. [Re-enter ANNE PAGE] Would I were young for your sake, Mistress Anne!
lines: Here comes fair Mistress Anne.
[Re-enter ANNE PAGE]
Would I were young for your sake, Mistress Anne!

speeches.speech: The dinner is on the table; my father desires your worships' company.
lines: The dinner is on the table; my father desires your
worships' company.

speeches.speech: I will wait on him, fair Mistress Anne.
lines: I will wait on him, fair Mistress Anne.

speeches.speech: Od's plessed will! I will not be absence at the grace.
lines: Od's plessed will! I will not be absence at the grace.

speeches.speech: Will't please your worship to come in, sir?
lines: Will't please your worship to come in, sir?

speeches.speech: No, I thank you, forsooth, heartily; I am very well.
lines: No, I thank you, forsooth, heartily; I am very well.

speeches.speech: The dinner attends you, sir.
lines: The dinner attends you, sir.

speeches.speech: I am not a-hungry, I thank you, forsooth. Go, sirrah, for all you are my man, go wait upon my cousin Shallow. [Exit SIMPLE] A justice of peace sometimes may be beholding to his friend for a man. I keep but three men and a boy yet, till my mother be dead: but what though? Yet I live like a poor gentleman born.
lines: I am not a-hungry, I thank you, forsooth. Go,
sirrah, for all you are my man, go wait upon my
cousin Shallow.
[Exit SIMPLE]
A justice of peace sometimes may be beholding to his
friend for a man. I keep but three men and a boy
yet, till my mother be dead: but what though? Yet I
live like a poor gentleman born.

speeches.speech: I may not go in without your worship: they will not sit till you come.
lines: I may not go in without your worship: they will not
sit till you come.

speeches.speech: I' faith, I'll eat nothing; I thank you as much as though I did.
lines: I' faith, I'll eat nothing; I thank you as much as
though I did.

speeches.speech: I pray you, sir, walk in.
lines: I pray you, sir, walk in.

speeches.speech: I had rather walk here, I thank you. I bruised my shin th' other day with playing at sword and dagger with a master of fence; three veneys for a dish of stewed prunes; and, by my troth, I cannot abide the smell of hot meat since. Why do your dogs bark so? be there bears i' the town?
lines: I had rather walk here, I thank you. I bruised
my shin th' other day with playing at sword and
dagger with a master of fence; three veneys for a
dish of stewed prunes; and, by my troth, I cannot
abide the smell of hot meat since. Why do your
dogs bark so? be there bears i' the town?

speeches.speech: I think there are, sir; I heard them talked of.
lines: I think there are, sir; I heard them talked of.

speeches.speech: I love the sport well but I shall as soon quarrel at it as any man in England. You are afraid, if you see the bear loose, are you not?
lines: I love the sport well but I shall as soon quarrel at
it as any man in England. You are afraid, if you see
the bear loose, are you not?

speeches.speech: Ay, indeed, sir.
lines: Ay, indeed, sir.

speeches.speech: That's meat and drink to me, now. I have seen Sackerson loose twenty times, and have taken him by the chain; but, I warrant you, the women have so cried and shrieked at it, that it passed: but women, indeed, cannot abide 'em; they are very ill-favored rough things.
lines: That's meat and drink to me, now. I have seen
Sackerson loose twenty times, and have taken him by
the chain; but, I warrant you, the women have so
cried and shrieked at it, that it passed: but women,
indeed, cannot abide 'em; they are very ill-favored
rough things.

speeches.speech: Come, gentle Master Slender, come; we stay for you.
lines: Come, gentle Master Slender, come; we stay for you.

speeches.speech: I'll eat nothing, I thank you, sir.
lines: I'll eat nothing, I thank you, sir.

speeches.speech: By cock and pie, you shall not choose, sir! come, come.
lines: By cock and pie, you shall not choose, sir! come, come.

speeches.speech: Nay, pray you, lead the way.
lines: Nay, pray you, lead the way.

speeches.speech: Come on, sir.
lines: Come on, sir.

speeches.speech: Mistress Anne, yourself shall go first.
lines: Mistress Anne, yourself shall go first.

speeches.speech: Not I, sir; pray you, keep on.
lines: Not I, sir; pray you, keep on.

speeches.speech: I'll rather be unmannerly than troublesome. You do yourself wrong, indeed, la!
lines: I'll rather be unmannerly than troublesome.
You do yourself wrong, indeed, la!

speeches.speech: Go your ways, and ask of Doctor Caius' house which is the way: and there dwells one Mistress Quickly, which is in the manner of his nurse, or his dry nurse, or his cook, or his laundry, his washer, and his wringer.
lines: Go your ways, and ask of Doctor Caius' house which
is the way: and there dwells one Mistress Quickly,
which is in the manner of his nurse, or his dry
nurse, or his cook, or his laundry, his washer, and
his wringer.

speeches.speech: Well, sir.
lines: Well, sir.

speeches.speech: Nay, it is petter yet. Give her this letter; for it is a 'oman that altogether's acquaintance with Mistress Anne Page: and the letter is, to desire and require her to solicit your master's desires to Mistress Anne Page. I pray you, be gone: I will make an end of my dinner; there's pippins and cheese to come.
lines: Nay, it is petter yet. Give her this letter; for it
is a 'oman that altogether's acquaintance with
Mistress Anne Page: and the letter is, to desire
and require her to solicit your master's desires to
Mistress Anne Page. I pray you, be gone: I will
make an end of my dinner; there's pippins and cheese to come.

speeches.speech: Mine host of the Garter!
lines: Mine host of the Garter!

speeches.speech: What says my bully-rook? speak scholarly and wisely.
lines: What says my bully-rook? speak scholarly and wisely.

speeches.speech: Truly, mine host, I must turn away some of my followers.
lines: Truly, mine host, I must turn away some of my
followers.

speeches.speech: Discard, bully Hercules; cashier: let them wag; trot, trot.
lines: Discard, bully Hercules; cashier: let them wag; trot, trot.

speeches.speech: I sit at ten pounds a week.
lines: I sit at ten pounds a week.

speeches.speech: Thou'rt an emperor, Caesar, Keisar, and Pheezar. I will entertain Bardolph; he shall draw, he shall tap: said I well, bully Hector?
lines: Thou'rt an emperor, Caesar, Keisar, and Pheezar. I
will entertain Bardolph; he shall draw, he shall
tap: said I well, bully Hector?

speeches.speech: Do so, good mine host.
lines: Do so, good mine host.

speeches.speech: I have spoke; let him follow. [To BARDOLPH] Let me see thee froth and lime: I am at a word; follow.
lines: I have spoke; let him follow.
[To BARDOLPH]
Let me see thee froth and lime: I am at a word; follow.

speeches.speech: Bardolph, follow him. A tapster is a good trade: an old cloak makes a new jerkin; a withered serving-man a fresh tapster. Go; adieu.
lines: Bardolph, follow him. A tapster is a good trade:
an old cloak makes a new jerkin; a withered
serving-man a fresh tapster. Go; adieu.

speeches.speech: It is a life that I have desired: I will thrive.
lines: It is a life that I have desired: I will thrive.

speeches.speech: O base Hungarian wight! wilt thou the spigot wield?
lines: O base Hungarian wight! wilt thou the spigot wield?

speeches.speech: He was gotten in drink: is not the humour conceited?
lines: He was gotten in drink: is not the humour conceited?

speeches.speech: I am glad I am so acquit of this tinderbox: his thefts were too open; his filching was like an unskilful singer; he kept not time.
lines: I am glad I am so acquit of this tinderbox: his
thefts were too open; his filching was like an
unskilful singer; he kept not time.

speeches.speech: The good humour is to steal at a minute's rest.
lines: The good humour is to steal at a minute's rest.

speeches.speech: 'Convey,' the wise it call. 'Steal!' foh! a fico for the phrase!
lines: 'Convey,' the wise it call. 'Steal!' foh! a fico
for the phrase!

speeches.speech: Well, sirs, I am almost out at heels.
lines: Well, sirs, I am almost out at heels.

speeches.speech: Why, then, let kibes ensue.
lines: Why, then, let kibes ensue.

speeches.speech: There is no remedy; I must cony-catch; I must shift.
lines: There is no remedy; I must cony-catch; I must shift.

speeches.speech: Young ravens must have food.
lines: Young ravens must have food.

speeches.speech: Which of you know Ford of this town?
lines: Which of you know Ford of this town?

speeches.speech: I ken the wight: he is of substance good.
lines: I ken the wight: he is of substance good.

speeches.speech: My honest lads, I will tell you what I am about.
lines: My honest lads, I will tell you what I am about.

speeches.speech: Two yards, and more.
lines: Two yards, and more.

speeches.speech: No quips now, Pistol! Indeed, I am in the waist two yards about; but I am now about no waste; I am about thrift. Briefly, I do mean to make love to Ford's wife: I spy entertainment in her; she discourses, she carves, she gives the leer of invitation: I can construe the action of her familiar style; and the hardest voice of her behavior, to be Englished rightly, is, 'I am Sir John Falstaff's.'
lines: No quips now, Pistol! Indeed, I am in the waist two
yards about; but I am now about no waste; I am about
thrift. Briefly, I do mean to make love to Ford's
wife: I spy entertainment in her; she discourses,
she carves, she gives the leer of invitation: I
can construe the action of her familiar style; and
the hardest voice of her behavior, to be Englished
rightly, is, 'I am Sir John Falstaff's.'

speeches.speech: He hath studied her will, and translated her will, out of honesty into English.
lines: He hath studied her will, and translated her will,
out of honesty into English.

speeches.speech: The anchor is deep: will that humour pass?
lines: The anchor is deep: will that humour pass?

speeches.speech: Now, the report goes she has all the rule of her husband's purse: he hath a legion of angels.
lines: Now, the report goes she has all the rule of her
husband's purse: he hath a legion of angels.

speeches.speech: As many devils entertain; and 'To her, boy,' say I.
lines: As many devils entertain; and 'To her, boy,' say I.

speeches.speech: The humour rises; it is good: humour me the angels.
lines: The humour rises; it is good: humour me the angels.

speeches.speech: I have writ me here a letter to her: and here another to Page's wife, who even now gave me good eyes too, examined my parts with most judicious oeillades; sometimes the beam of her view gilded my foot, sometimes my portly belly.
lines: I have writ me here a letter to her: and here
another to Page's wife, who even now gave me good
eyes too, examined my parts with most judicious
oeillades; sometimes the beam of her view gilded my
foot, sometimes my portly belly.

speeches.speech: Then did the sun on dunghill shine.
lines: Then did the sun on dunghill shine.

speeches.speech: I thank thee for that humour.
lines: I thank thee for that humour.

speeches.speech: O, she did so course o'er my exteriors with such a greedy intention, that the appetite of her eye did seem to scorch me up like a burning-glass! Here's another letter to her: she bears the purse too; she is a region in Guiana, all gold and bounty. I will be cheater to them both, and they shall be exchequers to me; they shall be my East and West Indies, and I will trade to them both. Go bear thou this letter to Mistress Page; and thou this to Mistress Ford: we will thrive, lads, we will thrive.
lines: O, she did so course o'er my exteriors with such a
greedy intention, that the appetite of her eye did
seem to scorch me up like a burning-glass! Here's
another letter to her: she bears the purse too; she
is a region in Guiana, all gold and bounty. I will
be cheater to them both, and they shall be
exchequers to me; they shall be my East and West
Indies, and I will trade to them both. Go bear thou
this letter to Mistress Page; and thou this to
Mistress Ford: we will thrive, lads, we will thrive.

speeches.speech: Shall I Sir Pandarus of Troy become, And by my side wear steel? then, Lucifer take all!
lines: Shall I Sir Pandarus of Troy become,
And by my side wear steel? then, Lucifer take all!

speeches.speech: I will run no base humour: here, take the humour-letter: I will keep the havior of reputation.
lines: I will run no base humour: here, take the
humour-letter: I will keep the havior of reputation.

speeches.speech: [To ROBIN] Hold, sirrah, bear you these letters tightly; Sail like my pinnace to these golden shores. Rogues, hence, avaunt! vanish like hailstones, go; Trudge, plod away o' the hoof; seek shelter, pack! Falstaff will learn the humour of the age, French thrift, you rogues; myself and skirted page.
lines: [To ROBIN] Hold, sirrah, bear you these letters tightly;
Sail like my pinnace to these golden shores.
Rogues, hence, avaunt! vanish like hailstones, go;
Trudge, plod away o' the hoof; seek shelter, pack!
Falstaff will learn the humour of the age,
French thrift, you rogues; myself and skirted page.

speeches.speech: Let vultures gripe thy guts! for gourd and fullam holds, And high and low beguiles the rich and poor: Tester I'll have in pouch when thou shalt lack, Base Phrygian Turk!
lines: Let vultures gripe thy guts! for gourd and fullam holds,
And high and low beguiles the rich and poor:
Tester I'll have in pouch when thou shalt lack,
Base Phrygian Turk!

speeches.speech: I have operations which be humours of revenge.
lines: I have operations which be humours of revenge.

speeches.speech: Wilt thou revenge?
lines: Wilt thou revenge?

speeches.speech: By welkin and her star!
lines: By welkin and her star!

speeches.speech: With wit or steel?
lines: With wit or steel?

speeches.speech: With both the humours, I: I will discuss the humour of this love to Page.
lines: With both the humours, I:
I will discuss the humour of this love to Page.

speeches.speech: And I to Ford shall eke unfold How Falstaff, varlet vile, His dove will prove, his gold will hold, And his soft couch defile.
lines: And I to Ford shall eke unfold
How Falstaff, varlet vile,
His dove will prove, his gold will hold,
And his soft couch defile.

speeches.speech: My humour shall not cool: I will incense Page to deal with poison; I will possess him with yellowness, for the revolt of mine is dangerous: that is my true humour.
lines: My humour shall not cool: I will incense Page to
deal with poison; I will possess him with
yellowness, for the revolt of mine is dangerous:
that is my true humour.

speeches.speech: Thou art the Mars of malecontents: I second thee; troop on.
lines: Thou art the Mars of malecontents: I second thee; troop on.

speeches.speech: What, John Rugby! I pray thee, go to the casement, and see if you can see my master, Master Doctor Caius, coming. If he do, i' faith, and find any body in the house, here will be an old abusing of God's patience and the king's English.
lines: What, John Rugby! I pray thee, go to the casement,
and see if you can see my master, Master Doctor
Caius, coming. If he do, i' faith, and find any
body in the house, here will be an old abusing of
God's patience and the king's English.

speeches.speech: I'll go watch.
lines: I'll go watch.

speeches.speech: Go; and we'll have a posset for't soon at night, in faith, at the latter end of a sea-coal fire. [Exit RUGBY] An honest, willing, kind fellow, as ever servant shall come in house withal, and, I warrant you, no tell-tale nor no breed-bate: his worst fault is, that he is given to prayer; he is something peevish that way: but nobody but has his fault; but let that pass. Peter Simple, you say your name is?
lines: Go; and we'll have a posset for't soon at night, in
faith, at the latter end of a sea-coal fire.
[Exit RUGBY]
An honest, willing, kind fellow, as ever servant
shall come in house withal, and, I warrant you, no
tell-tale nor no breed-bate: his worst fault is,
that he is given to prayer; he is something peevish
that way: but nobody but has his fault; but let
that pass. Peter Simple, you say your name is?

speeches.speech: Ay, for fault of a better.
lines: Ay, for fault of a better.

speeches.speech: And Master Slender's your master?
lines: And Master Slender's your master?

speeches.speech: Ay, forsooth.
lines: Ay, forsooth.

speeches.speech: Does he not wear a great round beard, like a glover's paring-knife?
lines: Does he not wear a great round beard, like a
glover's paring-knife?

speeches.speech: No, forsooth: he hath but a little wee face, with a little yellow beard, a Cain-coloured beard.
lines: No, forsooth: he hath but a little wee face, with a
little yellow beard, a Cain-coloured beard.

speeches.speech: A softly-sprighted man, is he not?
lines: A softly-sprighted man, is he not?

speeches.speech: Ay, forsooth: but he is as tall a man of his hands as any is between this and his head; he hath fought with a warrener.
lines: Ay, forsooth: but he is as tall a man of his hands
as any is between this and his head; he hath fought
with a warrener.

speeches.speech: How say you? O, I should remember him: does he not hold up his head, as it were, and strut in his gait?
lines: How say you? O, I should remember him: does he not
hold up his head, as it were, and strut in his gait?

speeches.speech: Yes, indeed, does he.
lines: Yes, indeed, does he.

speeches.speech: Well, heaven send Anne Page no worse fortune! Tell Master Parson Evans I will do what I can for your master: Anne is a good girl, and I wish--
lines: Well, heaven send Anne Page no worse fortune! Tell
Master Parson Evans I will do what I can for your
master: Anne is a good girl, and I wish--

speeches.speech: Out, alas! here comes my master.
lines: Out, alas! here comes my master.

speeches.speech: We shall all be shent. Run in here, good young man; go into this closet: he will not stay long. [Shuts SIMPLE in the closet] What, John Rugby! John! what, John, I say! Go, John, go inquire for my master; I doubt he be not well, that he comes not home. [Singing] And down, down, adown-a, &c.
lines: We shall all be shent. Run in here, good young man;
go into this closet: he will not stay long.
[Shuts SIMPLE in the closet]
What, John Rugby! John! what, John, I say!
Go, John, go inquire for my master; I doubt
he be not well, that he comes not home.
[Singing]
And down, down, adown-a, &c.

speeches.speech: Vat is you sing? I do not like des toys. Pray you, go and vetch me in my closet un boitier vert, a box, a green-a box: do intend vat I speak? a green-a box.
lines: Vat is you sing? I do not like des toys. Pray you,
go and vetch me in my closet un boitier vert, a box,
a green-a box: do intend vat I speak? a green-a box.

speeches.speech: Ay, forsooth; I'll fetch it you. [Aside] I am glad he went not in himself: if he had found the young man, he would have been horn-mad.
lines: Ay, forsooth; I'll fetch it you.
[Aside]
I am glad he went not in himself: if he had found
the young man, he would have been horn-mad.

speeches.speech: Fe, fe, fe, fe! ma foi, il fait fort chaud. Je m'en vais a la cour--la grande affaire.
lines: Fe, fe, fe, fe! ma foi, il fait fort chaud. Je
m'en vais a la cour--la grande affaire.

speeches.speech: Is it this, sir?
lines: Is it this, sir?

speeches.speech: Oui; mette le au mon pocket: depeche, quickly. Vere is dat knave Rugby?
lines: Oui; mette le au mon pocket: depeche, quickly. Vere
is dat knave Rugby?

speeches.speech: What, John Rugby! John!
lines: What, John Rugby! John!

speeches.speech: Here, sir!
lines: Here, sir!

speeches.speech: You are John Rugby, and you are Jack Rugby. Come, take-a your rapier, and come after my heel to the court.
lines: You are John Rugby, and you are Jack Rugby. Come,
take-a your rapier, and come after my heel to the court.

speeches.speech: 'Tis ready, sir, here in the porch.
lines: 'Tis ready, sir, here in the porch.

speeches.speech: By my trot, I tarry too long. Od's me! Qu'ai-j'oublie! dere is some simples in my closet, dat I vill not for the varld I shall leave behind.
lines: By my trot, I tarry too long. Od's me!
Qu'ai-j'oublie! dere is some simples in my closet,
dat I vill not for the varld I shall leave behind.

speeches.speech: Ay me, he'll find the young man here, and be mad!
lines: Ay me, he'll find the young man here, and be mad!

speeches.speech: O diable, diable! vat is in my closet? Villain! larron! [Pulling SIMPLE out] Rugby, my rapier!
lines: O diable, diable! vat is in my closet? Villain! larron!
[Pulling SIMPLE out]
Rugby, my rapier!

speeches.speech: Good master, be content.
lines: Good master, be content.

speeches.speech: Wherefore shall I be content-a?
lines: Wherefore shall I be content-a?

speeches.speech: The young man is an honest man.
lines: The young man is an honest man.

speeches.speech: What shall de honest man do in my closet? dere is no honest man dat shall come in my closet.
lines: What shall de honest man do in my closet? dere is
no honest man dat shall come in my closet.

speeches.speech: I beseech you, be not so phlegmatic. Hear the truth of it: he came of an errand to me from Parson Hugh.
lines: I beseech you, be not so phlegmatic. Hear the truth
of it: he came of an errand to me from Parson Hugh.

speeches.speech: Vell.
lines: Vell.

speeches.speech: Ay, forsooth; to desire her to--
lines: Ay, forsooth; to desire her to--

speeches.speech: Peace, I pray you.
lines: Peace, I pray you.

speeches.speech: Peace-a your tongue. Speak-a your tale.
lines: Peace-a your tongue. Speak-a your tale.

speeches.speech: To desire this honest gentlewoman, your maid, to speak a good word to Mistress Anne Page for my master in the way of marriage.
lines: To desire this honest gentlewoman, your maid, to
speak a good word to Mistress Anne Page for my
master in the way of marriage.

speeches.speech: This is all, indeed, la! but I'll ne'er put my finger in the fire, and need not.
lines: This is all, indeed, la! but I'll ne'er put my
finger in the fire, and need not.

speeches.speech: Sir Hugh send-a you? Rugby, baille me some paper. Tarry you a little-a while.
lines: Sir Hugh send-a you? Rugby, baille me some paper.
Tarry you a little-a while.

speeches.speech: [Aside to SIMPLE] I am glad he is so quiet: if he had been thoroughly moved, you should have heard him so loud and so melancholy. But notwithstanding, man, I'll do you your master what good I can: and the very yea and the no is, the French doctor, my master,--I may call him my master, look you, for I keep his house; and I wash, wring, brew, bake, scour, dress meat and drink, make the beds and do all myself,--
lines: [Aside to SIMPLE] I am glad he is so quiet: if he
had been thoroughly moved, you should have heard him
so loud and so melancholy. But notwithstanding,
man, I'll do you your master what good I can: and
the very yea and the no is, the French doctor, my
master,--I may call him my master, look you, for I
keep his house; and I wash, wring, brew, bake,
scour, dress meat and drink, make the beds and do
all myself,--

speeches.speech: [Aside to MISTRESS QUICKLY] 'Tis a great charge to come under one body's hand.
lines: [Aside to MISTRESS QUICKLY] 'Tis a great charge to
come under one body's hand.

speeches.speech: [Aside to SIMPLE] Are you avised o' that? you shall find it a great charge: and to be up early and down late; but notwithstanding,--to tell you in your ear; I would have no words of it,--my master himself is in love with Mistress Anne Page: but notwithstanding that, I know Anne's mind,--that's neither here nor there.
lines: [Aside to SIMPLE] Are you avised o' that? you
shall find it a great charge: and to be up early
and down late; but notwithstanding,--to tell you in
your ear; I would have no words of it,--my master
himself is in love with Mistress Anne Page: but
notwithstanding that, I know Anne's mind,--that's
neither here nor there.

speeches.speech: You jack'nape, give-a this letter to Sir Hugh; by gar, it is a shallenge: I will cut his troat in dee park; and I will teach a scurvy jack-a-nape priest to meddle or make. You may be gone; it is not good you tarry here. By gar, I will cut all his two stones; by gar, he shall not have a stone to throw at his dog:
lines: You jack'nape, give-a this letter to Sir Hugh; by
gar, it is a shallenge: I will cut his troat in dee
park; and I will teach a scurvy jack-a-nape priest
to meddle or make. You may be gone; it is not good
you tarry here. By gar, I will cut all his two
stones; by gar, he shall not have a stone to throw
at his dog:

speeches.speech: Alas, he speaks but for his friend.
lines: Alas, he speaks but for his friend.

speeches.speech: It is no matter-a ver dat: do not you tell-a me dat I shall have Anne Page for myself? By gar, I vill kill de Jack priest; and I have appointed mine host of de Jarteer to measure our weapon. By gar, I will myself have Anne Page.
lines: It is no matter-a ver dat: do not you tell-a me
dat I shall have Anne Page for myself? By gar, I
vill kill de Jack priest; and I have appointed mine
host of de Jarteer to measure our weapon. By gar, I
will myself have Anne Page.

speeches.speech: Sir, the maid loves you, and all shall be well. We must give folks leave to prate: what, the good-jer!
lines: Sir, the maid loves you, and all shall be well. We
must give folks leave to prate: what, the good-jer!

speeches.speech: Rugby, come to the court with me. By gar, if I have not Anne Page, I shall turn your head out of my door. Follow my heels, Rugby.
lines: Rugby, come to the court with me. By gar, if I have
not Anne Page, I shall turn your head out of my
door. Follow my heels, Rugby.

speeches.speech: You shall have An fool's-head of your own. No, I know Anne's mind for that: never a woman in Windsor knows more of Anne's mind than I do; nor can do more than I do with her, I thank heaven.
lines: You shall have An fool's-head of your own. No, I
know Anne's mind for that: never a woman in Windsor
knows more of Anne's mind than I do; nor can do more
than I do with her, I thank heaven.

speeches.speech: [Within] Who's within there? ho!
lines: [Within] Who's within there? ho!

speeches.speech: Who's there, I trow! Come near the house, I pray you.
lines: Who's there, I trow! Come near the house, I pray you.

speeches.speech: How now, good woman? how dost thou?
lines: How now, good woman? how dost thou?

speeches.speech: The better that it pleases your good worship to ask.
lines: The better that it pleases your good worship to ask.

speeches.speech: What news? how does pretty Mistress Anne?
lines: What news? how does pretty Mistress Anne?

speeches.speech: In truth, sir, and she is pretty, and honest, and gentle; and one that is your friend, I can tell you that by the way; I praise heaven for it.
lines: In truth, sir, and she is pretty, and honest, and
gentle; and one that is your friend, I can tell you
that by the way; I praise heaven for it.

speeches.speech: Shall I do any good, thinkest thou? shall I not lose my suit?
lines: Shall I do any good, thinkest thou? shall I not lose my suit?

speeches.speech: Troth, sir, all is in his hands above: but notwithstanding, Master Fenton, I'll be sworn on a book, she loves you. Have not your worship a wart above your eye?
lines: Troth, sir, all is in his hands above: but
notwithstanding, Master Fenton, I'll be sworn on a
book, she loves you. Have not your worship a wart
above your eye?

speeches.speech: Yes, marry, have I; what of that?
lines: Yes, marry, have I; what of that?

speeches.speech: Well, thereby hangs a tale: good faith, it is such another Nan; but, I detest, an honest maid as ever broke bread: we had an hour's talk of that wart. I shall never laugh but in that maid's company! But indeed she is given too much to allicholy and musing: but for you--well, go to.
lines: Well, thereby hangs a tale: good faith, it is such
another Nan; but, I detest, an honest maid as ever
broke bread: we had an hour's talk of that wart. I
shall never laugh but in that maid's company! But
indeed she is given too much to allicholy and
musing: but for you--well, go to.

speeches.speech: Well, I shall see her to-day. Hold, there's money for thee; let me have thy voice in my behalf: if thou seest her before me, commend me.
lines: Well, I shall see her to-day. Hold, there's money
for thee; let me have thy voice in my behalf: if
thou seest her before me, commend me.

speeches.speech: Will I? i'faith, that we will; and I will tell your worship more of the wart the next time we have confidence; and of other wooers.
lines: Will I? i'faith, that we will; and I will tell your
worship more of the wart the next time we have
confidence; and of other wooers.

speeches.speech: Well, farewell; I am in great haste now.
lines: Well, farewell; I am in great haste now.

speeches.speech: Farewell to your worship. [Exit FENTON] Truly, an honest gentleman: but Anne loves him not; for I know Anne's mind as well as another does. Out upon't! what have I forgot?
lines: Farewell to your worship.
[Exit FENTON]
Truly, an honest gentleman: but Anne loves him not;
for I know Anne's mind as well as another does. Out
upon't! what have I forgot?

speeches.speech: What, have I scaped love-letters in the holiday- time of my beauty, and am I now a subject for them? Let me see. [Reads] 'Ask me no reason why I love you; for though Love use Reason for his physician, he admits him not for his counsellor. You are not young, no more am I; go to then, there's sympathy: you are merry, so am I; ha, ha! then there's more sympathy: you love sack, and so do I; would you desire better sympathy? Let it suffice thee, Mistress Page,--at the least, if the love of soldier can suffice,-- that I love thee. I will not say, pity me; 'tis not a soldier-like phrase: but I say, love me. By me, Thine own true knight, By day or night, Or any kind of light, With all his might For thee to fight, JOHN FALSTAFF' What a Herod of Jewry is this! O wicked world! One that is well-nigh worn to pieces with age to show himself a young gallant! What an unweighed behavior hath this Flemish drunkard picked--with the devil's name!--out of my conversation, that he dares in this manner assay me? Why, he hath not been thrice in my company! What should I say to him? I was then frugal of my mirth: Heaven forgive me! Why, I'll exhibit a bill in the parliament for the putting down of men. How shall I be revenged on him? for revenged I will be, as sure as his guts are made of puddings.
lines: What, have I scaped love-letters in the holiday-
time of my beauty, and am I now a subject for them?
Let me see.
[Reads]
'Ask me no reason why I love you; for though
Love use Reason for his physician, he admits him
not for his counsellor. You are not young, no more
am I; go to then, there's sympathy: you are merry,
so am I; ha, ha! then there's more sympathy: you
love sack, and so do I; would you desire better
sympathy? Let it suffice thee, Mistress Page,--at
the least, if the love of soldier can suffice,--
that I love thee. I will not say, pity me; 'tis
not a soldier-like phrase: but I say, love me. By me,
Thine own true knight,
By day or night,
Or any kind of light,
With all his might
For thee to fight, JOHN FALSTAFF'
What a Herod of Jewry is this! O wicked
world! One that is well-nigh worn to pieces with
age to show himself a young gallant! What an
unweighed behavior hath this Flemish drunkard
picked--with the devil's name!--out of my
conversation, that he dares in this manner assay me?
Why, he hath not been thrice in my company! What
should I say to him? I was then frugal of my
mirth: Heaven forgive me! Why, I'll exhibit a bill
in the parliament for the putting down of men. How
shall I be revenged on him? for revenged I will be,
as sure as his guts are made of puddings.

speeches.speech: Mistress Page! trust me, I was going to your house.
lines: Mistress Page! trust me, I was going to your house.

speeches.speech: And, trust me, I was coming to you. You look very ill.
lines: And, trust me, I was coming to you. You look very
ill.

speeches.speech: Nay, I'll ne'er believe that; I have to show to the contrary.
lines: Nay, I'll ne'er believe that; I have to show to the contrary.

speeches.speech: Faith, but you do, in my mind.
lines: Faith, but you do, in my mind.

speeches.speech: Well, I do then; yet I say I could show you to the contrary. O Mistress Page, give me some counsel!
lines: Well, I do then; yet I say I could show you to the
contrary. O Mistress Page, give me some counsel!

speeches.speech: What's the matter, woman?
lines: What's the matter, woman?

speeches.speech: O woman, if it were not for one trifling respect, I could come to such honour!
lines: O woman, if it were not for one trifling respect, I
could come to such honour!

speeches.speech: Hang the trifle, woman! take the honour. What is it? dispense with trifles; what is it?
lines: Hang the trifle, woman! take the honour. What is
it? dispense with trifles; what is it?

speeches.speech: If I would but go to hell for an eternal moment or so, I could be knighted.
lines: If I would but go to hell for an eternal moment or so,
I could be knighted.

speeches.speech: What? thou liest! Sir Alice Ford! These knights will hack; and so thou shouldst not alter the article of thy gentry.
lines: What? thou liest! Sir Alice Ford! These knights
will hack; and so thou shouldst not alter the
article of thy gentry.

speeches.speech: We burn daylight: here, read, read; perceive how I might be knighted. I shall think the worse of fat men, as long as I have an eye to make difference of men's liking: and yet he would not swear; praised women's modesty; and gave such orderly and well-behaved reproof to all uncomeliness, that I would have sworn his disposition would have gone to the truth of his words; but they do no more adhere and keep place together than the Hundredth Psalm to the tune of 'Green Sleeves.' What tempest, I trow, threw this whale, with so many tuns of oil in his belly, ashore at Windsor? How shall I be revenged on him? I think the best way were to entertain him with hope, till the wicked fire of lust have melted him in his own grease. Did you ever hear the like?
lines: We burn daylight: here, read, read; perceive how I
might be knighted. I shall think the worse of fat
men, as long as I have an eye to make difference of
men's liking: and yet he would not swear; praised
women's modesty; and gave such orderly and
well-behaved reproof to all uncomeliness, that I
would have sworn his disposition would have gone to
the truth of his words; but they do no more adhere
and keep place together than the Hundredth Psalm to
the tune of 'Green Sleeves.' What tempest, I trow,
threw this whale, with so many tuns of oil in his
belly, ashore at Windsor? How shall I be revenged
on him? I think the best way were to entertain him
with hope, till the wicked fire of lust have melted
him in his own grease. Did you ever hear the like?

speeches.speech: Letter for letter, but that the name of Page and Ford differs! To thy great comfort in this mystery of ill opinions, here's the twin-brother of thy letter: but let thine inherit first; for, I protest, mine never shall. I warrant he hath a thousand of these letters, writ with blank space for different names--sure, more,--and these are of the second edition: he will print them, out of doubt; for he cares not what he puts into the press, when he would put us two. I had rather be a giantess, and lie under Mount Pelion. Well, I will find you twenty lascivious turtles ere one chaste man.
lines: Letter for letter, but that the name of Page and
Ford differs! To thy great comfort in this mystery
of ill opinions, here's the twin-brother of thy
letter: but let thine inherit first; for, I
protest, mine never shall. I warrant he hath a
thousand of these letters, writ with blank space for
different names--sure, more,--and these are of the
second edition: he will print them, out of doubt;
for he cares not what he puts into the press, when
he would put us two. I had rather be a giantess,
and lie under Mount Pelion. Well, I will find you
twenty lascivious turtles ere one chaste man.

speeches.speech: Why, this is the very same; the very hand, the very words. What doth he think of us?
lines: Why, this is the very same; the very hand, the very
words. What doth he think of us?

speeches.speech: Nay, I know not: it makes me almost ready to wrangle with mine own honesty. I'll entertain myself like one that I am not acquainted withal; for, sure, unless he know some strain in me, that I know not myself, he would never have boarded me in this fury.
lines: Nay, I know not: it makes me almost ready to
wrangle with mine own honesty. I'll entertain
myself like one that I am not acquainted withal;
for, sure, unless he know some strain in me, that I
know not myself, he would never have boarded me in this fury.

speeches.speech: 'Boarding,' call you it? I'll be sure to keep him above deck.
lines: 'Boarding,' call you it? I'll be sure to keep him
above deck.

speeches.speech: So will I. if he come under my hatches, I'll never to sea again. Let's be revenged on him: let's appoint him a meeting; give him a show of comfort in his suit and lead him on with a fine-baited delay, till he hath pawned his horses to mine host of the Garter.
lines: So will I. if he come under my hatches, I'll never
to sea again. Let's be revenged on him: let's
appoint him a meeting; give him a show of comfort in
his suit and lead him on with a fine-baited delay,
till he hath pawned his horses to mine host of the Garter.

speeches.speech: Nay, I will consent to act any villany against him, that may not sully the chariness of our honesty. O, that my husband saw this letter! it would give eternal food to his jealousy.
lines: Nay, I will consent to act any villany against him,
that may not sully the chariness of our honesty. O,
that my husband saw this letter! it would give
eternal food to his jealousy.

speeches.speech: Why, look where he comes; and my good man too: he's as far from jealousy as I am from giving him cause; and that I hope is an unmeasurable distance.
lines: Why, look where he comes; and my good man too: he's
as far from jealousy as I am from giving him cause;
and that I hope is an unmeasurable distance.

speeches.speech: You are the happier woman.
lines: You are the happier woman.

speeches.speech: Let's consult together against this greasy knight. Come hither.
lines: Let's consult together against this greasy knight.
Come hither.

speeches.speech: Well, I hope it be not so.
lines: Well, I hope it be not so.

speeches.speech: Hope is a curtal dog in some affairs: Sir John affects thy wife.
lines: Hope is a curtal dog in some affairs:
Sir John affects thy wife.

speeches.speech: Why, sir, my wife is not young.
lines: Why, sir, my wife is not young.

speeches.speech: He wooes both high and low, both rich and poor, Both young and old, one with another, Ford; He loves the gallimaufry: Ford, perpend.
lines: He wooes both high and low, both rich and poor,
Both young and old, one with another, Ford;
He loves the gallimaufry: Ford, perpend.

speeches.speech: Love my wife!
lines: Love my wife!

speeches.speech: With liver burning hot. Prevent, or go thou, Like Sir Actaeon he, with Ringwood at thy heels: O, odious is the name!
lines: With liver burning hot. Prevent, or go thou,
Like Sir Actaeon he, with Ringwood at thy heels:
O, odious is the name!

speeches.speech: What name, sir?
lines: What name, sir?

speeches.speech: The horn, I say. Farewell. Take heed, have open eye, for thieves do foot by night: Take heed, ere summer comes or cuckoo-birds do sing. Away, Sir Corporal Nym! Believe it, Page; he speaks sense.
lines: The horn, I say. Farewell.
Take heed, have open eye, for thieves do foot by night:
Take heed, ere summer comes or cuckoo-birds do sing.
Away, Sir Corporal Nym!
Believe it, Page; he speaks sense.

speeches.speech: [Aside] I will be patient; I will find out this.
lines: [Aside] I will be patient; I will find out this.

speeches.speech: [To PAGE] And this is true; I like not the humour of lying. He hath wronged me in some humours: I should have borne the humoured letter to her; but I have a sword and it shall bite upon my necessity. He loves your wife; there's the short and the long. My name is Corporal Nym; I speak and I avouch; 'tis true: my name is Nym and Falstaff loves your wife. Adieu. I love not the humour of bread and cheese, and there's the humour of it. Adieu.
lines: [To PAGE] And this is true; I like not the humour
of lying. He hath wronged me in some humours: I
should have borne the humoured letter to her; but I
have a sword and it shall bite upon my necessity.
He loves your wife; there's the short and the long.
My name is Corporal Nym; I speak and I avouch; 'tis
true: my name is Nym and Falstaff loves your wife.
Adieu. I love not the humour of bread and cheese,
and there's the humour of it. Adieu.

speeches.speech: 'The humour of it,' quoth a'! here's a fellow frights English out of his wits.
lines: 'The humour of it,' quoth a'! here's a fellow
frights English out of his wits.

speeches.speech: I will seek out Falstaff.
lines: I will seek out Falstaff.

speeches.speech: I never heard such a drawling, affecting rogue.
lines: I never heard such a drawling, affecting rogue.

speeches.speech: If I do find it: well.
lines: If I do find it: well.

speeches.speech: I will not believe such a Cataian, though the priest o' the town commended him for a true man.
lines: I will not believe such a Cataian, though the priest
o' the town commended him for a true man.

speeches.speech: 'Twas a good sensible fellow: well.
lines: 'Twas a good sensible fellow: well.

speeches.speech: How now, Meg!
lines: How now, Meg!

speeches.speech: Whither go you, George? Hark you.
lines: Whither go you, George? Hark you.

speeches.speech: How now, sweet Frank! why art thou melancholy?
lines: How now, sweet Frank! why art thou melancholy?

speeches.speech: I melancholy! I am not melancholy. Get you home, go.
lines: I melancholy! I am not melancholy. Get you home, go.

speeches.speech: Faith, thou hast some crotchets in thy head. Now, will you go, Mistress Page?
lines: Faith, thou hast some crotchets in thy head. Now,
will you go, Mistress Page?

speeches.speech: Have with you. You'll come to dinner, George. [Aside to MISTRESS FORD] Look who comes yonder: she shall be our messenger to this paltry knight.
lines: Have with you. You'll come to dinner, George.
[Aside to MISTRESS FORD]
Look who comes yonder: she shall be our messenger
to this paltry knight.

speeches.speech: [Aside to MISTRESS PAGE] Trust me, I thought on her: she'll fit it.
lines: [Aside to MISTRESS PAGE] Trust me, I thought on her:
she'll fit it.

speeches.speech: You are come to see my daughter Anne?
lines: You are come to see my daughter Anne?

speeches.speech: Ay, forsooth; and, I pray, how does good Mistress Anne?
lines: Ay, forsooth; and, I pray, how does good Mistress Anne?

speeches.speech: Go in with us and see: we have an hour's talk with you.
lines: Go in with us and see: we have an hour's talk with
you.

speeches.speech: How now, Master Ford!
lines: How now, Master Ford!

speeches.speech: You heard what this knave told me, did you not?
lines: You heard what this knave told me, did you not?

speeches.speech: Yes: and you heard what the other told me?
lines: Yes: and you heard what the other told me?

speeches.speech: Do you think there is truth in them?
lines: Do you think there is truth in them?

speeches.speech: Hang 'em, slaves! I do not think the knight would offer it: but these that accuse him in his intent towards our wives are a yoke of his discarded men; very rogues, now they be out of service.
lines: Hang 'em, slaves! I do not think the knight would
offer it: but these that accuse him in his intent
towards our wives are a yoke of his discarded men;
very rogues, now they be out of service.

speeches.speech: Were they his men?
lines: Were they his men?

speeches.speech: Marry, were they.
lines: Marry, were they.

speeches.speech: I like it never the better for that. Does he lie at the Garter?
lines: I like it never the better for that. Does he lie at
the Garter?

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, does he. If he should intend this voyage towards my wife, I would turn her loose to him; and what he gets more of her than sharp words, let it lie on my head.
lines: Ay, marry, does he. If he should intend this voyage
towards my wife, I would turn her loose to him; and
what he gets more of her than sharp words, let it
lie on my head.

speeches.speech: I do not misdoubt my wife; but I would be loath to turn them together. A man may be too confident: I would have nothing lie on my head: I cannot be thus satisfied.
lines: I do not misdoubt my wife; but I would be loath to
turn them together. A man may be too confident: I
would have nothing lie on my head: I cannot be thus satisfied.

speeches.speech: Look where my ranting host of the Garter comes: there is either liquor in his pate or money in his purse when he looks so merrily. [Enter Host] How now, mine host!
lines: Look where my ranting host of the Garter comes:
there is either liquor in his pate or money in his
purse when he looks so merrily.
[Enter Host]
How now, mine host!

speeches.speech: How now, bully-rook! thou'rt a gentleman. Cavaleiro-justice, I say!
lines: How now, bully-rook! thou'rt a gentleman.
Cavaleiro-justice, I say!

speeches.speech: I follow, mine host, I follow. Good even and twenty, good Master Page! Master Page, will you go with us? we have sport in hand.
lines: I follow, mine host, I follow. Good even and
twenty, good Master Page! Master Page, will you go
with us? we have sport in hand.

speeches.speech: Tell him, cavaleiro-justice; tell him, bully-rook.
lines: Tell him, cavaleiro-justice; tell him, bully-rook.

speeches.speech: Sir, there is a fray to be fought between Sir Hugh the Welsh priest and Caius the French doctor.
lines: Sir, there is a fray to be fought between Sir Hugh
the Welsh priest and Caius the French doctor.

speeches.speech: Good mine host o' the Garter, a word with you.
lines: Good mine host o' the Garter, a word with you.

speeches.speech: What sayest thou, my bully-rook?
lines: What sayest thou, my bully-rook?

speeches.speech: [To PAGE] Will you go with us to behold it? My merry host hath had the measuring of their weapons; and, I think, hath appointed them contrary places; for, believe me, I hear the parson is no jester. Hark, I will tell you what our sport shall be.
lines: [To PAGE] Will you go with us to behold it? My
merry host hath had the measuring of their weapons;
and, I think, hath appointed them contrary places;
for, believe me, I hear the parson is no jester.
Hark, I will tell you what our sport shall be.

speeches.speech: Hast thou no suit against my knight, my guest-cavaleire?
lines: Hast thou no suit against my knight, my
guest-cavaleire?

speeches.speech: None, I protest: but I'll give you a pottle of burnt sack to give me recourse to him and tell him my name is Brook; only for a jest.
lines: None, I protest: but I'll give you a pottle of
burnt sack to give me recourse to him and tell him
my name is Brook; only for a jest.

speeches.speech: My hand, bully; thou shalt have egress and regress; --said I well?--and thy name shall be Brook. It is a merry knight. Will you go, An-heires?
lines: My hand, bully; thou shalt have egress and regress;
--said I well?--and thy name shall be Brook. It is
a merry knight. Will you go, An-heires?

speeches.speech: Have with you, mine host.
lines: Have with you, mine host.

speeches.speech: I have heard the Frenchman hath good skill in his rapier.
lines: I have heard the Frenchman hath good skill in
his rapier.

speeches.speech: Tut, sir, I could have told you more. In these times you stand on distance, your passes, stoccadoes, and I know not what: 'tis the heart, Master Page; 'tis here, 'tis here. I have seen the time, with my long sword I would have made you four tall fellows skip like rats.
lines: Tut, sir, I could have told you more. In these times
you stand on distance, your passes, stoccadoes, and
I know not what: 'tis the heart, Master Page; 'tis
here, 'tis here. I have seen the time, with my long
sword I would have made you four tall fellows skip like rats.

speeches.speech: Here, boys, here, here! shall we wag?
lines: Here, boys, here, here! shall we wag?

speeches.speech: Have with you. I would rather hear them scold than fight.
lines: Have with you. I would rather hear them scold than fight.

speeches.speech: Though Page be a secure fool, an stands so firmly on his wife's frailty, yet I cannot put off my opinion so easily: she was in his company at Page's house; and what they made there, I know not. Well, I will look further into't: and I have a disguise to sound Falstaff. If I find her honest, I lose not my labour; if she be otherwise, 'tis labour well bestowed.
lines: Though Page be a secure fool, an stands so firmly
on his wife's frailty, yet I cannot put off my
opinion so easily: she was in his company at Page's
house; and what they made there, I know not. Well,
I will look further into't: and I have a disguise
to sound Falstaff. If I find her honest, I lose not
my labour; if she be otherwise, 'tis labour well bestowed.

speeches.speech: I will not lend thee a penny.
lines: I will not lend thee a penny.

speeches.speech: Why, then the world's mine oyster. Which I with sword will open.
lines: Why, then the world's mine oyster.
Which I with sword will open.

speeches.speech: Not a penny. I have been content, sir, you should lay my countenance to pawn; I have grated upon my good friends for three reprieves for you and your coach-fellow Nym; or else you had looked through the grate, like a geminy of baboons. I am damned in hell for swearing to gentlemen my friends, you were good soldiers and tall fellows; and when Mistress Bridget lost the handle of her fan, I took't upon mine honour thou hadst it not.
lines: Not a penny. I have been content, sir, you should
lay my countenance to pawn; I have grated upon my
good friends for three reprieves for you and your
coach-fellow Nym; or else you had looked through
the grate, like a geminy of baboons. I am damned in
hell for swearing to gentlemen my friends, you were
good soldiers and tall fellows; and when Mistress
Bridget lost the handle of her fan, I took't upon
mine honour thou hadst it not.

speeches.speech: Didst not thou share? hadst thou not fifteen pence?
lines: Didst not thou share? hadst thou not fifteen pence?

speeches.speech: Reason, you rogue, reason: thinkest thou I'll endanger my soul gratis? At a word, hang no more about me, I am no gibbet for you. Go. A short knife and a throng! To your manor of Pickt-hatch! Go. You'll not bear a letter for me, you rogue! you stand upon your honour! Why, thou unconfinable baseness, it is as much as I can do to keep the terms of my honour precise: I, I, I myself sometimes, leaving the fear of God on the left hand and hiding mine honour in my necessity, am fain to shuffle, to hedge and to lurch; and yet you, rogue, will ensconce your rags, your cat-a-mountain looks, your red-lattice phrases, and your bold-beating oaths, under the shelter of your honour! You will not do it, you!
lines: Reason, you rogue, reason: thinkest thou I'll
endanger my soul gratis? At a word, hang no more
about me, I am no gibbet for you. Go. A short knife
and a throng! To your manor of Pickt-hatch! Go.
You'll not bear a letter for me, you rogue! you
stand upon your honour! Why, thou unconfinable
baseness, it is as much as I can do to keep the
terms of my honour precise: I, I, I myself
sometimes, leaving the fear of God on the left hand
and hiding mine honour in my necessity, am fain to
shuffle, to hedge and to lurch; and yet you, rogue,
will ensconce your rags, your cat-a-mountain
looks, your red-lattice phrases, and your
bold-beating oaths, under the shelter of your
honour! You will not do it, you!

speeches.speech: I do relent: what would thou more of man?
lines: I do relent: what would thou more of man?

speeches.speech: Sir, here's a woman would speak with you.
lines: Sir, here's a woman would speak with you.

speeches.speech: Let her approach.
lines: Let her approach.

speeches.speech: Give your worship good morrow.
lines: Give your worship good morrow.

speeches.speech: Good morrow, good wife.
lines: Good morrow, good wife.

speeches.speech: Not so, an't please your worship.
lines: Not so, an't please your worship.

speeches.speech: Good maid, then.
lines: Good maid, then.

speeches.speech: I'll be sworn, As my mother was, the first hour I was born.
lines: I'll be sworn,
As my mother was, the first hour I was born.

speeches.speech: I do believe the swearer. What with me?
lines: I do believe the swearer. What with me?

speeches.speech: Shall I vouchsafe your worship a word or two?
lines: Shall I vouchsafe your worship a word or two?

speeches.speech: Two thousand, fair woman: and I'll vouchsafe thee the hearing.
lines: Two thousand, fair woman: and I'll vouchsafe thee
the hearing.

speeches.speech: There is one Mistress Ford, sir:--I pray, come a little nearer this ways:--I myself dwell with master Doctor Caius,--
lines: There is one Mistress Ford, sir:--I pray, come a
little nearer this ways:--I myself dwell with master
Doctor Caius,--

speeches.speech: Well, on: Mistress Ford, you say,--
lines: Well, on: Mistress Ford, you say,--

speeches.speech: Your worship says very true: I pray your worship, come a little nearer this ways.
lines: Your worship says very true: I pray your worship,
come a little nearer this ways.

speeches.speech: I warrant thee, nobody hears; mine own people, mine own people.
lines: I warrant thee, nobody hears; mine own people, mine
own people.

speeches.speech: Are they so? God bless them and make them his servants!
lines: Are they so? God bless them and make them his servants!

speeches.speech: Well, Mistress Ford; what of her?
lines: Well, Mistress Ford; what of her?

speeches.speech: Why, sir, she's a good creature. Lord Lord! your worship's a wanton! Well, heaven forgive you and all of us, I pray!
lines: Why, sir, she's a good creature. Lord Lord! your
worship's a wanton! Well, heaven forgive you and all
of us, I pray!

speeches.speech: Mistress Ford; come, Mistress Ford,--
lines: Mistress Ford; come, Mistress Ford,--

speeches.speech: Marry, this is the short and the long of it; you have brought her into such a canaries as 'tis wonderful. The best courtier of them all, when the court lay at Windsor, could never have brought her to such a canary. Yet there has been knights, and lords, and gentlemen, with their coaches, I warrant you, coach after coach, letter after letter, gift after gift; smelling so sweetly, all musk, and so rushling, I warrant you, in silk and gold; and in such alligant terms; and in such wine and sugar of the best and the fairest, that would have won any woman's heart; and, I warrant you, they could never get an eye-wink of her: I had myself twenty angels given me this morning; but I defy all angels, in any such sort, as they say, but in the way of honesty: and, I warrant you, they could never get her so much as sip on a cup with the proudest of them all: and yet there has been earls, nay, which is more, pensioners; but, I warrant you, all is one with her.
lines: Marry, this is the short and the long of it; you
have brought her into such a canaries as 'tis
wonderful. The best courtier of them all, when the
court lay at Windsor, could never have brought her
to such a canary. Yet there has been knights, and
lords, and gentlemen, with their coaches, I warrant
you, coach after coach, letter after letter, gift
after gift; smelling so sweetly, all musk, and so
rushling, I warrant you, in silk and gold; and in
such alligant terms; and in such wine and sugar of
the best and the fairest, that would have won any
woman's heart; and, I warrant you, they could never
get an eye-wink of her: I had myself twenty angels
given me this morning; but I defy all angels, in
any such sort, as they say, but in the way of
honesty: and, I warrant you, they could never get
her so much as sip on a cup with the proudest of
them all: and yet there has been earls, nay, which
is more, pensioners; but, I warrant you, all is one with her.

speeches.speech: But what says she to me? be brief, my good she-Mercury.
lines: But what says she to me? be brief, my good
she-Mercury.

speeches.speech: Marry, she hath received your letter, for the which she thanks you a thousand times; and she gives you to notify that her husband will be absence from his house between ten and eleven.
lines: Marry, she hath received your letter, for the which
she thanks you a thousand times; and she gives you
to notify that her husband will be absence from his
house between ten and eleven.

speeches.speech: Ten and eleven?
lines: Ten and eleven?

speeches.speech: Ay, forsooth; and then you may come and see the picture, she says, that you wot of: Master Ford, her husband, will be from home. Alas! the sweet woman leads an ill life with him: he's a very jealousy man: she leads a very frampold life with him, good heart.
lines: Ay, forsooth; and then you may come and see the
picture, she says, that you wot of: Master Ford,
her husband, will be from home. Alas! the sweet
woman leads an ill life with him: he's a very
jealousy man: she leads a very frampold life with
him, good heart.

speeches.speech: Ten and eleven. Woman, commend me to her; I will not fail her.
lines: Ten and eleven. Woman, commend me to her; I will
not fail her.

speeches.speech: Why, you say well. But I have another messenger to your worship.
