Mistress Page hath her hearty commendations to you too: and let me tell you in your ear, she's as fartuous a civil modest wife, and one, I tell you, that will not miss you morning nor evening prayer, as any is in Windsor, whoe'er be the other: and she bade me tell your worship that her husband is seldom from home; but she hopes there will come a time. I never knew a woman so dote upon a man: surely I think you have charms, la; yes, in truth.
lines: Why, you say well. But I have another messenger to
your worship. Mistress Page hath her hearty
commendations to you too: and let me tell you in
your ear, she's as fartuous a civil modest wife, and
one, I tell you, that will not miss you morning nor
evening prayer, as any is in Windsor, whoe'er be the
other: and she bade me tell your worship that her
husband is seldom from home; but she hopes there
will come a time. I never knew a woman so dote upon
a man: surely I think you have charms, la; yes, in truth.

speeches.speech: Not I, I assure thee: setting the attractions of my good parts aside I have no other charms.
lines: Not I, I assure thee: setting the attractions of my
good parts aside I have no other charms.

speeches.speech: Blessing on your heart for't!
lines: Blessing on your heart for't!

speeches.speech: But, I pray thee, tell me this: has Ford's wife and Page's wife acquainted each other how they love me?
lines: But, I pray thee, tell me this: has Ford's wife and
Page's wife acquainted each other how they love me?

speeches.speech: That were a jest indeed! they have not so little grace, I hope: that were a trick indeed! but Mistress Page would desire you to send her your little page, of all loves: her husband has a marvellous infection to the little page; and truly Master Page is an honest man. Never a wife in Windsor leads a better life than she does: do what she will, say what she will, take all, pay all, go to bed when she list, rise when she list, all is as she will: and truly she deserves it; for if there be a kind woman in Windsor, she is one. You must send her your page; no remedy.
lines: That were a jest indeed! they have not so little
grace, I hope: that were a trick indeed! but
Mistress Page would desire you to send her your
little page, of all loves: her husband has a
marvellous infection to the little page; and truly
Master Page is an honest man. Never a wife in
Windsor leads a better life than she does: do what
she will, say what she will, take all, pay all, go
to bed when she list, rise when she list, all is as
she will: and truly she deserves it; for if there
be a kind woman in Windsor, she is one. You must
send her your page; no remedy.

speeches.speech: Why, I will.
lines: Why, I will.

speeches.speech: Nay, but do so, then: and, look you, he may come and go between you both; and in any case have a nay-word, that you may know one another's mind, and the boy never need to understand any thing; for 'tis not good that children should know any wickedness: old folks, you know, have discretion, as they say, and know the world.
lines: Nay, but do so, then: and, look you, he may come and
go between you both; and in any case have a
nay-word, that you may know one another's mind, and
the boy never need to understand any thing; for
'tis not good that children should know any
wickedness: old folks, you know, have discretion,
as they say, and know the world.

speeches.speech: Fare thee well: commend me to them both: there's my purse; I am yet thy debtor. Boy, go along with this woman. [Exeunt MISTRESS QUICKLY and ROBIN] This news distracts me!
lines: Fare thee well: commend me to them both: there's
my purse; I am yet thy debtor. Boy, go along with
this woman.
[Exeunt MISTRESS QUICKLY and ROBIN]
This news distracts me!

speeches.speech: This punk is one of Cupid's carriers: Clap on more sails; pursue; up with your fights: Give fire: she is my prize, or ocean whelm them all!
lines: This punk is one of Cupid's carriers:
Clap on more sails; pursue; up with your fights:
Give fire: she is my prize, or ocean whelm them all!

speeches.speech: Sayest thou so, old Jack? go thy ways; I'll make more of thy old body than I have done. Will they yet look after thee? Wilt thou, after the expense of so much money, be now a gainer? Good body, I thank thee. Let them say 'tis grossly done; so it be fairly done, no matter.
lines: Sayest thou so, old Jack? go thy ways; I'll make
more of thy old body than I have done. Will they
yet look after thee? Wilt thou, after the expense
of so much money, be now a gainer? Good body, I
thank thee. Let them say 'tis grossly done; so it be
fairly done, no matter.

speeches.speech: Sir John, there's one Master Brook below would fain speak with you, and be acquainted with you; and hath sent your worship a morning's draught of sack.
lines: Sir John, there's one Master Brook below would fain
speak with you, and be acquainted with you; and hath
sent your worship a morning's draught of sack.

speeches.speech: Brook is his name?
lines: Brook is his name?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir.
lines: Ay, sir.

speeches.speech: Call him in. [Exit BARDOLPH] Such Brooks are welcome to me, that o'erflow such liquor. Ah, ha! Mistress Ford and Mistress Page have I encompassed you? go to; via!
lines: Call him in.
[Exit BARDOLPH]
Such Brooks are welcome to me, that o'erflow such
liquor. Ah, ha! Mistress Ford and Mistress Page
have I encompassed you? go to; via!

speeches.speech: Bless you, sir!
lines: Bless you, sir!

speeches.speech: And you, sir! Would you speak with me?
lines: And you, sir! Would you speak with me?

speeches.speech: I make bold to press with so little preparation upon you.
lines: I make bold to press with so little preparation upon
you.

speeches.speech: You're welcome. What's your will? Give us leave, drawer.
lines: You're welcome. What's your will? Give us leave, drawer.

speeches.speech: Sir, I am a gentleman that have spent much; my name is Brook.
lines: Sir, I am a gentleman that have spent much; my name is Brook.

speeches.speech: Good Master Brook, I desire more acquaintance of you.
lines: Good Master Brook, I desire more acquaintance of you.

speeches.speech: Good Sir John, I sue for yours: not to charge you; for I must let you understand I think myself in better plight for a lender than you are: the which hath something embolden'd me to this unseasoned intrusion; for they say, if money go before, all ways do lie open.
lines: Good Sir John, I sue for yours: not to charge you;
for I must let you understand I think myself in
better plight for a lender than you are: the which
hath something embolden'd me to this unseasoned
intrusion; for they say, if money go before, all
ways do lie open.

speeches.speech: Money is a good soldier, sir, and will on.
lines: Money is a good soldier, sir, and will on.

speeches.speech: Troth, and I have a bag of money here troubles me: if you will help to bear it, Sir John, take all, or half, for easing me of the carriage.
lines: Troth, and I have a bag of money here troubles me:
if you will help to bear it, Sir John, take all, or
half, for easing me of the carriage.

speeches.speech: Sir, I know not how I may deserve to be your porter.
lines: Sir, I know not how I may deserve to be your porter.

speeches.speech: I will tell you, sir, if you will give me the hearing.
lines: I will tell you, sir, if you will give me the hearing.

speeches.speech: Speak, good Master Brook: I shall be glad to be your servant.
lines: Speak, good Master Brook: I shall be glad to be
your servant.

speeches.speech: Sir, I hear you are a scholar,--I will be brief with you,--and you have been a man long known to me, though I had never so good means, as desire, to make myself acquainted with you. I shall discover a thing to you, wherein I must very much lay open mine own imperfection: but, good Sir John, as you have one eye upon my follies, as you hear them unfolded, turn another into the register of your own; that I may pass with a reproof the easier, sith you yourself know how easy it is to be such an offender.
lines: Sir, I hear you are a scholar,--I will be brief
with you,--and you have been a man long known to me,
though I had never so good means, as desire, to make
myself acquainted with you. I shall discover a
thing to you, wherein I must very much lay open mine
own imperfection: but, good Sir John, as you have
one eye upon my follies, as you hear them unfolded,
turn another into the register of your own; that I
may pass with a reproof the easier, sith you
yourself know how easy it is to be such an offender.

speeches.speech: Very well, sir; proceed.
lines: Very well, sir; proceed.

speeches.speech: There is a gentlewoman in this town; her husband's name is Ford.
lines: There is a gentlewoman in this town; her husband's
name is Ford.

speeches.speech: Well, sir.
lines: Well, sir.

speeches.speech: I have long loved her, and, I protest to you, bestowed much on her; followed her with a doting observance; engrossed opportunities to meet her; fee'd every slight occasion that could but niggardly give me sight of her; not only bought many presents to give her, but have given largely to many to know what she would have given; briefly, I have pursued her as love hath pursued me; which hath been on the wing of all occasions. But whatsoever I have merited, either in my mind or, in my means, meed, I am sure, I have received none; unless experience be a jewel that I have purchased at an infinite rate, and that hath taught me to say this: 'Love like a shadow flies when substance love pursues; Pursuing that that flies, and flying what pursues.'
lines: I have long loved her, and, I protest to you,
bestowed much on her; followed her with a doting
observance; engrossed opportunities to meet her;
fee'd every slight occasion that could but niggardly
give me sight of her; not only bought many presents
to give her, but have given largely to many to know
what she would have given; briefly, I have pursued
her as love hath pursued me; which hath been on the
wing of all occasions. But whatsoever I have
merited, either in my mind or, in my means, meed,
I am sure, I have received none; unless experience
be a jewel that I have purchased at an infinite
rate, and that hath taught me to say this:
'Love like a shadow flies when substance love pursues;
Pursuing that that flies, and flying what pursues.'

speeches.speech: Have you received no promise of satisfaction at her hands?
lines: Have you received no promise of satisfaction at her hands?

speeches.speech: Never.
lines: Never.

speeches.speech: Have you importuned her to such a purpose?
lines: Have you importuned her to such a purpose?

speeches.speech: Never.
lines: Never.

speeches.speech: Of what quality was your love, then?
lines: Of what quality was your love, then?

speeches.speech: Like a fair house built on another man's ground; so that I have lost my edifice by mistaking the place where I erected it.
lines: Like a fair house built on another man's ground; so
that I have lost my edifice by mistaking the place
where I erected it.

speeches.speech: To what purpose have you unfolded this to me?
lines: To what purpose have you unfolded this to me?

speeches.speech: When I have told you that, I have told you all. Some say, that though she appear honest to me, yet in other places she enlargeth her mirth so far that there is shrewd construction made of her. Now, Sir John, here is the heart of my purpose: you are a gentleman of excellent breeding, admirable discourse, of great admittance, authentic in your place and person, generally allowed for your many war-like, court-like, and learned preparations.
lines: When I have told you that, I have told you all.
Some say, that though she appear honest to me, yet in
other places she enlargeth her mirth so far that
there is shrewd construction made of her. Now, Sir
John, here is the heart of my purpose: you are a
gentleman of excellent breeding, admirable
discourse, of great admittance, authentic in your
place and person, generally allowed for your many
war-like, court-like, and learned preparations.

speeches.speech: O, sir!
lines: O, sir!

speeches.speech: Believe it, for you know it. There is money; spend it, spend it; spend more; spend all I have; only give me so much of your time in exchange of it, as to lay an amiable siege to the honesty of this Ford's wife: use your art of wooing; win her to consent to you: if any man may, you may as soon as any.
lines: Believe it, for you know it. There is money; spend
it, spend it; spend more; spend all I have; only
give me so much of your time in exchange of it, as
to lay an amiable siege to the honesty of this
Ford's wife: use your art of wooing; win her to
consent to you: if any man may, you may as soon as
any.

speeches.speech: Would it apply well to the vehemency of your affection, that I should win what you would enjoy? Methinks you prescribe to yourself very preposterously.
lines: Would it apply well to the vehemency of your
affection, that I should win what you would enjoy?
Methinks you prescribe to yourself very preposterously.

speeches.speech: O, understand my drift. She dwells so securely on the excellency of her honour, that the folly of my soul dares not present itself: she is too bright to be looked against. Now, could I could come to her with any detection in my hand, my desires had instance and argument to commend themselves: I could drive her then from the ward of her purity, her reputation, her marriage-vow, and a thousand other her defences, which now are too too strongly embattled against me. What say you to't, Sir John?
lines: O, understand my drift. She dwells so securely on
the excellency of her honour, that the folly of my
soul dares not present itself: she is too bright to
be looked against. Now, could I could come to her
with any detection in my hand, my desires had
instance and argument to commend themselves: I
could drive her then from the ward of her purity,
her reputation, her marriage-vow, and a thousand
other her defences, which now are too too strongly
embattled against me. What say you to't, Sir John?

speeches.speech: Master Brook, I will first make bold with your money; next, give me your hand; and last, as I am a gentleman, you shall, if you will, enjoy Ford's wife.
lines: Master Brook, I will first make bold with your
money; next, give me your hand; and last, as I am a
gentleman, you shall, if you will, enjoy Ford's wife.

speeches.speech: O good sir!
lines: O good sir!

speeches.speech: I say you shall.
lines: I say you shall.

speeches.speech: Want no money, Sir John; you shall want none.
lines: Want no money, Sir John; you shall want none.

speeches.speech: Want no Mistress Ford, Master Brook; you shall want none. I shall be with her, I may tell you, by her own appointment; even as you came in to me, her assistant or go-between parted from me: I say I shall be with her between ten and eleven; for at that time the jealous rascally knave her husband will be forth. Come you to me at night; you shall know how I speed.
lines: Want no Mistress Ford, Master Brook; you shall want
none. I shall be with her, I may tell you, by her
own appointment; even as you came in to me, her
assistant or go-between parted from me: I say I
shall be with her between ten and eleven; for at
that time the jealous rascally knave her husband
will be forth. Come you to me at night; you shall
know how I speed.

speeches.speech: I am blest in your acquaintance. Do you know Ford, sir?
lines: I am blest in your acquaintance. Do you know Ford,
sir?

speeches.speech: Hang him, poor cuckoldly knave! I know him not: yet I wrong him to call him poor; they say the jealous wittolly knave hath masses of money; for the which his wife seems to me well-favored. I will use her as the key of the cuckoldly rogue's coffer; and there's my harvest-home.
lines: Hang him, poor cuckoldly knave! I know him not:
yet I wrong him to call him poor; they say the
jealous wittolly knave hath masses of money; for the
which his wife seems to me well-favored. I will
use her as the key of the cuckoldly rogue's coffer;
and there's my harvest-home.

speeches.speech: I would you knew Ford, sir, that you might avoid him if you saw him.
lines: I would you knew Ford, sir, that you might avoid him
if you saw him.

speeches.speech: Hang him, mechanical salt-butter rogue! I will stare him out of his wits; I will awe him with my cudgel: it shall hang like a meteor o'er the cuckold's horns. Master Brook, thou shalt know I will predominate over the peasant, and thou shalt lie with his wife. Come to me soon at night. Ford's a knave, and I will aggravate his style; thou, Master Brook, shalt know him for knave and cuckold. Come to me soon at night.
lines: Hang him, mechanical salt-butter rogue! I will
stare him out of his wits; I will awe him with my
cudgel: it shall hang like a meteor o'er the
cuckold's horns. Master Brook, thou shalt know I
will predominate over the peasant, and thou shalt
lie with his wife. Come to me soon at night.
Ford's a knave, and I will aggravate his style;
thou, Master Brook, shalt know him for knave and
cuckold. Come to me soon at night.

speeches.speech: What a damned Epicurean rascal is this! My heart is ready to crack with impatience. Who says this is improvident jealousy? my wife hath sent to him; the hour is fixed; the match is made. Would any man have thought this? See the hell of having a false woman! My bed shall be abused, my coffers ransacked, my reputation gnawn at; and I shall not only receive this villanous wrong, but stand under the adoption of abominable terms, and by him that does me this wrong. Terms! names! Amaimon sounds well; Lucifer, well; Barbason, well; yet they are devils' additions, the names of fiends: but Cuckold! Wittol!--Cuckold! the devil himself hath not such a name. Page is an ass, a secure ass: he will trust his wife; he will not be jealous. I will rather trust a Fleming with my butter, Parson Hugh the Welshman with my cheese, an Irishman with my aqua-vitae bottle, or a thief to walk my ambling gelding, than my wife with herself; then she plots, then she ruminates, then she devises; and what they think in their hearts they may effect, they will break their hearts but they will effect. God be praised for my jealousy! Eleven o'clock the hour. I will prevent this, detect my wife, be revenged on Falstaff, and laugh at Page. I will about it; better three hours too soon than a minute too late. Fie, fie, fie! cuckold! cuckold! cuckold!
lines: What a damned Epicurean rascal is this! My heart is
ready to crack with impatience. Who says this is
improvident jealousy? my wife hath sent to him; the
hour is fixed; the match is made. Would any man
have thought this? See the hell of having a false
woman! My bed shall be abused, my coffers
ransacked, my reputation gnawn at; and I shall not
only receive this villanous wrong, but stand under
the adoption of abominable terms, and by him that
does me this wrong. Terms! names! Amaimon sounds
well; Lucifer, well; Barbason, well; yet they are
devils' additions, the names of fiends: but
Cuckold! Wittol!--Cuckold! the devil himself hath
not such a name. Page is an ass, a secure ass: he
will trust his wife; he will not be jealous. I will
rather trust a Fleming with my butter, Parson Hugh
the Welshman with my cheese, an Irishman with my
aqua-vitae bottle, or a thief to walk my ambling
gelding, than my wife with herself; then she plots,
then she ruminates, then she devises; and what they
think in their hearts they may effect, they will
break their hearts but they will effect. God be
praised for my jealousy! Eleven o'clock the hour.
I will prevent this, detect my wife, be revenged on
Falstaff, and laugh at Page. I will about it;
better three hours too soon than a minute too late.
Fie, fie, fie! cuckold! cuckold! cuckold!

speeches.speech: Jack Rugby!
lines: Jack Rugby!

speeches.speech: Sir?
lines: Sir?

speeches.speech: Vat is de clock, Jack?
lines: Vat is de clock, Jack?

speeches.speech: 'Tis past the hour, sir, that Sir Hugh promised to meet.
lines: 'Tis past the hour, sir, that Sir Hugh promised to meet.

speeches.speech: By gar, he has save his soul, dat he is no come; he has pray his Pible well, dat he is no come: by gar, Jack Rugby, he is dead already, if he be come.
lines: By gar, he has save his soul, dat he is no come; he
has pray his Pible well, dat he is no come: by gar,
Jack Rugby, he is dead already, if he be come.

speeches.speech: He is wise, sir; he knew your worship would kill him, if he came.
lines: He is wise, sir; he knew your worship would kill
him, if he came.

speeches.speech: By gar, de herring is no dead so as I vill kill him. Take your rapier, Jack; I vill tell you how I vill kill him.
lines: By gar, de herring is no dead so as I vill kill him.
Take your rapier, Jack; I vill tell you how I vill kill him.

speeches.speech: Alas, sir, I cannot fence.
lines: Alas, sir, I cannot fence.

speeches.speech: Villany, take your rapier.
lines: Villany, take your rapier.

speeches.speech: Forbear; here's company.
lines: Forbear; here's company.

speeches.speech: Bless thee, bully doctor!
lines: Bless thee, bully doctor!

speeches.speech: Save you, Master Doctor Caius!
lines: Save you, Master Doctor Caius!

speeches.speech: Now, good master doctor!
lines: Now, good master doctor!

speeches.speech: Give you good morrow, sir.
lines: Give you good morrow, sir.

speeches.speech: Vat be all you, one, two, tree, four, come for?
lines: Vat be all you, one, two, tree, four, come for?

speeches.speech: To see thee fight, to see thee foin, to see thee traverse; to see thee here, to see thee there; to see thee pass thy punto, thy stock, thy reverse, thy distance, thy montant. Is he dead, my Ethiopian? is he dead, my Francisco? ha, bully! What says my AEsculapius? my Galen? my heart of elder? ha! is he dead, bully stale? is he dead?
lines: To see thee fight, to see thee foin, to see thee
traverse; to see thee here, to see thee there; to
see thee pass thy punto, thy stock, thy reverse, thy
distance, thy montant. Is he dead, my Ethiopian? is
he dead, my Francisco? ha, bully! What says my
AEsculapius? my Galen? my heart of elder? ha! is
he dead, bully stale? is he dead?

speeches.speech: By gar, he is de coward Jack priest of de vorld; he is not show his face.
lines: By gar, he is de coward Jack priest of de vorld; he
is not show his face.

speeches.speech: Thou art a Castalion-King-Urinal. Hector of Greece, my boy!
lines: Thou art a Castalion-King-Urinal. Hector of Greece, my boy!

speeches.speech: I pray you, bear vitness that me have stay six or seven, two, tree hours for him, and he is no come.
lines: I pray you, bear vitness that me have stay six or
seven, two, tree hours for him, and he is no come.

speeches.speech: He is the wiser man, master doctor: he is a curer of souls, and you a curer of bodies; if you should fight, you go against the hair of your professions. Is it not true, Master Page?
lines: He is the wiser man, master doctor: he is a curer of
souls, and you a curer of bodies; if you should
fight, you go against the hair of your professions.
Is it not true, Master Page?

speeches.speech: Master Shallow, you have yourself been a great fighter, though now a man of peace.
lines: Master Shallow, you have yourself been a great
fighter, though now a man of peace.

speeches.speech: Bodykins, Master Page, though I now be old and of the peace, if I see a sword out, my finger itches to make one. Though we are justices and doctors and churchmen, Master Page, we have some salt of our youth in us; we are the sons of women, Master Page.
lines: Bodykins, Master Page, though I now be old and of
the peace, if I see a sword out, my finger itches to
make one. Though we are justices and doctors and
churchmen, Master Page, we have some salt of our
youth in us; we are the sons of women, Master Page.

speeches.speech: 'Tis true, Master Shallow.
lines: 'Tis true, Master Shallow.

speeches.speech: It will be found so, Master Page. Master Doctor Caius, I am come to fetch you home. I am sworn of the peace: you have showed yourself a wise physician, and Sir Hugh hath shown himself a wise and patient churchman. You must go with me, master doctor.
lines: It will be found so, Master Page. Master Doctor
Caius, I am come to fetch you home. I am sworn of
the peace: you have showed yourself a wise
physician, and Sir Hugh hath shown himself a wise
and patient churchman. You must go with me, master doctor.

speeches.speech: Pardon, guest-justice. A word, Mounseur Mockwater.
lines: Pardon, guest-justice. A word, Mounseur Mockwater.

speeches.speech: Mock-vater! vat is dat?
lines: Mock-vater! vat is dat?

speeches.speech: Mock-water, in our English tongue, is valour, bully.
lines: Mock-water, in our English tongue, is valour, bully.

speeches.speech: By gar, den, I have as mush mock-vater as de Englishman. Scurvy jack-dog priest! by gar, me vill cut his ears.
lines: By gar, den, I have as mush mock-vater as de
Englishman. Scurvy jack-dog priest! by gar, me
vill cut his ears.

speeches.speech: He will clapper-claw thee tightly, bully.
lines: He will clapper-claw thee tightly, bully.

speeches.speech: Clapper-de-claw! vat is dat?
lines: Clapper-de-claw! vat is dat?

speeches.speech: That is, he will make thee amends.
lines: That is, he will make thee amends.

speeches.speech: By gar, me do look he shall clapper-de-claw me; for, by gar, me vill have it.
lines: By gar, me do look he shall clapper-de-claw me;
for, by gar, me vill have it.

speeches.speech: And I will provoke him to't, or let him wag.
lines: And I will provoke him to't, or let him wag.

speeches.speech: Me tank you for dat.
lines: Me tank you for dat.

speeches.speech: And, moreover, bully,--but first, master guest, and Master Page, and eke Cavaleiro Slender, go you through the town to Frogmore.
lines: And, moreover, bully,--but first, master guest, and
Master Page, and eke Cavaleiro Slender, go you
through the town to Frogmore.

speeches.speech: Sir Hugh is there, is he?
lines: Sir Hugh is there, is he?

speeches.speech: He is there: see what humour he is in; and I will bring the doctor about by the fields. Will it do well?
lines: He is there: see what humour he is in; and I will
bring the doctor about by the fields. Will it do well?

speeches.speech: We will do it.
lines: We will do it.

speeches.speech: [with Shallow and Slender] Adieu, good master doctor.
lines: [with Shallow and Slender] Adieu, good master doctor.

speeches.speech: By gar, me vill kill de priest; for he speak for a jack-an-ape to Anne Page.
lines: By gar, me vill kill de priest; for he speak for a
jack-an-ape to Anne Page.

speeches.speech: Let him die: sheathe thy impatience, throw cold water on thy choler: go about the fields with me through Frogmore: I will bring thee where Mistress Anne Page is, at a farm-house a-feasting; and thou shalt woo her. Cried I aim? said I well?
lines: Let him die: sheathe thy impatience, throw cold
water on thy choler: go about the fields with me
through Frogmore: I will bring thee where Mistress
Anne Page is, at a farm-house a-feasting; and thou
shalt woo her. Cried I aim? said I well?

speeches.speech: By gar, me dank you for dat: by gar, I love you; and I shall procure-a you de good guest, de earl, de knight, de lords, de gentlemen, my patients.
lines: By gar, me dank you for dat: by gar, I love you;
and I shall procure-a you de good guest, de earl,
de knight, de lords, de gentlemen, my patients.

speeches.speech: For the which I will be thy adversary toward Anne Page. Said I well?
lines: For the which I will be thy adversary toward Anne
Page. Said I well?

speeches.speech: By gar, 'tis good; vell said.
lines: By gar, 'tis good; vell said.

speeches.speech: Let us wag, then.
lines: Let us wag, then.

speeches.speech: Come at my heels, Jack Rugby.
lines: Come at my heels, Jack Rugby.

speeches.speech: I pray you now, good master Slender's serving-man, and friend Simple by your name, which way have you looked for Master Caius, that calls himself doctor of physic?
lines: I pray you now, good master Slender's serving-man,
and friend Simple by your name, which way have you
looked for Master Caius, that calls himself doctor of physic?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, the pittie-ward, the park-ward, every way; old Windsor way, and every way but the town way.
lines: Marry, sir, the pittie-ward, the park-ward, every
way; old Windsor way, and every way but the town
way.

speeches.speech: I most fehemently desire you you will also look that way.
lines: I most fehemently desire you you will also look that
way.

speeches.speech: I will, sir.
lines: I will, sir.

speeches.speech: 'Pless my soul, how full of chollors I am, and trempling of mind! I shall be glad if he have deceived me. How melancholies I am! I will knog his urinals about his knave's costard when I have good opportunities for the ork. 'Pless my soul! [Sings] To shallow rivers, to whose falls Melodious birds sings madrigals; There will we make our peds of roses, And a thousand fragrant posies. To shallow-- Mercy on me! I have a great dispositions to cry. [Sings] Melodious birds sing madrigals-- When as I sat in Pabylon-- And a thousand vagram posies. To shallow &c.
lines: 'Pless my soul, how full of chollors I am, and
trempling of mind! I shall be glad if he have
deceived me. How melancholies I am! I will knog
his urinals about his knave's costard when I have
good opportunities for the ork. 'Pless my soul!
[Sings]
To shallow rivers, to whose falls
Melodious birds sings madrigals;
There will we make our peds of roses,
And a thousand fragrant posies.
To shallow--
Mercy on me! I have a great dispositions to cry.
[Sings]
Melodious birds sing madrigals--
When as I sat in Pabylon--
And a thousand vagram posies.
To shallow &c.

speeches.speech: Yonder he is coming, this way, Sir Hugh.
lines: Yonder he is coming, this way, Sir Hugh.

speeches.speech: He's welcome. [Sings] To shallow rivers, to whose falls- Heaven prosper the right! What weapons is he?
lines: He's welcome.
[Sings]
To shallow rivers, to whose falls-
Heaven prosper the right! What weapons is he?

speeches.speech: No weapons, sir. There comes my master, Master Shallow, and another gentleman, from Frogmore, over the stile, this way.
lines: No weapons, sir. There comes my master, Master
Shallow, and another gentleman, from Frogmore, over
the stile, this way.

speeches.speech: Pray you, give me my gown; or else keep it in your arms.
lines: Pray you, give me my gown; or else keep it in your arms.

speeches.speech: How now, master Parson! Good morrow, good Sir Hugh. Keep a gamester from the dice, and a good student from his book, and it is wonderful.
lines: How now, master Parson! Good morrow, good Sir Hugh.
Keep a gamester from the dice, and a good student
from his book, and it is wonderful.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Ah, sweet Anne Page!
lines: [Aside] Ah, sweet Anne Page!

speeches.speech: 'Save you, good Sir Hugh!
lines: 'Save you, good Sir Hugh!

speeches.speech: 'Pless you from his mercy sake, all of you!
lines: 'Pless you from his mercy sake, all of you!

speeches.speech: What, the sword and the word! do you study them both, master parson?
lines: What, the sword and the word! do you study them
both, master parson?

speeches.speech: And youthful still! in your doublet and hose this raw rheumatic day!
lines: And youthful still! in your doublet and hose this
raw rheumatic day!

speeches.speech: There is reasons and causes for it.
lines: There is reasons and causes for it.

speeches.speech: We are come to you to do a good office, master parson.
lines: We are come to you to do a good office, master parson.

speeches.speech: Fery well: what is it?
lines: Fery well: what is it?

speeches.speech: Yonder is a most reverend gentleman, who, belike having received wrong by some person, is at most odds with his own gravity and patience that ever you saw.
lines: Yonder is a most reverend gentleman, who, belike
having received wrong by some person, is at most
odds with his own gravity and patience that ever you
saw.

speeches.speech: I have lived fourscore years and upward; I never heard a man of his place, gravity and learning, so wide of his own respect.
lines: I have lived fourscore years and upward; I never
heard a man of his place, gravity and learning, so
wide of his own respect.

speeches.speech: What is he?
lines: What is he?

speeches.speech: I think you know him; Master Doctor Caius, the renowned French physician.
lines: I think you know him; Master Doctor Caius, the
renowned French physician.

speeches.speech: Got's will, and his passion of my heart! I had as lief you would tell me of a mess of porridge.
lines: Got's will, and his passion of my heart! I had as
lief you would tell me of a mess of porridge.

speeches.speech: Why?
lines: Why?

speeches.speech: He has no more knowledge in Hibocrates and Galen, --and he is a knave besides; a cowardly knave as you would desires to be acquainted withal.
lines: He has no more knowledge in Hibocrates and Galen,
--and he is a knave besides; a cowardly knave as you
would desires to be acquainted withal.

speeches.speech: I warrant you, he's the man should fight with him.
lines: I warrant you, he's the man should fight with him.

speeches.speech: [Aside] O sweet Anne Page!
lines: [Aside] O sweet Anne Page!

speeches.speech: It appears so by his weapons. Keep them asunder: here comes Doctor Caius.
lines: It appears so by his weapons. Keep them asunder:
here comes Doctor Caius.

speeches.speech: Nay, good master parson, keep in your weapon.
lines: Nay, good master parson, keep in your weapon.

speeches.speech: So do you, good master doctor.
lines: So do you, good master doctor.

speeches.speech: Disarm them, and let them question: let them keep their limbs whole and hack our English.
lines: Disarm them, and let them question: let them keep
their limbs whole and hack our English.

speeches.speech: I pray you, let-a me speak a word with your ear. Vherefore vill you not meet-a me?
lines: I pray you, let-a me speak a word with your ear.
Vherefore vill you not meet-a me?

speeches.speech: [Aside to DOCTOR CAIUS] Pray you, use your patience: in good time.
lines: [Aside to DOCTOR CAIUS] Pray you, use your patience:
in good time.

speeches.speech: By gar, you are de coward, de Jack dog, John ape.
lines: By gar, you are de coward, de Jack dog, John ape.

speeches.speech: [Aside to DOCTOR CAIUS] Pray you let us not be laughing-stocks to other men's humours; I desire you in friendship, and I will one way or other make you amends. [Aloud] I will knog your urinals about your knave's cockscomb for missing your meetings and appointments.
lines: [Aside to DOCTOR CAIUS] Pray you let us not be
laughing-stocks to other men's humours; I desire you
in friendship, and I will one way or other make you amends.
[Aloud]
I will knog your urinals about your knave's cockscomb
for missing your meetings and appointments.

speeches.speech: Diable! Jack Rugby,--mine host de Jarteer,--have I not stay for him to kill him? have I not, at de place I did appoint?
lines: Diable! Jack Rugby,--mine host de Jarteer,--have I
not stay for him to kill him? have I not, at de place
I did appoint?

speeches.speech: As I am a Christians soul now, look you, this is the place appointed: I'll be judgement by mine host of the Garter.
lines: As I am a Christians soul now, look you, this is the
place appointed: I'll be judgement by mine host of
the Garter.

speeches.speech: Peace, I say, Gallia and Gaul, French and Welsh, soul-curer and body-curer!
lines: Peace, I say, Gallia and Gaul, French and Welsh,
soul-curer and body-curer!

speeches.speech: Ay, dat is very good; excellent.
lines: Ay, dat is very good; excellent.

speeches.speech: Peace, I say! hear mine host of the Garter. Am I politic? am I subtle? am I a Machiavel? Shall I lose my doctor? no; he gives me the potions and the motions. Shall I lose my parson, my priest, my Sir Hugh? no; he gives me the proverbs and the no-verbs. Give me thy hand, terrestrial; so. Give me thy hand, celestial; so. Boys of art, I have deceived you both; I have directed you to wrong places: your hearts are mighty, your skins are whole, and let burnt sack be the issue. Come, lay their swords to pawn. Follow me, lads of peace; follow, follow, follow.
lines: Peace, I say! hear mine host of the Garter. Am I
politic? am I subtle? am I a Machiavel? Shall I
lose my doctor? no; he gives me the potions and the
motions. Shall I lose my parson, my priest, my Sir
Hugh? no; he gives me the proverbs and the
no-verbs. Give me thy hand, terrestrial; so. Give me
thy hand, celestial; so. Boys of art, I have
deceived you both; I have directed you to wrong
places: your hearts are mighty, your skins are
whole, and let burnt sack be the issue. Come, lay
their swords to pawn. Follow me, lads of peace;
follow, follow, follow.

speeches.speech: Trust me, a mad host. Follow, gentlemen, follow.
lines: Trust me, a mad host. Follow, gentlemen, follow.

speeches.speech: [Aside] O sweet Anne Page!
lines: [Aside] O sweet Anne Page!

speeches.speech: Ha, do I perceive dat? have you make-a de sot of us, ha, ha?
lines: Ha, do I perceive dat? have you make-a de sot of
us, ha, ha?

speeches.speech: This is well; he has made us his vlouting-stog. I desire you that we may be friends; and let us knog our prains together to be revenge on this same scall, scurvy cogging companion, the host of the Garter.
lines: This is well; he has made us his vlouting-stog. I
desire you that we may be friends; and let us knog
our prains together to be revenge on this same
scall, scurvy cogging companion, the host of the Garter.

speeches.speech: By gar, with all my heart. He promise to bring me where is Anne Page; by gar, he deceive me too.
lines: By gar, with all my heart. He promise to bring me
where is Anne Page; by gar, he deceive me too.

speeches.speech: Well, I will smite his noddles. Pray you, follow.
lines: Well, I will smite his noddles. Pray you, follow.

speeches.speech: Nay, keep your way, little gallant; you were wont to be a follower, but now you are a leader. Whether had you rather lead mine eyes, or eye your master's heels?
lines: Nay, keep your way, little gallant; you were wont to
be a follower, but now you are a leader. Whether
had you rather lead mine eyes, or eye your master's heels?

speeches.speech: I had rather, forsooth, go before you like a man than follow him like a dwarf.
lines: I had rather, forsooth, go before you like a man
than follow him like a dwarf.

speeches.speech: O, you are a flattering boy: now I see you'll be a courtier.
lines: O, you are a flattering boy: now I see you'll be a courtier.

speeches.speech: Well met, Mistress Page. Whither go you?
lines: Well met, Mistress Page. Whither go you?

speeches.speech: Truly, sir, to see your wife. Is she at home?
lines: Truly, sir, to see your wife. Is she at home?

speeches.speech: Ay; and as idle as she may hang together, for want of company. I think, if your husbands were dead, you two would marry.
lines: Ay; and as idle as she may hang together, for want
of company. I think, if your husbands were dead,
you two would marry.

speeches.speech: Be sure of that,--two other husbands.
lines: Be sure of that,--two other husbands.

speeches.speech: Where had you this pretty weather-cock?
lines: Where had you this pretty weather-cock?

speeches.speech: I cannot tell what the dickens his name is my husband had him of. What do you call your knight's name, sirrah?
lines: I cannot tell what the dickens his name is my
husband had him of. What do you call your knight's
name, sirrah?

speeches.speech: Sir John Falstaff.
lines: Sir John Falstaff.

speeches.speech: Sir John Falstaff!
lines: Sir John Falstaff!

speeches.speech: He, he; I can never hit on's name. There is such a league between my good man and he! Is your wife at home indeed?
lines: He, he; I can never hit on's name. There is such a
league between my good man and he! Is your wife at
home indeed?

speeches.speech: Indeed she is.
lines: Indeed she is.

speeches.speech: By your leave, sir: I am sick till I see her.
lines: By your leave, sir: I am sick till I see her.

speeches.speech: Has Page any brains? hath he any eyes? hath he any thinking? Sure, they sleep; he hath no use of them. Why, this boy will carry a letter twenty mile, as easy as a cannon will shoot point-blank twelve score. He pieces out his wife's inclination; he gives her folly motion and advantage: and now she's going to my wife, and Falstaff's boy with her. A man may hear this shower sing in the wind. And Falstaff's boy with her! Good plots, they are laid; and our revolted wives share damnation together. Well; I will take him, then torture my wife, pluck the borrowed veil of modesty from the so seeming Mistress Page, divulge Page himself for a secure and wilful Actaeon; and to these violent proceedings all my neighbours shall cry aim. [Clock heard] The clock gives me my cue, and my assurance bids me search: there I shall find Falstaff: I shall be rather praised for this than mocked; for it is as positive as the earth is firm that Falstaff is there: I will go. [Enter PAGE, SHALLOW, SLENDER, Host,] SIR HUGH EVANS, DOCTOR CAIUS, and RUGBY]
lines: Has Page any brains? hath he any eyes? hath he any
thinking? Sure, they sleep; he hath no use of them.
Why, this boy will carry a letter twenty mile, as
easy as a cannon will shoot point-blank twelve
score. He pieces out his wife's inclination; he
gives her folly motion and advantage: and now she's
going to my wife, and Falstaff's boy with her. A
man may hear this shower sing in the wind. And
Falstaff's boy with her! Good plots, they are laid;
and our revolted wives share damnation together.
Well; I will take him, then torture my wife, pluck
the borrowed veil of modesty from the so seeming
Mistress Page, divulge Page himself for a secure and
wilful Actaeon; and to these violent proceedings all
my neighbours shall cry aim.
[Clock heard]
The clock gives me my cue, and my assurance bids me
search: there I shall find Falstaff: I shall be
rather praised for this than mocked; for it is as
positive as the earth is firm that Falstaff is
there: I will go.
[Enter PAGE, SHALLOW, SLENDER, Host,]
SIR HUGH EVANS, DOCTOR CAIUS, and RUGBY]

speeches.speech: [with Page and others] Well met, Master Ford.
lines: [with Page and others] Well met, Master Ford.

speeches.speech: Trust me, a good knot: I have good cheer at home; and I pray you all go with me.
lines: Trust me, a good knot: I have good cheer at home;
and I pray you all go with me.

speeches.speech: I must excuse myself, Master Ford.
lines: I must excuse myself, Master Ford.

speeches.speech: And so must I, sir: we have appointed to dine with Mistress Anne, and I would not break with her for more money than I'll speak of.
lines: And so must I, sir: we have appointed to dine with
Mistress Anne, and I would not break with her for
more money than I'll speak of.

speeches.speech: We have lingered about a match between Anne Page and my cousin Slender, and this day we shall have our answer.
lines: We have lingered about a match between Anne Page and
my cousin Slender, and this day we shall have our answer.

speeches.speech: I hope I have your good will, father Page.
lines: I hope I have your good will, father Page.

speeches.speech: You have, Master Slender; I stand wholly for you: but my wife, master doctor, is for you altogether.
lines: You have, Master Slender; I stand wholly for you:
but my wife, master doctor, is for you altogether.

speeches.speech: Ay, be-gar; and de maid is love-a me: my nursh-a Quickly tell me so mush.
lines: Ay, be-gar; and de maid is love-a me: my nursh-a
Quickly tell me so mush.

speeches.speech: What say you to young Master Fenton? he capers, he dances, he has eyes of youth, he writes verses, he speaks holiday, he smells April and May: he will carry't, he will carry't; 'tis in his buttons; he will carry't.
lines: What say you to young Master Fenton? he capers, he
dances, he has eyes of youth, he writes verses, he
speaks holiday, he smells April and May: he will
carry't, he will carry't; 'tis in his buttons; he
will carry't.

speeches.speech: Not by my consent, I promise you. The gentleman is of no having: he kept company with the wild prince and Poins; he is of too high a region; he knows too much. No, he shall not knit a knot in his fortunes with the finger of my substance: if he take her, let him take her simply; the wealth I have waits on my consent, and my consent goes not that way.
lines: Not by my consent, I promise you. The gentleman is
of no having: he kept company with the wild prince
and Poins; he is of too high a region; he knows too
much. No, he shall not knit a knot in his fortunes
with the finger of my substance: if he take her,
let him take her simply; the wealth I have waits on
my consent, and my consent goes not that way.

speeches.speech: I beseech you heartily, some of you go home with me to dinner: besides your cheer, you shall have sport; I will show you a monster. Master doctor, you shall go; so shall you, Master Page; and you, Sir Hugh.
lines: I beseech you heartily, some of you go home with me
to dinner: besides your cheer, you shall have
sport; I will show you a monster. Master doctor,
you shall go; so shall you, Master Page; and you, Sir Hugh.

speeches.speech: Well, fare you well: we shall have the freer wooing at Master Page's.
lines: Well, fare you well: we shall have the freer wooing
at Master Page's.

speeches.speech: Go home, John Rugby; I come anon.
lines: Go home, John Rugby; I come anon.

speeches.speech: Farewell, my hearts: I will to my honest knight Falstaff, and drink canary with him.
lines: Farewell, my hearts: I will to my honest knight
Falstaff, and drink canary with him.

speeches.speech: [Aside] I think I shall drink in pipe wine first with him; I'll make him dance. Will you go, gentles?
lines: [Aside] I think I shall drink in pipe wine first
with him; I'll make him dance. Will you go, gentles?

speeches.speech: Have with you to see this monster.
lines: Have with you to see this monster.

speeches.speech: What, John! What, Robert!
lines: What, John! What, Robert!

speeches.speech: Quickly, quickly! is the buck-basket--
lines: Quickly, quickly! is the buck-basket--

speeches.speech: I warrant. What, Robin, I say!
lines: I warrant. What, Robin, I say!

speeches.speech: Come, come, come.
lines: Come, come, come.

speeches.speech: Here, set it down.
lines: Here, set it down.

speeches.speech: Give your men the charge; we must be brief.
lines: Give your men the charge; we must be brief.

speeches.speech: Marry, as I told you before, John and Robert, be ready here hard by in the brew-house: and when I suddenly call you, come forth, and without any pause or staggering take this basket on your shoulders: that done, trudge with it in all haste, and carry it among the whitsters in Datchet-mead, and there empty it in the muddy ditch close by the Thames side.
lines: Marry, as I told you before, John and Robert, be
ready here hard by in the brew-house: and when I
suddenly call you, come forth, and without any pause
or staggering take this basket on your shoulders:
that done, trudge with it in all haste, and carry
it among the whitsters in Datchet-mead, and there
empty it in the muddy ditch close by the Thames side.

speeches.speech: You will do it?
lines: You will do it?

speeches.speech: I ha' told them over and over; they lack no direction. Be gone, and come when you are called.
lines: I ha' told them over and over; they lack no
direction. Be gone, and come when you are called.

speeches.speech: Here comes little Robin.
lines: Here comes little Robin.

speeches.speech: How now, my eyas-musket! what news with you?
lines: How now, my eyas-musket! what news with you?

speeches.speech: My master, Sir John, is come in at your back-door, Mistress Ford, and requests your company.
lines: My master, Sir John, is come in at your back-door,
Mistress Ford, and requests your company.

speeches.speech: You little Jack-a-Lent, have you been true to us?
lines: You little Jack-a-Lent, have you been true to us?

speeches.speech: Ay, I'll be sworn. My master knows not of your being here and hath threatened to put me into everlasting liberty if I tell you of it; for he swears he'll turn me away.
lines: Ay, I'll be sworn. My master knows not of your
being here and hath threatened to put me into
everlasting liberty if I tell you of it; for he
swears he'll turn me away.

speeches.speech: Thou'rt a good boy: this secrecy of thine shall be a tailor to thee and shall make thee a new doublet and hose. I'll go hide me.
lines: Thou'rt a good boy: this secrecy of thine shall be
a tailor to thee and shall make thee a new doublet
and hose. I'll go hide me.

speeches.speech: Do so. Go tell thy master I am alone. [Exit ROBIN] Mistress Page, remember you your cue.
lines: Do so. Go tell thy master I am alone.
[Exit ROBIN]
Mistress Page, remember you your cue.

speeches.speech: I warrant thee; if I do not act it, hiss me.
lines: I warrant thee; if I do not act it, hiss me.

speeches.speech: Go to, then: we'll use this unwholesome humidity, this gross watery pumpion; we'll teach him to know turtles from jays.
lines: Go to, then: we'll use this unwholesome humidity,
this gross watery pumpion; we'll teach him to know
turtles from jays.

speeches.speech: Have I caught thee, my heavenly jewel? Why, now let me die, for I have lived long enough: this is the period of my ambition: O this blessed hour!
lines: Have I caught thee, my heavenly jewel? Why, now let
me die, for I have lived long enough: this is the
period of my ambition: O this blessed hour!

speeches.speech: O sweet Sir John!
lines: O sweet Sir John!

speeches.speech: Mistress Ford, I cannot cog, I cannot prate, Mistress Ford. Now shall I sin in my wish: I would thy husband were dead: I'll speak it before the best lord; I would make thee my lady.
lines: Mistress Ford, I cannot cog, I cannot prate,
Mistress Ford. Now shall I sin in my wish: I would
thy husband were dead: I'll speak it before the
best lord; I would make thee my lady.

speeches.speech: I your lady, Sir John! alas, I should be a pitiful lady!
lines: I your lady, Sir John! alas, I should be a pitiful lady!

speeches.speech: Let the court of France show me such another. I see how thine eye would emulate the diamond: thou hast the right arched beauty of the brow that becomes the ship-tire, the tire-valiant, or any tire of Venetian admittance.
lines: Let the court of France show me such another. I see
how thine eye would emulate the diamond: thou hast
the right arched beauty of the brow that becomes the
ship-tire, the tire-valiant, or any tire of
Venetian admittance.

speeches.speech: A plain kerchief, Sir John: my brows become nothing else; nor that well neither.
lines: A plain kerchief, Sir John: my brows become nothing
else; nor that well neither.

speeches.speech: By the Lord, thou art a traitor to say so: thou wouldst make an absolute courtier; and the firm fixture of thy foot would give an excellent motion to thy gait in a semi-circled farthingale. I see what thou wert, if Fortune thy foe were not, Nature thy friend. Come, thou canst not hide it.
lines: By the Lord, thou art a traitor to say so: thou
wouldst make an absolute courtier; and the firm
fixture of thy foot would give an excellent motion
to thy gait in a semi-circled farthingale. I see
what thou wert, if Fortune thy foe were not, Nature
thy friend. Come, thou canst not hide it.

speeches.speech: Believe me, there is no such thing in me.
lines: Believe me, there is no such thing in me.

speeches.speech: What made me love thee? let that persuade thee there's something extraordinary in thee. Come, I cannot cog and say thou art this and that, like a many of these lisping hawthorn-buds, that come like women in men's apparel, and smell like Bucklersbury in simple time; I cannot: but I love thee; none but thee; and thou deservest it.
lines: What made me love thee? let that persuade thee
there's something extraordinary in thee. Come, I
cannot cog and say thou art this and that, like a
many of these lisping hawthorn-buds, that come like
women in men's apparel, and smell like Bucklersbury
in simple time; I cannot: but I love thee; none
but thee; and thou deservest it.

speeches.speech: Do not betray me, sir. I fear you love Mistress Page.
lines: Do not betray me, sir. I fear you love Mistress Page.

speeches.speech: Thou mightst as well say I love to walk by the Counter-gate, which is as hateful to me as the reek of a lime-kiln.
lines: Thou mightst as well say I love to walk by the
Counter-gate, which is as hateful to me as the reek
of a lime-kiln.

speeches.speech: Well, heaven knows how I love you; and you shall one day find it.
lines: Well, heaven knows how I love you; and you shall one
day find it.

speeches.speech: Keep in that mind; I'll deserve it.
lines: Keep in that mind; I'll deserve it.

speeches.speech: Nay, I must tell you, so you do; or else I could not be in that mind.
lines: Nay, I must tell you, so you do; or else I could not
be in that mind.

speeches.speech: [Within] Mistress Ford, Mistress Ford! here's Mistress Page at the door, sweating and blowing and looking wildly, and would needs speak with you presently.
lines: [Within] Mistress Ford, Mistress Ford! here's
Mistress Page at the door, sweating and blowing and
looking wildly, and would needs speak with you presently.

speeches.speech: She shall not see me: I will ensconce me behind the arras.
lines: She shall not see me: I will ensconce me behind the arras.

speeches.speech: Pray you, do so: she's a very tattling woman. [FALSTAFF hides himself] [Re-enter MISTRESS PAGE and ROBIN] What's the matter? how now!
lines: Pray you, do so: she's a very tattling woman.
[FALSTAFF hides himself]
[Re-enter MISTRESS PAGE and ROBIN]
What's the matter? how now!

speeches.speech: O Mistress Ford, what have you done? You're shamed, you're overthrown, you're undone for ever!
lines: O Mistress Ford, what have you done? You're shamed,
you're overthrown, you're undone for ever!

speeches.speech: What's the matter, good Mistress Page?
lines: What's the matter, good Mistress Page?

speeches.speech: O well-a-day, Mistress Ford! having an honest man to your husband, to give him such cause of suspicion!
lines: O well-a-day, Mistress Ford! having an honest man
to your husband, to give him such cause of suspicion!

speeches.speech: What cause of suspicion?
lines: What cause of suspicion?

speeches.speech: What cause of suspicion! Out pon you! how am I mistook in you!
lines: What cause of suspicion! Out pon you! how am I
mistook in you!

speeches.speech: Why, alas, what's the matter?
lines: Why, alas, what's the matter?

speeches.speech: Your husband's coming hither, woman, with all the officers in Windsor, to search for a gentleman that he says is here now in the house by your consent, to take an ill advantage of his assence: you are undone.
lines: Your husband's coming hither, woman, with all the
officers in Windsor, to search for a gentleman that
he says is here now in the house by your consent, to
take an ill advantage of his assence: you are undone.

speeches.speech: 'Tis not so, I hope.
lines: 'Tis not so, I hope.

speeches.speech: Pray heaven it be not so, that you have such a man here! but 'tis most certain your husband's coming, with half Windsor at his heels, to search for such a one. I come before to tell you. If you know yourself clear, why, I am glad of it; but if you have a friend here convey, convey him out. Be not amazed; call all your senses to you; defend your reputation, or bid farewell to your good life for ever.
lines: Pray heaven it be not so, that you have such a man
here! but 'tis most certain your husband's coming,
with half Windsor at his heels, to search for such a
one. I come before to tell you. If you know
yourself clear, why, I am glad of it; but if you
have a friend here convey, convey him out. Be not
amazed; call all your senses to you; defend your
reputation, or bid farewell to your good life for ever.

speeches.speech: What shall I do? There is a gentleman my dear friend; and I fear not mine own shame so much as his peril: I had rather than a thousand pound he were out of the house.
lines: What shall I do? There is a gentleman my dear
friend; and I fear not mine own shame so much as his
peril: I had rather than a thousand pound he were
out of the house.

speeches.speech: For shame! never stand 'you had rather' and 'you had rather:' your husband's here at hand, bethink you of some conveyance: in the house you cannot hide him. O, how have you deceived me! Look, here is a basket: if he be of any reasonable stature, he may creep in here; and throw foul linen upon him, as if it were going to bucking: or--it is whiting-time --send him by your two men to Datchet-mead.
lines: For shame! never stand 'you had rather' and 'you
had rather:' your husband's here at hand, bethink
you of some conveyance: in the house you cannot
hide him. O, how have you deceived me! Look, here
is a basket: if he be of any reasonable stature, he
may creep in here; and throw foul linen upon him, as
if it were going to bucking: or--it is whiting-time
--send him by your two men to Datchet-mead.

speeches.speech: He's too big to go in there. What shall I do?
lines: He's too big to go in there. What shall I do?

speeches.speech: [Coming forward] Let me see't, let me see't, O, let me see't! I'll in, I'll in. Follow your friend's counsel. I'll in.
lines: [Coming forward] Let me see't, let me see't, O, let
me see't! I'll in, I'll in. Follow your friend's
counsel. I'll in.

speeches.speech: What, Sir John Falstaff! Are these your letters, knight?
lines: What, Sir John Falstaff! Are these your letters, knight?

speeches.speech: I love thee. Help me away. Let me creep in here. I'll never--
lines: I love thee. Help me away. Let me creep in here.
I'll never--

speeches.speech: Help to cover your master, boy. Call your men, Mistress Ford. You dissembling knight!
lines: Help to cover your master, boy. Call your men,
Mistress Ford. You dissembling knight!

speeches.speech: What, John! Robert! John! [Exit ROBIN] [Re-enter Servants] Go take up these clothes here quickly. Where's the cowl-staff? look, how you drumble! Carry them to the laundress in Datchet-meat; quickly, come.
lines: What, John! Robert! John!
[Exit ROBIN]
[Re-enter Servants]
Go take up these clothes here quickly. Where's the
cowl-staff? look, how you drumble! Carry them to
the laundress in Datchet-meat; quickly, come.

speeches.speech: Pray you, come near: if I suspect without cause, why then make sport at me; then let me be your jest; I deserve it. How now! whither bear you this?
lines: Pray you, come near: if I suspect without cause,
why then make sport at me; then let me be your jest;
I deserve it. How now! whither bear you this?

speeches.speech: To the laundress, forsooth.
lines: To the laundress, forsooth.

speeches.speech: Why, what have you to do whither they bear it? You were best meddle with buck-washing.
lines: Why, what have you to do whither they bear it? You
were best meddle with buck-washing.

speeches.speech: Buck! I would I could wash myself of the buck! Buck, buck, buck! Ay, buck; I warrant you, buck; and of the season too, it shall appear. [Exeunt Servants with the basket] Gentlemen, I have dreamed to-night; I'll tell you my dream. Here, here, here be my keys: ascend my chambers; search, seek, find out: I'll warrant we'll unkennel the fox. Let me stop this way first. [Locking the door] So, now uncape.
lines: Buck! I would I could wash myself of the buck!
Buck, buck, buck! Ay, buck; I warrant you, buck;
and of the season too, it shall appear.
[Exeunt Servants with the basket]
Gentlemen, I have dreamed to-night; I'll tell you my
dream. Here, here, here be my keys: ascend my
chambers; search, seek, find out: I'll warrant
we'll unkennel the fox. Let me stop this way first.
[Locking the door]
So, now uncape.

speeches.speech: Good Master Ford, be contented: you wrong yourself too much.
lines: Good Master Ford, be contented: you wrong yourself too much.

speeches.speech: True, Master Page. Up, gentlemen: you shall see sport anon: follow me, gentlemen.
lines: True, Master Page. Up, gentlemen: you shall see
sport anon: follow me, gentlemen.

speeches.speech: This is fery fantastical humours and jealousies.
lines: This is fery fantastical humours and jealousies.

speeches.speech: By gar, 'tis no the fashion of France; it is not jealous in France.
lines: By gar, 'tis no the fashion of France; it is not
jealous in France.

speeches.speech: Nay, follow him, gentlemen; see the issue of his search.
lines: Nay, follow him, gentlemen; see the issue of his search.

speeches.speech: Is there not a double excellency in this?
lines: Is there not a double excellency in this?

speeches.speech: I know not which pleases me better, that my husband is deceived, or Sir John.
lines: I know not which pleases me better, that my husband
is deceived, or Sir John.

speeches.speech: What a taking was he in when your husband asked who was in the basket!
lines: What a taking was he in when your husband asked who
was in the basket!

speeches.speech: I am half afraid he will have need of washing; so throwing him into the water will do him a benefit.
lines: I am half afraid he will have need of washing; so
throwing him into the water will do him a benefit.

speeches.speech: Hang him, dishonest rascal! I would all of the same strain were in the same distress.
lines: Hang him, dishonest rascal! I would all of the same
strain were in the same distress.

speeches.speech: I think my husband hath some special suspicion of Falstaff's being here; for I never saw him so gross in his jealousy till now.
lines: I think my husband hath some special suspicion of
Falstaff's being here; for I never saw him so gross
in his jealousy till now.

speeches.speech: I will lay a plot to try that; and we will yet have more tricks with Falstaff: his dissolute disease will scarce obey this medicine.
lines: I will lay a plot to try that; and we will yet have
more tricks with Falstaff: his dissolute disease will
scarce obey this medicine.

speeches.speech: Shall we send that foolish carrion, Mistress Quickly, to him, and excuse his throwing into the water; and give him another hope, to betray him to another punishment?
lines: Shall we send that foolish carrion, Mistress
Quickly, to him, and excuse his throwing into the
water; and give him another hope, to betray him to
another punishment?

speeches.speech: We will do it: let him be sent for to-morrow, eight o'clock, to have amends.
lines: We will do it: let him be sent for to-morrow,
eight o'clock, to have amends.

speeches.speech: I cannot find him: may be the knave bragged of that he could not compass.
lines: I cannot find him: may be the knave bragged of that
he could not compass.

speeches.speech: [Aside to MISTRESS FORD] Heard you that?
lines: [Aside to MISTRESS FORD] Heard you that?

speeches.speech: You use me well, Master Ford, do you?
lines: You use me well, Master Ford, do you?

speeches.speech: Ay, I do so.
lines: Ay, I do so.

speeches.speech: Heaven make you better than your thoughts!
lines: Heaven make you better than your thoughts!

speeches.speech: Amen!
lines: Amen!

speeches.speech: You do yourself mighty wrong, Master Ford.
lines: You do yourself mighty wrong, Master Ford.

speeches.speech: Ay, ay; I must bear it.
lines: Ay, ay; I must bear it.

speeches.speech: If there be any pody in the house, and in the chambers, and in the coffers, and in the presses, heaven forgive my sins at the day of judgment!
lines: If there be any pody in the house, and in the
chambers, and in the coffers, and in the presses,
heaven forgive my sins at the day of judgment!

speeches.speech: By gar, nor I too: there is no bodies.
lines: By gar, nor I too: there is no bodies.

speeches.speech: Fie, fie, Master Ford! are you not ashamed? What spirit, what devil suggests this imagination? I would not ha' your distemper in this kind for the wealth of Windsor Castle.
lines: Fie, fie, Master Ford! are you not ashamed? What
spirit, what devil suggests this imagination? I
would not ha' your distemper in this kind for the
wealth of Windsor Castle.

speeches.speech: 'Tis my fault, Master Page: I suffer for it.
lines: 'Tis my fault, Master Page: I suffer for it.

speeches.speech: You suffer for a pad conscience: your wife is as honest a 'omans as I will desires among five thousand, and five hundred too.
lines: You suffer for a pad conscience: your wife is as
honest a 'omans as I will desires among five
thousand, and five hundred too.

speeches.speech: By gar, I see 'tis an honest woman.
lines: By gar, I see 'tis an honest woman.

speeches.speech: Well, I promised you a dinner. Come, come, walk in the Park: I pray you, pardon me; I will hereafter make known to you why I have done this. Come, wife; come, Mistress Page. I pray you, pardon me; pray heartily, pardon me.
lines: Well, I promised you a dinner. Come, come, walk in
the Park: I pray you, pardon me; I will hereafter
make known to you why I have done this. Come,
wife; come, Mistress Page. I pray you, pardon me;
pray heartily, pardon me.

speeches.speech: Let's go in, gentlemen; but, trust me, we'll mock him. I do invite you to-morrow morning to my house to breakfast: after, we'll a-birding together; I have a fine hawk for the bush. Shall it be so?
lines: Let's go in, gentlemen; but, trust me, we'll mock
him. I do invite you to-morrow morning to my house
to breakfast: after, we'll a-birding together; I
have a fine hawk for the bush. Shall it be so?

speeches.speech: Any thing.
lines: Any thing.

speeches.speech: If there is one, I shall make two in the company.
lines: If there is one, I shall make two in the company.

speeches.speech: If dere be one or two, I shall make-a the turd.
lines: If dere be one or two, I shall make-a the turd.

speeches.speech: Pray you, go, Master Page.
lines: Pray you, go, Master Page.

speeches.speech: I pray you now, remembrance tomorrow on the lousy knave, mine host.
lines: I pray you now, remembrance tomorrow on the lousy
knave, mine host.

speeches.speech: Dat is good; by gar, with all my heart!
lines: Dat is good; by gar, with all my heart!

speeches.speech: A lousy knave, to have his gibes and his mockeries!
lines: A lousy knave, to have his gibes and his mockeries!

speeches.speech: I see I cannot get thy father's love; Therefore no more turn me to him, sweet Nan.
lines: I see I cannot get thy father's love;
Therefore no more turn me to him, sweet Nan.

speeches.speech: Alas, how then?
lines: Alas, how then?

speeches.speech: Why, thou must be thyself. He doth object I am too great of birth--, And that, my state being gall'd with my expense, I seek to heal it only by his wealth: Besides these, other bars he lays before me, My riots past, my wild societies; And tells me 'tis a thing impossible I should love thee but as a property.
lines: Why, thou must be thyself.
He doth object I am too great of birth--,
And that, my state being gall'd with my expense,
I seek to heal it only by his wealth:
Besides these, other bars he lays before me,
My riots past, my wild societies;
And tells me 'tis a thing impossible
I should love thee but as a property.

speeches.speech: May be he tells you true.
lines: May be he tells you true.

speeches.speech: No, heaven so speed me in my time to come! Albeit I will confess thy father's wealth Was the first motive that I woo'd thee, Anne: Yet, wooing thee, I found thee of more value Than stamps in gold or sums in sealed bags; And 'tis the very riches of thyself That now I aim at.
lines: No, heaven so speed me in my time to come!
Albeit I will confess thy father's wealth
Was the first motive that I woo'd thee, Anne:
Yet, wooing thee, I found thee of more value
Than stamps in gold or sums in sealed bags;
And 'tis the very riches of thyself
That now I aim at.

speeches.speech: Gentle Master Fenton, Yet seek my father's love; still seek it, sir: If opportunity and humblest suit Cannot attain it, why, then,--hark you hither!
lines: Gentle Master Fenton,
Yet seek my father's love; still seek it, sir:
If opportunity and humblest suit
Cannot attain it, why, then,--hark you hither!

speeches.speech: Break their talk, Mistress Quickly: my kinsman shall speak for himself.
lines: Break their talk, Mistress Quickly: my kinsman shall
speak for himself.

speeches.speech: I'll make a shaft or a bolt on't: 'slid, 'tis but venturing.
lines: I'll make a shaft or a bolt on't: 'slid, 'tis but
venturing.

speeches.speech: Be not dismayed.
lines: Be not dismayed.

speeches.speech: No, she shall not dismay me: I care not for that, but that I am afeard.
lines: No, she shall not dismay me: I care not for that,
but that I am afeard.

speeches.speech: Hark ye; Master Slender would speak a word with you.
lines: Hark ye; Master Slender would speak a word with you.

speeches.speech: I come to him. [Aside] This is my father's choice. O, what a world of vile ill-favor'd faults Looks handsome in three hundred pounds a-year!
lines: I come to him.
[Aside]
This is my father's choice.
O, what a world of vile ill-favor'd faults
Looks handsome in three hundred pounds a-year!

speeches.speech: And how does good Master Fenton? Pray you, a word with you.
lines: And how does good Master Fenton? Pray you, a word with you.

speeches.speech: She's coming; to her, coz. O boy, thou hadst a father!
lines: She's coming; to her, coz. O boy, thou hadst a father!

speeches.speech: I had a father, Mistress Anne; my uncle can tell you good jests of him. Pray you, uncle, tell Mistress Anne the jest, how my father stole two geese out of a pen, good uncle.
lines: I had a father, Mistress Anne; my uncle can tell you
good jests of him. Pray you, uncle, tell Mistress
Anne the jest, how my father stole two geese out of
a pen, good uncle.

speeches.speech: Mistress Anne, my cousin loves you.
lines: Mistress Anne, my cousin loves you.

speeches.speech: Ay, that I do; as well as I love any woman in Gloucestershire.
lines: Ay, that I do; as well as I love any woman in
Gloucestershire.

speeches.speech: He will maintain you like a gentlewoman.
lines: He will maintain you like a gentlewoman.

speeches.speech: Ay, that I will, come cut and long-tail, under the degree of a squire.
lines: Ay, that I will, come cut and long-tail, under the
degree of a squire.

speeches.speech: He will make you a hundred and fifty pounds jointure.
lines: He will make you a hundred and fifty pounds jointure.

speeches.speech: Good Master Shallow, let him woo for himself.
lines: Good Master Shallow, let him woo for himself.

speeches.speech: Marry, I thank you for it; I thank you for that good comfort. She calls you, coz: I'll leave you.
lines: Marry, I thank you for it; I thank you for that good
comfort. She calls you, coz: I'll leave you.

speeches.speech: Now, Master Slender,--
lines: Now, Master Slender,--

speeches.speech: Now, good Mistress Anne,--
lines: Now, good Mistress Anne,--

speeches.speech: What is your will?
lines: What is your will?

speeches.speech: My will! 'od's heartlings, that's a pretty jest indeed! I ne'er made my will yet, I thank heaven; I am not such a sickly creature, I give heaven praise.
lines: My will! 'od's heartlings, that's a pretty jest
indeed! I ne'er made my will yet, I thank heaven; I
am not such a sickly creature, I give heaven praise.

speeches.speech: I mean, Master Slender, what would you with me?
lines: I mean, Master Slender, what would you with me?

speeches.speech: Truly, for mine own part, I would little or nothing with you. Your father and my uncle hath made motions: if it be my luck, so; if not, happy man be his dole! They can tell you how things go better than I can: you may ask your father; here he comes.
lines: Truly, for mine own part, I would little or nothing
with you. Your father and my uncle hath made
motions: if it be my luck, so; if not, happy man be
his dole! They can tell you how things go better
than I can: you may ask your father; here he comes.

speeches.speech: Now, Master Slender: love him, daughter Anne. Why, how now! what does Master Fenton here? You wrong me, sir, thus still to haunt my house: I told you, sir, my daughter is disposed of.
lines: Now, Master Slender: love him, daughter Anne.
Why, how now! what does Master Fenton here?
You wrong me, sir, thus still to haunt my house:
I told you, sir, my daughter is disposed of.

speeches.speech: Nay, Master Page, be not impatient.
lines: Nay, Master Page, be not impatient.

speeches.speech: Good Master Fenton, come not to my child.
lines: Good Master Fenton, come not to my child.

speeches.speech: She is no match for you.
lines: She is no match for you.

speeches.speech: Sir, will you hear me?
lines: Sir, will you hear me?

speeches.speech: No, good Master Fenton. Come, Master Shallow; come, son Slender, in. Knowing my mind, you wrong me, Master Fenton.
lines: No, good Master Fenton.
Come, Master Shallow; come, son Slender, in.
Knowing my mind, you wrong me, Master Fenton.

speeches.speech: Speak to Mistress Page.
lines: Speak to Mistress Page.

speeches.speech: Good Mistress Page, for that I love your daughter In such a righteous fashion as I do, Perforce, against all cheques, rebukes and manners, I must advance the colours of my love And not retire: let me have your good will.
lines: Good Mistress Page, for that I love your daughter
In such a righteous fashion as I do,
Perforce, against all cheques, rebukes and manners,
I must advance the colours of my love
And not retire: let me have your good will.

speeches.speech: Good mother, do not marry me to yond fool.
lines: Good mother, do not marry me to yond fool.

speeches.speech: I mean it not; I seek you a better husband.
lines: I mean it not; I seek you a better husband.

speeches.speech: That's my master, master doctor.
lines: That's my master, master doctor.

speeches.speech: Alas, I had rather be set quick i' the earth And bowl'd to death with turnips!
lines: Alas, I had rather be set quick i' the earth
And bowl'd to death with turnips!

speeches.speech: Come, trouble not yourself. Good Master Fenton, I will not be your friend nor enemy: My daughter will I question how she loves you, And as I find her, so am I affected. Till then farewell, sir: she must needs go in; Her father will be angry.
lines: Come, trouble not yourself. Good Master Fenton,
I will not be your friend nor enemy:
My daughter will I question how she loves you,
And as I find her, so am I affected.
Till then farewell, sir: she must needs go in;
Her father will be angry.

speeches.speech: Farewell, gentle mistress: farewell, Nan.
lines: Farewell, gentle mistress: farewell, Nan.

speeches.speech: This is my doing, now: 'Nay,' said I, 'will you cast away your child on a fool, and a physician? Look on Master Fenton:' this is my doing.
lines: This is my doing, now: 'Nay,' said I, 'will you cast
away your child on a fool, and a physician? Look on
Master Fenton:' this is my doing.

speeches.speech: I thank thee; and I pray thee, once to-night Give my sweet Nan this ring: there's for thy pains.
lines: I thank thee; and I pray thee, once to-night
Give my sweet Nan this ring: there's for thy pains.

speeches.speech: Now heaven send thee good fortune! [Exit FENTON] A kind heart he hath: a woman would run through fire and water for such a kind heart. But yet I would my master had Mistress Anne; or I would Master Slender had her; or, in sooth, I would Master Fenton had her; I will do what I can for them all three; for so I have promised, and I'll be as good as my word; but speciously for Master Fenton. Well, I must of another errand to Sir John Falstaff from my two mistresses: what a beast am I to slack it!
lines: Now heaven send thee good fortune!
[Exit FENTON]
A kind heart he hath: a woman would run through
fire and water for such a kind heart. But yet I
would my master had Mistress Anne; or I would
Master Slender had her; or, in sooth, I would Master
Fenton had her; I will do what I can for them all
three; for so I have promised, and I'll be as good
as my word; but speciously for Master Fenton. Well,
I must of another errand to Sir John Falstaff from
my two mistresses: what a beast am I to slack it!

speeches.speech: Bardolph, I say,--
lines: Bardolph, I say,--

speeches.speech: Here, sir.
lines: Here, sir.

speeches.speech: Go fetch me a quart of sack; put a toast in't. [Exit BARDOLPH] Have I lived to be carried in a basket, like a barrow of butcher's offal, and to be thrown in the Thames? Well, if I be served such another trick, I'll have my brains ta'en out and buttered, and give them to a dog for a new-year's gift. The rogues slighted me into the river with as little remorse as they would have drowned a blind bitch's puppies, fifteen i' the litter: and you may know by my size that I have a kind of alacrity in sinking; if the bottom were as deep as hell, I should down. I had been drowned, but that the shore was shelvy and shallow,--a death that I abhor; for the water swells a man; and what a thing should I have been when I had been swelled! I should have been a mountain of mummy.
lines: Go fetch me a quart of sack; put a toast in't.
[Exit BARDOLPH]
Have I lived to be carried in a basket, like a
barrow of butcher's offal, and to be thrown in the
Thames? Well, if I be served such another trick,
I'll have my brains ta'en out and buttered, and give
them to a dog for a new-year's gift. The rogues
slighted me into the river with as little remorse as
they would have drowned a blind bitch's puppies,
fifteen i' the litter: and you may know by my size
that I have a kind of alacrity in sinking; if the
bottom were as deep as hell, I should down. I had
been drowned, but that the shore was shelvy and
shallow,--a death that I abhor; for the water swells
a man; and what a thing should I have been when I
had been swelled! I should have been a mountain of mummy.

speeches.speech: Here's Mistress Quickly, sir, to speak with you.
lines: Here's Mistress Quickly, sir, to speak with you.

speeches.speech: Let me pour in some sack to the Thames water; for my belly's as cold as if I had swallowed snowballs for pills to cool the reins. Call her in.
lines: Let me pour in some sack to the Thames water; for my
belly's as cold as if I had swallowed snowballs for
pills to cool the reins. Call her in.

speeches.speech: Come in, woman!
lines: Come in, woman!

speeches.speech: By your leave; I cry you mercy: give your worship good morrow.
lines: By your leave; I cry you mercy: give your worship
good morrow.

speeches.speech: Take away these chalices. Go brew me a pottle of sack finely.
lines: Take away these chalices. Go brew me a pottle of
sack finely.

speeches.speech: With eggs, sir?
lines: With eggs, sir?

speeches.speech: Simple of itself; I'll no pullet-sperm in my brewage. [Exit BARDOLPH] How now!
lines: Simple of itself; I'll no pullet-sperm in my brewage.
[Exit BARDOLPH]
How now!

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, I come to your worship from Mistress Ford.
lines: Marry, sir, I come to your worship from Mistress Ford.

speeches.speech: Mistress Ford! I have had ford enough; I was thrown into the ford; I have my belly full of ford.
lines: Mistress Ford! I have had ford enough; I was thrown
into the ford; I have my belly full of ford.

speeches.speech: Alas the day! good heart, that was not her fault: she does so take on with her men; they mistook their erection.
lines: Alas the day! good heart, that was not her fault:
she does so take on with her men; they mistook their erection.

speeches.speech: So did I mine, to build upon a foolish woman's promise.
lines: So did I mine, to build upon a foolish woman's promise.
