speeches.speech: Well, she laments, sir, for it, that it would yearn your heart to see it. Her husband goes this morning a-birding; she desires you once more to come to her between eight and nine: I must carry her word quickly: she'll make you amends, I warrant you.
lines: Well, she laments, sir, for it, that it would yearn
your heart to see it. Her husband goes this morning
a-birding; she desires you once more to come to her
between eight and nine: I must carry her word
quickly: she'll make you amends, I warrant you.

speeches.speech: Well, I will visit her: tell her so; and bid her think what a man is: let her consider his frailty, and then judge of my merit.
lines: Well, I will visit her: tell her so; and bid her
think what a man is: let her consider his frailty,
and then judge of my merit.

speeches.speech: I will tell her.
lines: I will tell her.

speeches.speech: Do so. Between nine and ten, sayest thou?
lines: Do so. Between nine and ten, sayest thou?

speeches.speech: Eight and nine, sir.
lines: Eight and nine, sir.

speeches.speech: Well, be gone: I will not miss her.
lines: Well, be gone: I will not miss her.

speeches.speech: Peace be with you, sir.
lines: Peace be with you, sir.

speeches.speech: I marvel I hear not of Master Brook; he sent me word to stay within: I like his money well. O, here he comes.
lines: I marvel I hear not of Master Brook; he sent me word
to stay within: I like his money well. O, here he comes.

speeches.speech: Bless you, sir!
lines: Bless you, sir!

speeches.speech: Now, master Brook, you come to know what hath passed between me and Ford's wife?
lines: Now, master Brook, you come to know what hath passed
between me and Ford's wife?

speeches.speech: That, indeed, Sir John, is my business.
lines: That, indeed, Sir John, is my business.

speeches.speech: Master Brook, I will not lie to you: I was at her house the hour she appointed me.
lines: Master Brook, I will not lie to you: I was at her
house the hour she appointed me.

speeches.speech: And sped you, sir?
lines: And sped you, sir?

speeches.speech: Very ill-favoredly, Master Brook.
lines: Very ill-favoredly, Master Brook.

speeches.speech: How so, sir? Did she change her determination?
lines: How so, sir? Did she change her determination?

speeches.speech: No, Master Brook; but the peaking Cornuto her husband, Master Brook, dwelling in a continual 'larum of jealousy, comes me in the instant of our encounter, after we had embraced, kissed, protested, and, as it were, spoke the prologue of our comedy; and at his heels a rabble of his companions, thither provoked and instigated by his distemper, and, forsooth, to search his house for his wife's love.
lines: No, Master Brook; but the peaking Cornuto her
husband, Master Brook, dwelling in a continual
'larum of jealousy, comes me in the instant of our
encounter, after we had embraced, kissed, protested,
and, as it were, spoke the prologue of our comedy;
and at his heels a rabble of his companions, thither
provoked and instigated by his distemper, and,
forsooth, to search his house for his wife's love.

speeches.speech: What, while you were there?
lines: What, while you were there?

speeches.speech: While I was there.
lines: While I was there.

speeches.speech: And did he search for you, and could not find you?
lines: And did he search for you, and could not find you?

speeches.speech: You shall hear. As good luck would have it, comes in one Mistress Page; gives intelligence of Ford's approach; and, in her invention and Ford's wife's distraction, they conveyed me into a buck-basket.
lines: You shall hear. As good luck would have it, comes
in one Mistress Page; gives intelligence of Ford's
approach; and, in her invention and Ford's wife's
distraction, they conveyed me into a buck-basket.

speeches.speech: A buck-basket!
lines: A buck-basket!

speeches.speech: By the Lord, a buck-basket! rammed me in with foul shirts and smocks, socks, foul stockings, greasy napkins; that, Master Brook, there was the rankest compound of villanous smell that ever offended nostril.
lines: By the Lord, a buck-basket! rammed me in with foul
shirts and smocks, socks, foul stockings, greasy
napkins; that, Master Brook, there was the rankest
compound of villanous smell that ever offended nostril.

speeches.speech: And how long lay you there?
lines: And how long lay you there?

speeches.speech: Nay, you shall hear, Master Brook, what I have suffered to bring this woman to evil for your good. Being thus crammed in the basket, a couple of Ford's knaves, his hinds, were called forth by their mistress to carry me in the name of foul clothes to Datchet-lane: they took me on their shoulders; met the jealous knave their master in the door, who asked them once or twice what they had in their basket: I quaked for fear, lest the lunatic knave would have searched it; but fate, ordaining he should be a cuckold, held his hand. Well: on went he for a search, and away went I for foul clothes. But mark the sequel, Master Brook: I suffered the pangs of three several deaths; first, an intolerable fright, to be detected with a jealous rotten bell-wether; next, to be compassed, like a good bilbo, in the circumference of a peck, hilt to point, heel to head; and then, to be stopped in, like a strong distillation, with stinking clothes that fretted in their own grease: think of that,--a man of my kidney,--think of that,--that am as subject to heat as butter; a man of continual dissolution and thaw: it was a miracle to scape suffocation. And in the height of this bath, when I was more than half stewed in grease, like a Dutch dish, to be thrown into the Thames, and cooled, glowing hot, in that surge, like a horse-shoe; think of that,--hissing hot,--think of that, Master Brook.
lines: Nay, you shall hear, Master Brook, what I have
suffered to bring this woman to evil for your good.
Being thus crammed in the basket, a couple of Ford's
knaves, his hinds, were called forth by their
mistress to carry me in the name of foul clothes to
Datchet-lane: they took me on their shoulders; met
the jealous knave their master in the door, who
asked them once or twice what they had in their
basket: I quaked for fear, lest the lunatic knave
would have searched it; but fate, ordaining he
should be a cuckold, held his hand. Well: on went he
for a search, and away went I for foul clothes. But
mark the sequel, Master Brook: I suffered the pangs
of three several deaths; first, an intolerable
fright, to be detected with a jealous rotten
bell-wether; next, to be compassed, like a good
bilbo, in the circumference of a peck, hilt to
point, heel to head; and then, to be stopped in,
like a strong distillation, with stinking clothes
that fretted in their own grease: think of that,--a
man of my kidney,--think of that,--that am as subject
to heat as butter; a man of continual dissolution
and thaw: it was a miracle to scape suffocation.
And in the height of this bath, when I was more than
half stewed in grease, like a Dutch dish, to be
thrown into the Thames, and cooled, glowing hot,
in that surge, like a horse-shoe; think of
that,--hissing hot,--think of that, Master Brook.

speeches.speech: In good sadness, I am sorry that for my sake you have sufferd all this. My suit then is desperate; you'll undertake her no more?
lines: In good sadness, I am sorry that for my sake you
have sufferd all this. My suit then is desperate;
you'll undertake her no more?

speeches.speech: Master Brook, I will be thrown into Etna, as I have been into Thames, ere I will leave her thus. Her husband is this morning gone a-birding: I have received from her another embassy of meeting; 'twixt eight and nine is the hour, Master Brook.
lines: Master Brook, I will be thrown into Etna, as I have
been into Thames, ere I will leave her thus. Her
husband is this morning gone a-birding: I have
received from her another embassy of meeting; 'twixt
eight and nine is the hour, Master Brook.

speeches.speech: 'Tis past eight already, sir.
lines: 'Tis past eight already, sir.

speeches.speech: Is it? I will then address me to my appointment. Come to me at your convenient leisure, and you shall know how I speed; and the conclusion shall be crowned with your enjoying her. Adieu. You shall have her, Master Brook; Master Brook, you shall cuckold Ford.
lines: Is it? I will then address me to my appointment.
Come to me at your convenient leisure, and you shall
know how I speed; and the conclusion shall be
crowned with your enjoying her. Adieu. You shall
have her, Master Brook; Master Brook, you shall
cuckold Ford.

speeches.speech: Hum! ha! is this a vision? is this a dream? do I sleep? Master Ford awake! awake, Master Ford! there's a hole made in your best coat, Master Ford. This 'tis to be married! this 'tis to have linen and buck-baskets! Well, I will proclaim myself what I am: I will now take the lecher; he is at my house; he cannot 'scape me; 'tis impossible he should; he cannot creep into a halfpenny purse, nor into a pepper-box: but, lest the devil that guides him should aid him, I will search impossible places. Though what I am I cannot avoid, yet to be what I would not shall not make me tame: if I have horns to make one mad, let the proverb go with me: I'll be horn-mad.
lines: Hum! ha! is this a vision? is this a dream? do I
sleep? Master Ford awake! awake, Master Ford!
there's a hole made in your best coat, Master Ford.
This 'tis to be married! this 'tis to have linen
and buck-baskets! Well, I will proclaim myself
what I am: I will now take the lecher; he is at my
house; he cannot 'scape me; 'tis impossible he
should; he cannot creep into a halfpenny purse,
nor into a pepper-box: but, lest the devil that
guides him should aid him, I will search
impossible places. Though what I am I cannot avoid,
yet to be what I would not shall not make me tame:
if I have horns to make one mad, let the proverb go
with me: I'll be horn-mad.

speeches.speech: Is he at Master Ford's already, think'st thou?
lines: Is he at Master Ford's already, think'st thou?

speeches.speech: Sure he is by this, or will be presently: but, truly, he is very courageous mad about his throwing into the water. Mistress Ford desires you to come suddenly.
lines: Sure he is by this, or will be presently: but,
truly, he is very courageous mad about his throwing
into the water. Mistress Ford desires you to come suddenly.

speeches.speech: I'll be with her by and by; I'll but bring my young man here to school. Look, where his master comes; 'tis a playing-day, I see. [Enter SIR HUGH EVANS] How now, Sir Hugh! no school to-day?
lines: I'll be with her by and by; I'll but bring my young
man here to school. Look, where his master comes;
'tis a playing-day, I see.
[Enter SIR HUGH EVANS]
How now, Sir Hugh! no school to-day?

speeches.speech: No; Master Slender is let the boys leave to play.
lines: No; Master Slender is let the boys leave to play.

speeches.speech: Blessing of his heart!
lines: Blessing of his heart!

speeches.speech: Sir Hugh, my husband says my son profits nothing in the world at his book. I pray you, ask him some questions in his accidence.
lines: Sir Hugh, my husband says my son profits nothing in
the world at his book. I pray you, ask him some
questions in his accidence.

speeches.speech: Come hither, William; hold up your head; come.
lines: Come hither, William; hold up your head; come.

speeches.speech: Come on, sirrah; hold up your head; answer your master, be not afraid.
lines: Come on, sirrah; hold up your head; answer your
master, be not afraid.

speeches.speech: William, how many numbers is in nouns?
lines: William, how many numbers is in nouns?

speeches.speech: Two.
lines: Two.

speeches.speech: Truly, I thought there had been one number more, because they say, ''Od's nouns.'
lines: Truly, I thought there had been one number more,
because they say, ''Od's nouns.'

speeches.speech: Peace your tattlings! What is 'fair,' William?
lines: Peace your tattlings! What is 'fair,' William?

speeches.speech: Pulcher.
lines: Pulcher.

speeches.speech: Polecats! there are fairer things than polecats, sure.
lines: Polecats! there are fairer things than polecats, sure.

speeches.speech: You are a very simplicity 'oman: I pray you peace. What is 'lapis,' William?
lines: You are a very simplicity 'oman: I pray you peace.
What is 'lapis,' William?

speeches.speech: A stone.
lines: A stone.

speeches.speech: And what is 'a stone,' William?
lines: And what is 'a stone,' William?

speeches.speech: A pebble.
lines: A pebble.

speeches.speech: No, it is 'lapis:' I pray you, remember in your prain.
lines: No, it is 'lapis:' I pray you, remember in your prain.

speeches.speech: Lapis.
lines: Lapis.

speeches.speech: That is a good William. What is he, William, that does lend articles?
lines: That is a good William. What is he, William, that
does lend articles?

speeches.speech: Articles are borrowed of the pronoun, and be thus declined, Singulariter, nominativo, hic, haec, hoc.
lines: Articles are borrowed of the pronoun, and be thus
declined, Singulariter, nominativo, hic, haec, hoc.

speeches.speech: Nominativo, hig, hag, hog; pray you, mark: genitivo, hujus. Well, what is your accusative case?
lines: Nominativo, hig, hag, hog; pray you, mark:
genitivo, hujus. Well, what is your accusative case?

speeches.speech: Accusativo, hinc.
lines: Accusativo, hinc.

speeches.speech: I pray you, have your remembrance, child, accusative, hung, hang, hog.
lines: I pray you, have your remembrance, child,
accusative, hung, hang, hog.

speeches.speech: 'Hang-hog' is Latin for bacon, I warrant you.
lines: 'Hang-hog' is Latin for bacon, I warrant you.

speeches.speech: Leave your prabbles, 'oman. What is the focative case, William?
lines: Leave your prabbles, 'oman. What is the focative
case, William?

speeches.speech: O,--vocativo, O.
lines: O,--vocativo, O.

speeches.speech: Remember, William; focative is caret.
lines: Remember, William; focative is caret.

speeches.speech: And that's a good root.
lines: And that's a good root.

speeches.speech: 'Oman, forbear.
lines: 'Oman, forbear.

speeches.speech: Peace!
lines: Peace!

speeches.speech: What is your genitive case plural, William?
lines: What is your genitive case plural, William?

speeches.speech: Genitive case!
lines: Genitive case!

speeches.speech: Ay.
lines: Ay.

speeches.speech: Genitive,--horum, harum, horum.
lines: Genitive,--horum, harum, horum.

speeches.speech: Vengeance of Jenny's case! fie on her! never name her, child, if she be a whore.
lines: Vengeance of Jenny's case! fie on her! never name
her, child, if she be a whore.

speeches.speech: For shame, 'oman.
lines: For shame, 'oman.

speeches.speech: You do ill to teach the child such words: he teaches him to hick and to hack, which they'll do fast enough of themselves, and to call 'horum:' fie upon you!
lines: You do ill to teach the child such words: he
teaches him to hick and to hack, which they'll do
fast enough of themselves, and to call 'horum:' fie upon you!

speeches.speech: 'Oman, art thou lunatics? hast thou no understandings for thy cases and the numbers of the genders? Thou art as foolish Christian creatures as I would desires.
lines: 'Oman, art thou lunatics? hast thou no
understandings for thy cases and the numbers of the
genders? Thou art as foolish Christian creatures as
I would desires.

speeches.speech: Prithee, hold thy peace.
lines: Prithee, hold thy peace.

speeches.speech: Show me now, William, some declensions of your pronouns.
lines: Show me now, William, some declensions of your pronouns.

speeches.speech: Forsooth, I have forgot.
lines: Forsooth, I have forgot.

speeches.speech: It is qui, quae, quod: if you forget your 'quies,' your 'quaes,' and your 'quods,' you must be preeches. Go your ways, and play; go.
lines: It is qui, quae, quod: if you forget your 'quies,'
your 'quaes,' and your 'quods,' you must be
preeches. Go your ways, and play; go.

speeches.speech: He is a better scholar than I thought he was.
lines: He is a better scholar than I thought he was.

speeches.speech: He is a good sprag memory. Farewell, Mistress Page.
lines: He is a good sprag memory. Farewell, Mistress Page.

speeches.speech: Adieu, good Sir Hugh. [Exit SIR HUGH EVANS] Get you home, boy. Come, we stay too long.
lines: Adieu, good Sir Hugh.
[Exit SIR HUGH EVANS]
Get you home, boy. Come, we stay too long.

speeches.speech: Mistress Ford, your sorrow hath eaten up my sufferance. I see you are obsequious in your love, and I profess requital to a hair's breadth; not only, Mistress Ford, in the simple office of love, but in all the accoutrement, complement and ceremony of it. But are you sure of your husband now?
lines: Mistress Ford, your sorrow hath eaten up my
sufferance. I see you are obsequious in your love,
and I profess requital to a hair's breadth; not
only, Mistress Ford, in the simple
office of love, but in all the accoutrement,
complement and ceremony of it. But are you
sure of your husband now?

speeches.speech: He's a-birding, sweet Sir John.
lines: He's a-birding, sweet Sir John.

speeches.speech: [Within] What, ho, gossip Ford! what, ho!
lines: [Within] What, ho, gossip Ford! what, ho!

speeches.speech: Step into the chamber, Sir John.
lines: Step into the chamber, Sir John.

speeches.speech: How now, sweetheart! who's at home besides yourself?
lines: How now, sweetheart! who's at home besides yourself?

speeches.speech: Why, none but mine own people.
lines: Why, none but mine own people.

speeches.speech: Indeed!
lines: Indeed!

speeches.speech: No, certainly. [Aside to her] Speak louder.
lines: No, certainly.
[Aside to her]
Speak louder.

speeches.speech: Truly, I am so glad you have nobody here.
lines: Truly, I am so glad you have nobody here.

speeches.speech: Why?
lines: Why?

speeches.speech: Why, woman, your husband is in his old lunes again: he so takes on yonder with my husband; so rails against all married mankind; so curses all Eve's daughters, of what complexion soever; and so buffets himself on the forehead, crying, 'Peer out, peer out!' that any madness I ever yet beheld seemed but tameness, civility and patience, to this his distemper he is in now: I am glad the fat knight is not here.
lines: Why, woman, your husband is in his old lunes again:
he so takes on yonder with my husband; so rails
against all married mankind; so curses all Eve's
daughters, of what complexion soever; and so buffets
himself on the forehead, crying, 'Peer out, peer
out!' that any madness I ever yet beheld seemed but
tameness, civility and patience, to this his
distemper he is in now: I am glad the fat knight is not here.

speeches.speech: Why, does he talk of him?
lines: Why, does he talk of him?

speeches.speech: Of none but him; and swears he was carried out, the last time he searched for him, in a basket; protests to my husband he is now here, and hath drawn him and the rest of their company from their sport, to make another experiment of his suspicion: but I am glad the knight is not here; now he shall see his own foolery.
lines: Of none but him; and swears he was carried out, the
last time he searched for him, in a basket; protests
to my husband he is now here, and hath drawn him and
the rest of their company from their sport, to make
another experiment of his suspicion: but I am glad
the knight is not here; now he shall see his own foolery.

speeches.speech: How near is he, Mistress Page?
lines: How near is he, Mistress Page?

speeches.speech: Hard by; at street end; he will be here anon.
lines: Hard by; at street end; he will be here anon.

speeches.speech: I am undone! The knight is here.
lines: I am undone! The knight is here.

speeches.speech: Why then you are utterly shamed, and he's but a dead man. What a woman are you!--Away with him, away with him! better shame than murder.
lines: Why then you are utterly shamed, and he's but a dead
man. What a woman are you!--Away with him, away
with him! better shame than murder.

speeches.speech: Which way should be go? how should I bestow him? Shall I put him into the basket again?
lines: Which way should be go? how should I bestow him?
Shall I put him into the basket again?

speeches.speech: No, I'll come no more i' the basket. May I not go out ere he come?
lines: No, I'll come no more i' the basket. May I not go
out ere he come?

speeches.speech: Alas, three of Master Ford's brothers watch the door with pistols, that none shall issue out; otherwise you might slip away ere he came. But what make you here?
lines: Alas, three of Master Ford's brothers watch the door
with pistols, that none shall issue out; otherwise
you might slip away ere he came. But what make you here?

speeches.speech: What shall I do? I'll creep up into the chimney.
lines: What shall I do? I'll creep up into the chimney.

speeches.speech: There they always use to discharge their birding-pieces. Creep into the kiln-hole.
lines: There they always use to discharge their
birding-pieces. Creep into the kiln-hole.

speeches.speech: Where is it?
lines: Where is it?

speeches.speech: He will seek there, on my word. Neither press, coffer, chest, trunk, well, vault, but he hath an abstract for the remembrance of such places, and goes to them by his note: there is no hiding you in the house.
lines: He will seek there, on my word. Neither press,
coffer, chest, trunk, well, vault, but he hath an
abstract for the remembrance of such places, and
goes to them by his note: there is no hiding you in the house.

speeches.speech: I'll go out then.
lines: I'll go out then.

speeches.speech: If you go out in your own semblance, you die, Sir John. Unless you go out disguised--
lines: If you go out in your own semblance, you die, Sir
John. Unless you go out disguised--

speeches.speech: How might we disguise him?
lines: How might we disguise him?

speeches.speech: Alas the day, I know not! There is no woman's gown big enough for him otherwise he might put on a hat, a muffler and a kerchief, and so escape.
lines: Alas the day, I know not! There is no woman's gown
big enough for him otherwise he might put on a hat,
a muffler and a kerchief, and so escape.

speeches.speech: Good hearts, devise something: any extremity rather than a mischief.
lines: Good hearts, devise something: any extremity rather
than a mischief.

speeches.speech: My maid's aunt, the fat woman of Brentford, has a gown above.
lines: My maid's aunt, the fat woman of Brentford, has a
gown above.

speeches.speech: On my word, it will serve him; she's as big as he is: and there's her thrummed hat and her muffler too. Run up, Sir John.
lines: On my word, it will serve him; she's as big as he
is: and there's her thrummed hat and her muffler
too. Run up, Sir John.

speeches.speech: Go, go, sweet Sir John: Mistress Page and I will look some linen for your head.
lines: Go, go, sweet Sir John: Mistress Page and I will
look some linen for your head.

speeches.speech: Quick, quick! we'll come dress you straight: put on the gown the while.
lines: Quick, quick! we'll come dress you straight: put
on the gown the while.

speeches.speech: I would my husband would meet him in this shape: he cannot abide the old woman of Brentford; he swears she's a witch; forbade her my house and hath threatened to beat her.
lines: I would my husband would meet him in this shape: he
cannot abide the old woman of Brentford; he swears
she's a witch; forbade her my house and hath
threatened to beat her.

speeches.speech: Heaven guide him to thy husband's cudgel, and the devil guide his cudgel afterwards!
lines: Heaven guide him to thy husband's cudgel, and the
devil guide his cudgel afterwards!

speeches.speech: But is my husband coming?
lines: But is my husband coming?

speeches.speech: Ah, in good sadness, is he; and talks of the basket too, howsoever he hath had intelligence.
lines: Ah, in good sadness, is he; and talks of the basket
too, howsoever he hath had intelligence.

speeches.speech: We'll try that; for I'll appoint my men to carry the basket again, to meet him at the door with it, as they did last time.
lines: We'll try that; for I'll appoint my men to carry the
basket again, to meet him at the door with it, as
they did last time.

speeches.speech: Nay, but he'll be here presently: let's go dress him like the witch of Brentford.
lines: Nay, but he'll be here presently: let's go dress him
like the witch of Brentford.

speeches.speech: I'll first direct my men what they shall do with the basket. Go up; I'll bring linen for him straight.
lines: I'll first direct my men what they shall do with the
basket. Go up; I'll bring linen for him straight.

speeches.speech: Hang him, dishonest varlet! we cannot misuse him enough. We'll leave a proof, by that which we will do, Wives may be merry, and yet honest too: We do not act that often jest and laugh; 'Tis old, but true, Still swine eat all the draff.
lines: Hang him, dishonest varlet! we cannot misuse him enough.
We'll leave a proof, by that which we will do,
Wives may be merry, and yet honest too:
We do not act that often jest and laugh;
'Tis old, but true, Still swine eat all the draff.

speeches.speech: Go, sirs, take the basket again on your shoulders: your master is hard at door; if he bid you set it down, obey him: quickly, dispatch.
lines: Go, sirs, take the basket again on your shoulders:
your master is hard at door; if he bid you set it
down, obey him: quickly, dispatch.

speeches.speech: Come, come, take it up.
lines: Come, come, take it up.

speeches.speech: Pray heaven it be not full of knight again.
lines: Pray heaven it be not full of knight again.

speeches.speech: I hope not; I had as lief bear so much lead.
lines: I hope not; I had as lief bear so much lead.

speeches.speech: Ay, but if it prove true, Master Page, have you any way then to unfool me again? Set down the basket, villain! Somebody call my wife. Youth in a basket! O you panderly rascals! there's a knot, a ging, a pack, a conspiracy against me: now shall the devil be shamed. What, wife, I say! Come, come forth! Behold what honest clothes you send forth to bleaching!
lines: Ay, but if it prove true, Master Page, have you any
way then to unfool me again? Set down the basket,
villain! Somebody call my wife. Youth in a basket!
O you panderly rascals! there's a knot, a ging, a
pack, a conspiracy against me: now shall the devil
be shamed. What, wife, I say! Come, come forth!
Behold what honest clothes you send forth to bleaching!

speeches.speech: Why, this passes, Master Ford; you are not to go loose any longer; you must be pinioned.
lines: Why, this passes, Master Ford; you are not to go
loose any longer; you must be pinioned.

speeches.speech: Why, this is lunatics! this is mad as a mad dog!
lines: Why, this is lunatics! this is mad as a mad dog!

speeches.speech: Indeed, Master Ford, this is not well, indeed.
lines: Indeed, Master Ford, this is not well, indeed.

speeches.speech: So say I too, sir. [Re-enter MISTRESS FORD] Come hither, Mistress Ford; Mistress Ford the honest woman, the modest wife, the virtuous creature, that hath the jealous fool to her husband! I suspect without cause, mistress, do I?
lines: So say I too, sir.
[Re-enter MISTRESS FORD]
Come hither, Mistress Ford; Mistress Ford the honest
woman, the modest wife, the virtuous creature, that
hath the jealous fool to her husband! I suspect
without cause, mistress, do I?

speeches.speech: Heaven be my witness you do, if you suspect me in any dishonesty.
lines: Heaven be my witness you do, if you suspect me in
any dishonesty.

speeches.speech: Well said, brazen-face! hold it out. Come forth, sirrah!
lines: Well said, brazen-face! hold it out. Come forth, sirrah!

speeches.speech: This passes!
lines: This passes!

speeches.speech: Are you not ashamed? let the clothes alone.
lines: Are you not ashamed? let the clothes alone.

speeches.speech: I shall find you anon.
lines: I shall find you anon.

speeches.speech: 'Tis unreasonable! Will you take up your wife's clothes? Come away.
lines: 'Tis unreasonable! Will you take up your wife's
clothes? Come away.

speeches.speech: Empty the basket, I say!
lines: Empty the basket, I say!

speeches.speech: Why, man, why?
lines: Why, man, why?

speeches.speech: Master Page, as I am a man, there was one conveyed out of my house yesterday in this basket: why may not he be there again? In my house I am sure he is: my intelligence is true; my jealousy is reasonable. Pluck me out all the linen.
lines: Master Page, as I am a man, there was one conveyed
out of my house yesterday in this basket: why may
not he be there again? In my house I am sure he is:
my intelligence is true; my jealousy is reasonable.
Pluck me out all the linen.

speeches.speech: If you find a man there, he shall die a flea's death.
lines: If you find a man there, he shall die a flea's death.

speeches.speech: Here's no man.
lines: Here's no man.

speeches.speech: By my fidelity, this is not well, Master Ford; this wrongs you.
lines: By my fidelity, this is not well, Master Ford; this
wrongs you.

speeches.speech: Master Ford, you must pray, and not follow the imaginations of your own heart: this is jealousies.
lines: Master Ford, you must pray, and not follow the
imaginations of your own heart: this is jealousies.

speeches.speech: Well, he's not here I seek for.
lines: Well, he's not here I seek for.

speeches.speech: No, nor nowhere else but in your brain.
lines: No, nor nowhere else but in your brain.

speeches.speech: Help to search my house this one time. If I find not what I seek, show no colour for my extremity; let me for ever be your table-sport; let them say of me, 'As jealous as Ford, Chat searched a hollow walnut for his wife's leman.' Satisfy me once more; once more search with me.
lines: Help to search my house this one time. If I find
not what I seek, show no colour for my extremity; let
me for ever be your table-sport; let them say of
me, 'As jealous as Ford, Chat searched a hollow
walnut for his wife's leman.' Satisfy me once more;
once more search with me.

speeches.speech: What, ho, Mistress Page! come you and the old woman down; my husband will come into the chamber.
lines: What, ho, Mistress Page! come you and the old woman
down; my husband will come into the chamber.

speeches.speech: Old woman! what old woman's that?
lines: Old woman! what old woman's that?

speeches.speech: Nay, it is my maid's aunt of Brentford.
lines: Nay, it is my maid's aunt of Brentford.

speeches.speech: A witch, a quean, an old cozening quean! Have I not forbid her my house? She comes of errands, does she? We are simple men; we do not know what's brought to pass under the profession of fortune-telling. She works by charms, by spells, by the figure, and such daubery as this is, beyond our element we know nothing. Come down, you witch, you hag, you; come down, I say!
lines: A witch, a quean, an old cozening quean! Have I not
forbid her my house? She comes of errands, does
she? We are simple men; we do not know what's
brought to pass under the profession of
fortune-telling. She works by charms, by spells,
by the figure, and such daubery as this is, beyond
our element we know nothing. Come down, you witch,
you hag, you; come down, I say!

speeches.speech: Nay, good, sweet husband! Good gentlemen, let him not strike the old woman.
lines: Nay, good, sweet husband! Good gentlemen, let him
not strike the old woman.

speeches.speech: Come, Mother Prat; come, give me your hand.
lines: Come, Mother Prat; come, give me your hand.

speeches.speech: I'll prat her. [Beating him] Out of my door, you witch, you hag, you baggage, you polecat, you runyon! out, out! I'll conjure you, I'll fortune-tell you.
lines: I'll prat her.
[Beating him]
Out of my door, you witch, you hag, you baggage, you
polecat, you runyon! out, out! I'll conjure you,
I'll fortune-tell you.

speeches.speech: Are you not ashamed? I think you have killed the poor woman.
lines: Are you not ashamed? I think you have killed the
poor woman.

speeches.speech: Nay, he will do it. 'Tis a goodly credit for you.
lines: Nay, he will do it. 'Tis a goodly credit for you.

speeches.speech: Hang her, witch!
lines: Hang her, witch!

speeches.speech: By the yea and no, I think the 'oman is a witch indeed: I like not when a 'oman has a great peard; I spy a great peard under his muffler.
lines: By the yea and no, I think the 'oman is a witch
indeed: I like not when a 'oman has a great peard;
I spy a great peard under his muffler.

speeches.speech: Will you follow, gentlemen? I beseech you, follow; see but the issue of my jealousy: if I cry out thus upon no trail, never trust me when I open again.
lines: Will you follow, gentlemen? I beseech you, follow;
see but the issue of my jealousy: if I cry out thus
upon no trail, never trust me when I open again.

speeches.speech: Let's obey his humour a little further: come, gentlemen.
lines: Let's obey his humour a little further: come,
gentlemen.

speeches.speech: Trust me, he beat him most pitifully.
lines: Trust me, he beat him most pitifully.

speeches.speech: Nay, by the mass, that he did not; he beat him most unpitifully, methought.
lines: Nay, by the mass, that he did not; he beat him most
unpitifully, methought.

speeches.speech: I'll have the cudgel hallowed and hung o'er the altar; it hath done meritorious service.
lines: I'll have the cudgel hallowed and hung o'er the
altar; it hath done meritorious service.

speeches.speech: What think you? may we, with the warrant of womanhood and the witness of a good conscience, pursue him with any further revenge?
lines: What think you? may we, with the warrant of
womanhood and the witness of a good conscience,
pursue him with any further revenge?

speeches.speech: The spirit of wantonness is, sure, scared out of him: if the devil have him not in fee-simple, with fine and recovery, he will never, I think, in the way of waste, attempt us again.
lines: The spirit of wantonness is, sure, scared out of
him: if the devil have him not in fee-simple, with
fine and recovery, he will never, I think, in the
way of waste, attempt us again.

speeches.speech: Shall we tell our husbands how we have served him?
lines: Shall we tell our husbands how we have served him?

speeches.speech: Yes, by all means; if it be but to scrape the figures out of your husband's brains. If they can find in their hearts the poor unvirtuous fat knight shall be any further afflicted, we two will still be the ministers.
lines: Yes, by all means; if it be but to scrape the
figures out of your husband's brains. If they can
find in their hearts the poor unvirtuous fat knight
shall be any further afflicted, we two will still be
the ministers.

speeches.speech: I'll warrant they'll have him publicly shamed: and methinks there would be no period to the jest, should he not be publicly shamed.
lines: I'll warrant they'll have him publicly shamed: and
methinks there would be no period to the jest,
should he not be publicly shamed.

speeches.speech: Come, to the forge with it then; shape it: I would not have things cool.
lines: Come, to the forge with it then; shape it: I would
not have things cool.

speeches.speech: Sir, the Germans desire to have three of your horses: the duke himself will be to-morrow at court, and they are going to meet him.
lines: Sir, the Germans desire to have three of your
horses: the duke himself will be to-morrow at
court, and they are going to meet him.

speeches.speech: What duke should that be comes so secretly? I hear not of him in the court. Let me speak with the gentlemen: they speak English?
lines: What duke should that be comes so secretly? I hear
not of him in the court. Let me speak with the
gentlemen: they speak English?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir; I'll call them to you.
lines: Ay, sir; I'll call them to you.

speeches.speech: They shall have my horses; but I'll make them pay; I'll sauce them: they have had my house a week at command; I have turned away my other guests: they must come off; I'll sauce them. Come.
lines: They shall have my horses; but I'll make them pay;
I'll sauce them: they have had my house a week at
command; I have turned away my other guests: they
must come off; I'll sauce them. Come.

speeches.speech: 'Tis one of the best discretions of a 'oman as ever I did look upon.
lines: 'Tis one of the best discretions of a 'oman as ever
I did look upon.

speeches.speech: And did he send you both these letters at an instant?
lines: And did he send you both these letters at an instant?

speeches.speech: Within a quarter of an hour.
lines: Within a quarter of an hour.

speeches.speech: Pardon me, wife. Henceforth do what thou wilt; I rather will suspect the sun with cold Than thee with wantonness: now doth thy honour stand In him that was of late an heretic, As firm as faith.
lines: Pardon me, wife. Henceforth do what thou wilt;
I rather will suspect the sun with cold
Than thee with wantonness: now doth thy honour stand
In him that was of late an heretic,
As firm as faith.

speeches.speech: 'Tis well, 'tis well; no more: Be not as extreme in submission As in offence. But let our plot go forward: let our wives Yet once again, to make us public sport, Appoint a meeting with this old fat fellow, Where we may take him and disgrace him for it.
lines: 'Tis well, 'tis well; no more:
Be not as extreme in submission
As in offence.
But let our plot go forward: let our wives
Yet once again, to make us public sport,
Appoint a meeting with this old fat fellow,
Where we may take him and disgrace him for it.

speeches.speech: There is no better way than that they spoke of.
lines: There is no better way than that they spoke of.

speeches.speech: How? to send him word they'll meet him in the park at midnight? Fie, fie! he'll never come.
lines: How? to send him word they'll meet him in the park
at midnight? Fie, fie! he'll never come.

speeches.speech: You say he has been thrown in the rivers and has been grievously peaten as an old 'oman: methinks there should be terrors in him that he should not come; methinks his flesh is punished, he shall have no desires.
lines: You say he has been thrown in the rivers and has
been grievously peaten as an old 'oman: methinks
there should be terrors in him that he should not
come; methinks his flesh is punished, he shall have
no desires.

speeches.speech: So think I too.
lines: So think I too.

speeches.speech: Devise but how you'll use him when he comes, And let us two devise to bring him thither.
lines: Devise but how you'll use him when he comes,
And let us two devise to bring him thither.

speeches.speech: There is an old tale goes that Herne the hunter, Sometime a keeper here in Windsor forest, Doth all the winter-time, at still midnight, Walk round about an oak, with great ragg'd horns; And there he blasts the tree and takes the cattle And makes milch-kine yield blood and shakes a chain In a most hideous and dreadful manner: You have heard of such a spirit, and well you know The superstitious idle-headed eld Received and did deliver to our age This tale of Herne the hunter for a truth.
lines: There is an old tale goes that Herne the hunter,
Sometime a keeper here in Windsor forest,
Doth all the winter-time, at still midnight,
Walk round about an oak, with great ragg'd horns;
And there he blasts the tree and takes the cattle
And makes milch-kine yield blood and shakes a chain
In a most hideous and dreadful manner:
You have heard of such a spirit, and well you know
The superstitious idle-headed eld
Received and did deliver to our age
This tale of Herne the hunter for a truth.

speeches.speech: Why, yet there want not many that do fear In deep of night to walk by this Herne's oak: But what of this?
lines: Why, yet there want not many that do fear
In deep of night to walk by this Herne's oak:
But what of this?

speeches.speech: Marry, this is our device; That Falstaff at that oak shall meet with us.
lines: Marry, this is our device;
That Falstaff at that oak shall meet with us.

speeches.speech: Well, let it not be doubted but he'll come: And in this shape when you have brought him thither, What shall be done with him? what is your plot?
lines: Well, let it not be doubted but he'll come:
And in this shape when you have brought him thither,
What shall be done with him? what is your plot?

speeches.speech: That likewise have we thought upon, and thus: Nan Page my daughter and my little son And three or four more of their growth we'll dress Like urchins, ouphes and fairies, green and white, With rounds of waxen tapers on their heads, And rattles in their hands: upon a sudden, As Falstaff, she and I, are newly met, Let them from forth a sawpit rush at once With some diffused song: upon their sight, We two in great amazedness will fly: Then let them all encircle him about And, fairy-like, to-pinch the unclean knight, And ask him why, that hour of fairy revel, In their so sacred paths he dares to tread In shape profane.
lines: That likewise have we thought upon, and thus:
Nan Page my daughter and my little son
And three or four more of their growth we'll dress
Like urchins, ouphes and fairies, green and white,
With rounds of waxen tapers on their heads,
And rattles in their hands: upon a sudden,
As Falstaff, she and I, are newly met,
Let them from forth a sawpit rush at once
With some diffused song: upon their sight,
We two in great amazedness will fly:
Then let them all encircle him about
And, fairy-like, to-pinch the unclean knight,
And ask him why, that hour of fairy revel,
In their so sacred paths he dares to tread
In shape profane.

speeches.speech: And till he tell the truth, Let the supposed fairies pinch him sound And burn him with their tapers.
lines: And till he tell the truth,
Let the supposed fairies pinch him sound
And burn him with their tapers.

speeches.speech: The truth being known, We'll all present ourselves, dis-horn the spirit, And mock him home to Windsor.
lines: The truth being known,
We'll all present ourselves, dis-horn the spirit,
And mock him home to Windsor.

speeches.speech: The children must Be practised well to this, or they'll ne'er do't.
lines: The children must
Be practised well to this, or they'll ne'er do't.

speeches.speech: I will teach the children their behaviors; and I will be like a jack-an-apes also, to burn the knight with my taber.
lines: I will teach the children their behaviors; and I
will be like a jack-an-apes also, to burn the
knight with my taber.

speeches.speech: That will be excellent. I'll go and buy them vizards.
lines: That will be excellent. I'll go and buy them vizards.

speeches.speech: My Nan shall be the queen of all the fairies, Finely attired in a robe of white.
lines: My Nan shall be the queen of all the fairies,
Finely attired in a robe of white.

speeches.speech: That silk will I go buy. [Aside] And in that time Shall Master Slender steal my Nan away And marry her at Eton. Go send to Falstaff straight.
lines: That silk will I go buy.
[Aside]
And in that time
Shall Master Slender steal my Nan away
And marry her at Eton. Go send to Falstaff straight.

speeches.speech: Nay I'll to him again in name of Brook He'll tell me all his purpose: sure, he'll come.
lines: Nay I'll to him again in name of Brook
He'll tell me all his purpose: sure, he'll come.

speeches.speech: Fear not you that. Go get us properties And tricking for our fairies.
lines: Fear not you that. Go get us properties
And tricking for our fairies.

speeches.speech: Let us about it: it is admirable pleasures and fery honest knaveries.
lines: Let us about it: it is admirable pleasures and fery
honest knaveries.

speeches.speech: Go, Mistress Ford, Send quickly to Sir John, to know his mind. [Exit MISTRESS FORD] I'll to the doctor: he hath my good will, And none but he, to marry with Nan Page. That Slender, though well landed, is an idiot; And he my husband best of all affects. The doctor is well money'd, and his friends Potent at court: he, none but he, shall have her, Though twenty thousand worthier come to crave her.
lines: Go, Mistress Ford,
Send quickly to Sir John, to know his mind.
[Exit MISTRESS FORD]
I'll to the doctor: he hath my good will,
And none but he, to marry with Nan Page.
That Slender, though well landed, is an idiot;
And he my husband best of all affects.
The doctor is well money'd, and his friends
Potent at court: he, none but he, shall have her,
Though twenty thousand worthier come to crave her.

speeches.speech: What wouldst thou have, boor? what: thick-skin? speak, breathe, discuss; brief, short, quick, snap.
lines: What wouldst thou have, boor? what: thick-skin?
speak, breathe, discuss; brief, short, quick, snap.

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, I come to speak with Sir John Falstaff from Master Slender.
lines: Marry, sir, I come to speak with Sir John Falstaff
from Master Slender.

speeches.speech: There's his chamber, his house, his castle, his standing-bed and truckle-bed; 'tis painted about with the story of the Prodigal, fresh and new. Go knock and call; hell speak like an Anthropophaginian unto thee: knock, I say.
lines: There's his chamber, his house, his castle, his
standing-bed and truckle-bed; 'tis painted about
with the story of the Prodigal, fresh and new. Go
knock and call; hell speak like an Anthropophaginian
unto thee: knock, I say.

speeches.speech: There's an old woman, a fat woman, gone up into his chamber: I'll be so bold as stay, sir, till she come down; I come to speak with her, indeed.
lines: There's an old woman, a fat woman, gone up into his
chamber: I'll be so bold as stay, sir, till she come
down; I come to speak with her, indeed.

speeches.speech: Ha! a fat woman! the knight may be robbed: I'll call. Bully knight! bully Sir John! speak from thy lungs military: art thou there? it is thine host, thine Ephesian, calls.
lines: Ha! a fat woman! the knight may be robbed: I'll
call. Bully knight! bully Sir John! speak from
thy lungs military: art thou there? it is thine
host, thine Ephesian, calls.

speeches.speech: [Above] How now, mine host!
lines: [Above] How now, mine host!

speeches.speech: Here's a Bohemian-Tartar tarries the coming down of thy fat woman. Let her descend, bully, let her descend; my chambers are honourable: fie! privacy? fie!
lines: Here's a Bohemian-Tartar tarries the coming down of
thy fat woman. Let her descend, bully, let her
descend; my chambers are honourable: fie! privacy?
fie!

speeches.speech: There was, mine host, an old fat woman even now with me; but she's gone.
lines: There was, mine host, an old fat woman even now with
me; but she's gone.

speeches.speech: Pray you, sir, was't not the wise woman of Brentford?
lines: Pray you, sir, was't not the wise woman of
Brentford?

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, was it, mussel-shell: what would you with her?
lines: Ay, marry, was it, mussel-shell: what would you with her?

speeches.speech: My master, sir, Master Slender, sent to her, seeing her go through the streets, to know, sir, whether one Nym, sir, that beguiled him of a chain, had the chain or no.
lines: My master, sir, Master Slender, sent to her, seeing
her go through the streets, to know, sir, whether
one Nym, sir, that beguiled him of a chain, had the
chain or no.

speeches.speech: I spake with the old woman about it.
lines: I spake with the old woman about it.

speeches.speech: And what says she, I pray, sir?
lines: And what says she, I pray, sir?

speeches.speech: Marry, she says that the very same man that beguiled Master Slender of his chain cozened him of it.
lines: Marry, she says that the very same man that
beguiled Master Slender of his chain cozened him of
it.

speeches.speech: I would I could have spoken with the woman herself; I had other things to have spoken with her too from him.
lines: I would I could have spoken with the woman herself;
I had other things to have spoken with her too from
him.

speeches.speech: What are they? let us know.
lines: What are they? let us know.

speeches.speech: Ay, come; quick.
lines: Ay, come; quick.

speeches.speech: I may not conceal them, sir.
lines: I may not conceal them, sir.

speeches.speech: Conceal them, or thou diest.
lines: Conceal them, or thou diest.

speeches.speech: Why, sir, they were nothing but about Mistress Anne Page; to know if it were my master's fortune to have her or no.
lines: Why, sir, they were nothing but about Mistress Anne
Page; to know if it were my master's fortune to
have her or no.

speeches.speech: 'Tis, 'tis his fortune.
lines: 'Tis, 'tis his fortune.

speeches.speech: What, sir?
lines: What, sir?

speeches.speech: To have her, or no. Go; say the woman told me so.
lines: To have her, or no. Go; say the woman told me so.

speeches.speech: May I be bold to say so, sir?
lines: May I be bold to say so, sir?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir; like who more bold.
lines: Ay, sir; like who more bold.

speeches.speech: I thank your worship: I shall make my master glad with these tidings.
lines: I thank your worship: I shall make my master glad
with these tidings.

speeches.speech: Thou art clerkly, thou art clerkly, Sir John. Was there a wise woman with thee?
lines: Thou art clerkly, thou art clerkly, Sir John. Was
there a wise woman with thee?

speeches.speech: Ay, that there was, mine host; one that hath taught me more wit than ever I learned before in my life; and I paid nothing for it neither, but was paid for my learning.
lines: Ay, that there was, mine host; one that hath taught
me more wit than ever I learned before in my life;
and I paid nothing for it neither, but was paid for
my learning.

speeches.speech: Out, alas, sir! cozenage, mere cozenage!
lines: Out, alas, sir! cozenage, mere cozenage!

speeches.speech: Where be my horses? speak well of them, varletto.
lines: Where be my horses? speak well of them, varletto.

speeches.speech: Run away with the cozeners; for so soon as I came beyond Eton, they threw me off from behind one of them, in a slough of mire; and set spurs and away, like three German devils, three Doctor Faustuses.
lines: Run away with the cozeners; for so soon as I came
beyond Eton, they threw me off from behind one of
them, in a slough of mire; and set spurs and away,
like three German devils, three Doctor Faustuses.

speeches.speech: They are gone but to meet the duke, villain: do not say they be fled; Germans are honest men.
lines: They are gone but to meet the duke, villain: do not
say they be fled; Germans are honest men.

speeches.speech: Where is mine host?
lines: Where is mine host?

speeches.speech: What is the matter, sir?
lines: What is the matter, sir?

speeches.speech: Have a care of your entertainments: there is a friend of mine come to town tells me there is three cozen-germans that has cozened all the hosts of Readins, of Maidenhead, of Colebrook, of horses and money. I tell you for good will, look you: you are wise and full of gibes and vlouting-stocks, and 'tis not convenient you should be cozened. Fare you well.
lines: Have a care of your entertainments: there is a
friend of mine come to town tells me there is three
cozen-germans that has cozened all the hosts of
Readins, of Maidenhead, of Colebrook, of horses and
money. I tell you for good will, look you: you
are wise and full of gibes and vlouting-stocks, and
'tis not convenient you should be cozened. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: Vere is mine host de Jarteer?
lines: Vere is mine host de Jarteer?

speeches.speech: Here, master doctor, in perplexity and doubtful dilemma.
lines: Here, master doctor, in perplexity and doubtful dilemma.

speeches.speech: I cannot tell vat is dat: but it is tell-a me dat you make grand preparation for a duke de Jamany: by my trot, dere is no duke dat the court is know to come. I tell you for good vill: adieu.
lines: I cannot tell vat is dat: but it is tell-a me dat
you make grand preparation for a duke de Jamany: by
my trot, dere is no duke dat the court is know to
come. I tell you for good vill: adieu.

speeches.speech: Hue and cry, villain, go! Assist me, knight. I am undone! Fly, run, hue and cry, villain! I am undone!
lines: Hue and cry, villain, go! Assist me, knight. I am
undone! Fly, run, hue and cry, villain! I am undone!

speeches.speech: I would all the world might be cozened; for I have been cozened and beaten too. If it should come to the ear of the court, how I have been transformed and how my transformation hath been washed and cudgelled, they would melt me out of my fat drop by drop and liquor fishermen's boots with me; I warrant they would whip me with their fine wits till I were as crest-fallen as a dried pear. I never prospered since I forswore myself at primero. Well, if my wind were but long enough to say my prayers, I would repent. [Enter MISTRESS QUICKLY] Now, whence come you?
lines: I would all the world might be cozened; for I have
been cozened and beaten too. If it should come to
the ear of the court, how I have been transformed
and how my transformation hath been washed and
cudgelled, they would melt me out of my fat drop by
drop and liquor fishermen's boots with me; I warrant
they would whip me with their fine wits till I were
as crest-fallen as a dried pear. I never prospered
since I forswore myself at primero. Well, if my
wind were but long enough to say my prayers, I would repent.
[Enter MISTRESS QUICKLY]
Now, whence come you?

speeches.speech: From the two parties, forsooth.
lines: From the two parties, forsooth.

speeches.speech: The devil take one party and his dam the other! and so they shall be both bestowed. I have suffered more for their sakes, more than the villanous inconstancy of man's disposition is able to bear.
lines: The devil take one party and his dam the other! and
so they shall be both bestowed. I have suffered more
for their sakes, more than the villanous inconstancy
of man's disposition is able to bear.

speeches.speech: And have not they suffered? Yes, I warrant; speciously one of them; Mistress Ford, good heart, is beaten black and blue, that you cannot see a white spot about her.
lines: And have not they suffered? Yes, I warrant;
speciously one of them; Mistress Ford, good heart,
is beaten black and blue, that you cannot see a
white spot about her.

speeches.speech: What tellest thou me of black and blue? I was beaten myself into all the colours of the rainbow; and I was like to be apprehended for the witch of Brentford: but that my admirable dexterity of wit, my counterfeiting the action of an old woman, delivered me, the knave constable had set me i' the stocks, i' the common stocks, for a witch.
lines: What tellest thou me of black and blue? I was
beaten myself into all the colours of the rainbow;
and I was like to be apprehended for the witch of
Brentford: but that my admirable dexterity of wit,
my counterfeiting the action of an old woman,
delivered me, the knave constable had set me i' the
stocks, i' the common stocks, for a witch.

speeches.speech: Sir, let me speak with you in your chamber: you shall hear how things go; and, I warrant, to your content. Here is a letter will say somewhat. Good hearts, what ado here is to bring you together! Sure, one of you does not serve heaven well, that you are so crossed.
lines: Sir, let me speak with you in your chamber: you
shall hear how things go; and, I warrant, to your
content. Here is a letter will say somewhat. Good
hearts, what ado here is to bring you together!
Sure, one of you does not serve heaven well, that
you are so crossed.

speeches.speech: Come up into my chamber.
lines: Come up into my chamber.

speeches.speech: Master Fenton, talk not to me; my mind is heavy: I will give over all.
lines: Master Fenton, talk not to me; my mind is heavy: I
will give over all.

speeches.speech: Yet hear me speak. Assist me in my purpose, And, as I am a gentleman, I'll give thee A hundred pound in gold more than your loss.
lines: Yet hear me speak. Assist me in my purpose,
And, as I am a gentleman, I'll give thee
A hundred pound in gold more than your loss.

speeches.speech: I will hear you, Master Fenton; and I will at the least keep your counsel.
lines: I will hear you, Master Fenton; and I will at the
least keep your counsel.

speeches.speech: From time to time I have acquainted you With the dear love I bear to fair Anne Page; Who mutually hath answer'd my affection, So far forth as herself might be her chooser, Even to my wish: I have a letter from her Of such contents as you will wonder at; The mirth whereof so larded with my matter, That neither singly can be manifested, Without the show of both; fat Falstaff Hath a great scene: the image of the jest I'll show you here at large. Hark, good mine host. To-night at Herne's oak, just 'twixt twelve and one, Must my sweet Nan present the Fairy Queen; The purpose why, is here: in which disguise, While other jests are something rank on foot, Her father hath commanded her to slip Away with Slender and with him at Eton Immediately to marry: she hath consented: Now, sir, Her mother, ever strong against that match And firm for Doctor Caius, hath appointed That he shall likewise shuffle her away, While other sports are tasking of their minds, And at the deanery, where a priest attends, Straight marry her: to this her mother's plot She seemingly obedient likewise hath Made promise to the doctor. Now, thus it rests: Her father means she shall be all in white, And in that habit, when Slender sees his time To take her by the hand and bid her go, She shall go with him: her mother hath intended, The better to denote her to the doctor, For they must all be mask'd and vizarded, That quaint in green she shall be loose enrobed, With ribands pendent, flaring 'bout her head; And when the doctor spies his vantage ripe, To pinch her by the hand, and, on that token, The maid hath given consent to go with him.
lines: From time to time I have acquainted you
With the dear love I bear to fair Anne Page;
Who mutually hath answer'd my affection,
So far forth as herself might be her chooser,
Even to my wish: I have a letter from her
Of such contents as you will wonder at;
The mirth whereof so larded with my matter,
That neither singly can be manifested,
Without the show of both; fat Falstaff
Hath a great scene: the image of the jest
I'll show you here at large. Hark, good mine host.
To-night at Herne's oak, just 'twixt twelve and one,
Must my sweet Nan present the Fairy Queen;
The purpose why, is here: in which disguise,
While other jests are something rank on foot,
Her father hath commanded her to slip
Away with Slender and with him at Eton
Immediately to marry: she hath consented: Now, sir,
Her mother, ever strong against that match
And firm for Doctor Caius, hath appointed
That he shall likewise shuffle her away,
While other sports are tasking of their minds,
And at the deanery, where a priest attends,
Straight marry her: to this her mother's plot
She seemingly obedient likewise hath
Made promise to the doctor. Now, thus it rests:
Her father means she shall be all in white,
And in that habit, when Slender sees his time
To take her by the hand and bid her go,
She shall go with him: her mother hath intended,
The better to denote her to the doctor,
For they must all be mask'd and vizarded,
That quaint in green she shall be loose enrobed,
With ribands pendent, flaring 'bout her head;
And when the doctor spies his vantage ripe,
To pinch her by the hand, and, on that token,
The maid hath given consent to go with him.

speeches.speech: Which means she to deceive, father or mother?
lines: Which means she to deceive, father or mother?

speeches.speech: Both, my good host, to go along with me: And here it rests, that you'll procure the vicar To stay for me at church 'twixt twelve and one, And, in the lawful name of marrying, To give our hearts united ceremony.
lines: Both, my good host, to go along with me:
And here it rests, that you'll procure the vicar
To stay for me at church 'twixt twelve and one,
And, in the lawful name of marrying,
To give our hearts united ceremony.

speeches.speech: Well, husband your device; I'll to the vicar: Bring you the maid, you shall not lack a priest.
lines: Well, husband your device; I'll to the vicar:
Bring you the maid, you shall not lack a priest.

speeches.speech: So shall I evermore be bound to thee; Besides, I'll make a present recompense.
lines: So shall I evermore be bound to thee;
Besides, I'll make a present recompense.

speeches.speech: Prithee, no more prattling; go. I'll hold. This is the third time; I hope good luck lies in odd numbers. Away I go. They say there is divinity in odd numbers, either in nativity, chance, or death. Away!
lines: Prithee, no more prattling; go. I'll hold. This is
the third time; I hope good luck lies in odd
numbers. Away I go. They say there is divinity in
odd numbers, either in nativity, chance, or death. Away!

speeches.speech: I'll provide you a chain; and I'll do what I can to get you a pair of horns.
lines: I'll provide you a chain; and I'll do what I can to
get you a pair of horns.

speeches.speech: Away, I say; time wears: hold up your head, and mince. [Exit MISTRESS QUICKLY] [Enter FORD] How now, Master Brook! Master Brook, the matter will be known to-night, or never. Be you in the Park about midnight, at Herne's oak, and you shall see wonders.
lines: Away, I say; time wears: hold up your head, and mince.
[Exit MISTRESS QUICKLY]
[Enter FORD]
How now, Master Brook! Master Brook, the matter
will be known to-night, or never. Be you in the
Park about midnight, at Herne's oak, and you shall
see wonders.

speeches.speech: Went you not to her yesterday, sir, as you told me you had appointed?
lines: Went you not to her yesterday, sir, as you told me
you had appointed?

speeches.speech: I went to her, Master Brook, as you see, like a poor old man: but I came from her, Master Brook, like a poor old woman. That same knave Ford, her husband, hath the finest mad devil of jealousy in him, Master Brook, that ever governed frenzy. I will tell you: he beat me grievously, in the shape of a woman; for in the shape of man, Master Brook, I fear not Goliath with a weaver's beam; because I know also life is a shuttle. I am in haste; go along with me: I'll tell you all, Master Brook. Since I plucked geese, played truant and whipped top, I knew not what 'twas to be beaten till lately. Follow me: I'll tell you strange things of this knave Ford, on whom to-night I will be revenged, and I will deliver his wife into your hand. Follow. Strange things in hand, Master Brook! Follow.
lines: I went to her, Master Brook, as you see, like a poor
old man: but I came from her, Master Brook, like a
poor old woman. That same knave Ford, her husband,
hath the finest mad devil of jealousy in him,
Master Brook, that ever governed frenzy. I will tell
you: he beat me grievously, in the shape of a
woman; for in the shape of man, Master Brook, I fear
not Goliath with a weaver's beam; because I know
also life is a shuttle. I am in haste; go along
with me: I'll tell you all, Master Brook. Since I
plucked geese, played truant and whipped top, I knew
not what 'twas to be beaten till lately. Follow
me: I'll tell you strange things of this knave
Ford, on whom to-night I will be revenged, and I
will deliver his wife into your hand. Follow.
Strange things in hand, Master Brook! Follow.

speeches.speech: Come, come; we'll couch i' the castle-ditch till we see the light of our fairies. Remember, son Slender, my daughter.
lines: Come, come; we'll couch i' the castle-ditch till we
see the light of our fairies. Remember, son Slender,
my daughter.

speeches.speech: Ay, forsooth; I have spoke with her and we have a nay-word how to know one another: I come to her in white, and cry 'mum;' she cries 'budget;' and by that we know one another.
lines: Ay, forsooth; I have spoke with her and we have a
nay-word how to know one another: I come to her in
white, and cry 'mum;' she cries 'budget;' and by
that we know one another.

speeches.speech: That's good too: but what needs either your 'mum' or her 'budget?' the white will decipher her well enough. It hath struck ten o'clock.
lines: That's good too: but what needs either your 'mum'
or her 'budget?' the white will decipher her well
enough. It hath struck ten o'clock.

speeches.speech: The night is dark; light and spirits will become it well. Heaven prosper our sport! No man means evil but the devil, and we shall know him by his horns. Let's away; follow me.
lines: The night is dark; light and spirits will become it
well. Heaven prosper our sport! No man means evil
but the devil, and we shall know him by his horns.
Let's away; follow me.

speeches.speech: Master doctor, my daughter is in green: when you see your time, take her by the band, away with her to the deanery, and dispatch it quickly. Go before into the Park: we two must go together.
lines: Master doctor, my daughter is in green: when you
see your time, take her by the band, away with her
to the deanery, and dispatch it quickly. Go before
into the Park: we two must go together.

speeches.speech: I know vat I have to do. Adieu.
lines: I know vat I have to do. Adieu.

speeches.speech: Fare you well, sir. [Exit DOCTOR CAIUS] My husband will not rejoice so much at the abuse of Falstaff as he will chafe at the doctor's marrying my daughter: but 'tis no matter; better a little chiding than a great deal of heart-break.
lines: Fare you well, sir.
[Exit DOCTOR CAIUS]
My husband will not rejoice so much at the abuse of
Falstaff as he will chafe at the doctor's marrying
my daughter: but 'tis no matter; better a little
chiding than a great deal of heart-break.

speeches.speech: Where is Nan now and her troop of fairies, and the Welsh devil Hugh?
lines: Where is Nan now and her troop of fairies, and the
Welsh devil Hugh?

speeches.speech: They are all couched in a pit hard by Herne's oak, with obscured lights; which, at the very instant of Falstaff's and our meeting, they will at once display to the night.
lines: They are all couched in a pit hard by Herne's oak,
with obscured lights; which, at the very instant of
Falstaff's and our meeting, they will at once
display to the night.

speeches.speech: That cannot choose but amaze him.
lines: That cannot choose but amaze him.

speeches.speech: If he be not amazed, he will be mocked; if he be amazed, he will every way be mocked.
lines: If he be not amazed, he will be mocked; if he be
amazed, he will every way be mocked.

speeches.speech: We'll betray him finely.
lines: We'll betray him finely.

speeches.speech: Against such lewdsters and their lechery Those that betray them do no treachery.
lines: Against such lewdsters and their lechery
Those that betray them do no treachery.

speeches.speech: The hour draws on. To the oak, to the oak!
lines: The hour draws on. To the oak, to the oak!

speeches.speech: Trib, trib, fairies; come; and remember your parts: be pold, I pray you; follow me into the pit; and when I give the watch-'ords, do as I pid you: come, come; trib, trib.
lines: Trib, trib, fairies; come; and remember your parts:
be pold, I pray you; follow me into the pit; and
when I give the watch-'ords, do as I pid you:
come, come; trib, trib.

speeches.speech: The Windsor bell hath struck twelve; the minute draws on. Now, the hot-blooded gods assist me! Remember, Jove, thou wast a bull for thy Europa; love set on thy horns. O powerful love! that, in some respects, makes a beast a man, in some other, a man a beast. You were also, Jupiter, a swan for the love of Leda. O omnipotent Love! how near the god drew to the complexion of a goose! A fault done first in the form of a beast. O Jove, a beastly fault! And then another fault in the semblance of a fowl; think on 't, Jove; a foul fault! When gods have hot backs, what shall poor men do? For me, I am here a Windsor stag; and the fattest, I think, i' the forest. Send me a cool rut-time, Jove, or who can blame me to piss my tallow? Who comes here? my doe?
lines: The Windsor bell hath struck twelve; the minute
draws on. Now, the hot-blooded gods assist me!
Remember, Jove, thou wast a bull for thy Europa; love
set on thy horns. O powerful love! that, in some
respects, makes a beast a man, in some other, a man
a beast. You were also, Jupiter, a swan for the love
of Leda. O omnipotent Love! how near the god drew
to the complexion of a goose! A fault done first in
the form of a beast. O Jove, a beastly fault! And
then another fault in the semblance of a fowl; think
on 't, Jove; a foul fault! When gods have hot
backs, what shall poor men do? For me, I am here a
Windsor stag; and the fattest, I think, i' the
forest. Send me a cool rut-time, Jove, or who can
blame me to piss my tallow? Who comes here? my
doe?

speeches.speech: Sir John! art thou there, my deer? my male deer?
lines: Sir John! art thou there, my deer? my male deer?

speeches.speech: My doe with the black scut! Let the sky rain potatoes; let it thunder to the tune of Green Sleeves, hail kissing-comfits and snow eringoes; let there come a tempest of provocation, I will shelter me here.
lines: My doe with the black scut! Let the sky rain
potatoes; let it thunder to the tune of Green
Sleeves, hail kissing-comfits and snow eringoes; let
there come a tempest of provocation, I will shelter me here.

speeches.speech: Mistress Page is come with me, sweetheart.
lines: Mistress Page is come with me, sweetheart.

speeches.speech: Divide me like a bribe buck, each a haunch: I will keep my sides to myself, my shoulders for the fellow of this walk, and my horns I bequeath your husbands. Am I a woodman, ha? Speak I like Herne the hunter? Why, now is Cupid a child of conscience; he makes restitution. As I am a true spirit, welcome!
lines: Divide me like a bribe buck, each a haunch: I will
keep my sides to myself, my shoulders for the fellow
of this walk, and my horns I bequeath your husbands.
Am I a woodman, ha? Speak I like Herne the hunter?
Why, now is Cupid a child of conscience; he makes
restitution. As I am a true spirit, welcome!

speeches.speech: Alas, what noise?
lines: Alas, what noise?

speeches.speech: Heaven forgive our sins
lines: Heaven forgive our sins

speeches.speech: What should this be?
lines: What should this be?

speeches.speech: [with Mistress Page] Away, away!
lines: [with Mistress Page] Away, away!

speeches.speech: I think the devil will not have me damned, lest the oil that's in me should set hell on fire; he would never else cross me thus. [Enter SIR HUGH EVANS, disguised as before; PISTOL,] as Hobgoblin; MISTRESS QUICKLY, ANNE PAGE, and others, as Fairies, with tapers]
lines: I think the devil will not have me damned, lest the
oil that's in me should set hell on fire; he would
never else cross me thus.
[Enter SIR HUGH EVANS, disguised as before; PISTOL,]
as Hobgoblin; MISTRESS QUICKLY, ANNE PAGE, and
others, as Fairies, with tapers]

speeches.speech: Fairies, black, grey, green, and white, You moonshine revellers and shades of night, You orphan heirs of fixed destiny, Attend your office and your quality. Crier Hobgoblin, make the fairy oyes.
lines: Fairies, black, grey, green, and white,
You moonshine revellers and shades of night,
You orphan heirs of fixed destiny,
Attend your office and your quality.
Crier Hobgoblin, make the fairy oyes.

speeches.speech: Elves, list your names; silence, you airy toys. Cricket, to Windsor chimneys shalt thou leap: Where fires thou find'st unraked and hearths unswept, There pinch the maids as blue as bilberry: Our radiant queen hates sluts and sluttery.
lines: Elves, list your names; silence, you airy toys.
Cricket, to Windsor chimneys shalt thou leap:
Where fires thou find'st unraked and hearths unswept,
There pinch the maids as blue as bilberry:
Our radiant queen hates sluts and sluttery.

speeches.speech: They are fairies; he that speaks to them shall die: I'll wink and couch: no man their works must eye.
lines: They are fairies; he that speaks to them shall die:
I'll wink and couch: no man their works must eye.

speeches.speech: Where's Bede? Go you, and where you find a maid That, ere she sleep, has thrice her prayers said, Raise up the organs of her fantasy; Sleep she as sound as careless infancy: But those as sleep and think not on their sins, Pinch them, arms, legs, backs, shoulders, sides and shins.
lines: Where's Bede? Go you, and where you find a maid
That, ere she sleep, has thrice her prayers said,
Raise up the organs of her fantasy;
Sleep she as sound as careless infancy:
But those as sleep and think not on their sins,
Pinch them, arms, legs, backs, shoulders, sides and shins.

speeches.speech: About, about; Search Windsor Castle, elves, within and out: Strew good luck, ouphes, on every sacred room:. That it may stand till the perpetual doom, In state as wholesome as in state 'tis fit, Worthy the owner, and the owner it. The several chairs of order look you scour With juice of balm and every precious flower: Each fair instalment, coat, and several crest, With loyal blazon, evermore be blest! And nightly, meadow-fairies, look you sing, Like to the Garter's compass, in a ring: The expressure that it bears, green let it be, More fertile-fresh than all the field to see; And 'Honi soit qui mal y pense' write In emerald tufts, flowers purple, blue and white; Let sapphire, pearl and rich embroidery, Buckled below fair knighthood's bending knee: Fairies use flowers for their charactery. Away; disperse: but till 'tis one o'clock, Our dance of custom round about the oak Of Herne the hunter, let us not forget.
lines: About, about;
Search Windsor Castle, elves, within and out:
Strew good luck, ouphes, on every sacred room:. That it may stand till the perpetual doom,
In state as wholesome as in state 'tis fit,
Worthy the owner, and the owner it.
The several chairs of order look you scour
With juice of balm and every precious flower:
Each fair instalment, coat, and several crest,
With loyal blazon, evermore be blest!
And nightly, meadow-fairies, look you sing,
Like to the Garter's compass, in a ring:
The expressure that it bears, green let it be,
More fertile-fresh than all the field to see;
And 'Honi soit qui mal y pense' write
In emerald tufts, flowers purple, blue and white;
Let sapphire, pearl and rich embroidery,
Buckled below fair knighthood's bending knee:
Fairies use flowers for their charactery.
Away; disperse: but till 'tis one o'clock,
Our dance of custom round about the oak
Of Herne the hunter, let us not forget.

speeches.speech: Pray you, lock hand in hand; yourselves in order set And twenty glow-worms shall our lanterns be, To guide our measure round about the tree. But, stay; I smell a man of middle-earth.
lines: Pray you, lock hand in hand; yourselves in order set
And twenty glow-worms shall our lanterns be,
To guide our measure round about the tree.
But, stay; I smell a man of middle-earth.

speeches.speech: Heavens defend me from that Welsh fairy, lest he transform me to a piece of cheese!
lines: Heavens defend me from that Welsh fairy, lest he
transform me to a piece of cheese!

speeches.speech: Vile worm, thou wast o'erlook'd even in thy birth.
lines: Vile worm, thou wast o'erlook'd even in thy birth.

speeches.speech: With trial-fire touch me his finger-end: If he be chaste, the flame will back descend And turn him to no pain; but if he start, It is the flesh of a corrupted heart.
lines: With trial-fire touch me his finger-end:
If he be chaste, the flame will back descend
And turn him to no pain; but if he start,
It is the flesh of a corrupted heart.

speeches.speech: A trial, come.
lines: A trial, come.

speeches.speech: Come, will this wood take fire?
lines: Come, will this wood take fire?

speeches.speech: Oh, Oh, Oh!
lines: Oh, Oh, Oh!

speeches.speech: Corrupt, corrupt, and tainted in desire! About him, fairies; sing a scornful rhyme; And, as you trip, still pinch him to your time. SONG. Fie on sinful fantasy! Fie on lust and luxury! Lust is but a bloody fire, Kindled with unchaste desire, Fed in heart, whose flames aspire As thoughts do blow them, higher and higher. Pinch him, fairies, mutually; Pinch him for his villany; Pinch him, and burn him, and turn him about, Till candles and starlight and moonshine be out. [During this song they pinch FALSTAFF. DOCTOR CAIUS] comes one way, and steals away a boy in green; SLENDER another way, and takes off a boy in white; and FENTON comes and steals away ANN PAGE. A noise of hunting is heard within. All the Fairies run away. FALSTAFF pulls off his buck's head, and rises]
lines: Corrupt, corrupt, and tainted in desire!
About him, fairies; sing a scornful rhyme;
And, as you trip, still pinch him to your time.
SONG.
Fie on sinful fantasy!
Fie on lust and luxury!
Lust is but a bloody fire,
Kindled with unchaste desire,
Fed in heart, whose flames aspire
As thoughts do blow them, higher and higher.
Pinch him, fairies, mutually;
Pinch him for his villany;
Pinch him, and burn him, and turn him about,
Till candles and starlight and moonshine be out.
[During this song they pinch FALSTAFF. DOCTOR CAIUS]
comes one way, and steals away a boy in green;
SLENDER another way, and takes off a boy in white;
and FENTON comes and steals away ANN PAGE.
A noise of hunting is heard within. All the
Fairies run away. FALSTAFF pulls off his buck's
head, and rises]

speeches.speech: Nay, do not fly; I think we have watch'd you now Will none but Herne the hunter serve your turn?
lines: Nay, do not fly; I think we have watch'd you now
Will none but Herne the hunter serve your turn?

speeches.speech: I pray you, come, hold up the jest no higher Now, good Sir John, how like you Windsor wives? See you these, husband? do not these fair yokes Become the forest better than the town?
lines: I pray you, come, hold up the jest no higher
Now, good Sir John, how like you Windsor wives?
See you these, husband? do not these fair yokes
Become the forest better than the town?

speeches.speech: Now, sir, who's a cuckold now? Master Brook, Falstaff's a knave, a cuckoldly knave; here are his horns, Master Brook: and, Master Brook, he hath enjoyed nothing of Ford's but his buck-basket, his cudgel, and twenty pounds of money, which must be paid to Master Brook; his horses are arrested for it, Master Brook.
lines: Now, sir, who's a cuckold now? Master Brook,
Falstaff's a knave, a cuckoldly knave; here are his
horns, Master Brook: and, Master Brook, he hath
enjoyed nothing of Ford's but his buck-basket, his
cudgel, and twenty pounds of money, which must be
paid to Master Brook; his horses are arrested for
it, Master Brook.

speeches.speech: Sir John, we have had ill luck; we could never meet. I will never take you for my love again; but I will always count you my deer.
lines: Sir John, we have had ill luck; we could never meet.
I will never take you for my love again; but I will
always count you my deer.

speeches.speech: I do begin to perceive that I am made an ass.
lines: I do begin to perceive that I am made an ass.

speeches.speech: Ay, and an ox too: both the proofs are extant.
lines: Ay, and an ox too: both the proofs are extant.

speeches.speech: And these are not fairies? I was three or four times in the thought they were not fairies: and yet the guiltiness of my mind, the sudden surprise of my powers, drove the grossness of the foppery into a received belief, in despite of the teeth of all rhyme and reason, that they were fairies. See now how wit may be made a Jack-a-Lent, when 'tis upon ill employment!
lines: And these are not fairies? I was three or four
times in the thought they were not fairies: and yet
the guiltiness of my mind, the sudden surprise of my
powers, drove the grossness of the foppery into a
received belief, in despite of the teeth of all
rhyme and reason, that they were fairies. See now
how wit may be made a Jack-a-Lent, when 'tis upon
ill employment!

speeches.speech: Sir John Falstaff, serve Got, and leave your desires, and fairies will not pinse you.
lines: Sir John Falstaff, serve Got, and leave your
desires, and fairies will not pinse you.
