speeches.speech: Well said, fairy Hugh.
lines: Well said, fairy Hugh.

speeches.speech: And leave your jealousies too, I pray you.
lines: And leave your jealousies too, I pray you.

speeches.speech: I will never mistrust my wife again till thou art able to woo her in good English.
lines: I will never mistrust my wife again till thou art
able to woo her in good English.

speeches.speech: Have I laid my brain in the sun and dried it, that it wants matter to prevent so gross o'erreaching as this? Am I ridden with a Welsh goat too? shall I have a coxcomb of frize? 'Tis time I were choked with a piece of toasted cheese.
lines: Have I laid my brain in the sun and dried it, that
it wants matter to prevent so gross o'erreaching as
this? Am I ridden with a Welsh goat too? shall I
have a coxcomb of frize? 'Tis time I were choked
with a piece of toasted cheese.

speeches.speech: Seese is not good to give putter; your belly is all putter.
lines: Seese is not good to give putter; your belly is all putter.

speeches.speech: 'Seese' and 'putter'! have I lived to stand at the taunt of one that makes fritters of English? This is enough to be the decay of lust and late-walking through the realm.
lines: 'Seese' and 'putter'! have I lived to stand at the
taunt of one that makes fritters of English? This
is enough to be the decay of lust and late-walking
through the realm.

speeches.speech: Why Sir John, do you think, though we would have the virtue out of our hearts by the head and shoulders and have given ourselves without scruple to hell, that ever the devil could have made you our delight?
lines: Why Sir John, do you think, though we would have the
virtue out of our hearts by the head and shoulders
and have given ourselves without scruple to hell,
that ever the devil could have made you our delight?

speeches.speech: What, a hodge-pudding? a bag of flax?
lines: What, a hodge-pudding? a bag of flax?

speeches.speech: A puffed man?
lines: A puffed man?

speeches.speech: Old, cold, withered and of intolerable entrails?
lines: Old, cold, withered and of intolerable entrails?

speeches.speech: And one that is as slanderous as Satan?
lines: And one that is as slanderous as Satan?

speeches.speech: And as poor as Job?
lines: And as poor as Job?

speeches.speech: And as wicked as his wife?
lines: And as wicked as his wife?

speeches.speech: And given to fornications, and to taverns and sack and wine and metheglins, and to drinkings and swearings and starings, pribbles and prabbles?
lines: And given to fornications, and to taverns and sack
and wine and metheglins, and to drinkings and
swearings and starings, pribbles and prabbles?

speeches.speech: Well, I am your theme: you have the start of me; I am dejected; I am not able to answer the Welsh flannel; ignorance itself is a plummet o'er me: use me as you will.
lines: Well, I am your theme: you have the start of me; I
am dejected; I am not able to answer the Welsh
flannel; ignorance itself is a plummet o'er me: use
me as you will.

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, we'll bring you to Windsor, to one Master Brook, that you have cozened of money, to whom you should have been a pander: over and above that you have suffered, I think to repay that money will be a biting affliction.
lines: Marry, sir, we'll bring you to Windsor, to one
Master Brook, that you have cozened of money, to
whom you should have been a pander: over and above
that you have suffered, I think to repay that money
will be a biting affliction.

speeches.speech: Yet be cheerful, knight: thou shalt eat a posset to-night at my house; where I will desire thee to laugh at my wife, that now laughs at thee: tell her Master Slender hath married her daughter.
lines: Yet be cheerful, knight: thou shalt eat a posset
to-night at my house; where I will desire thee to
laugh at my wife, that now laughs at thee: tell her
Master Slender hath married her daughter.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Doctors doubt that: if Anne Page be my daughter, she is, by this, Doctor Caius' wife.
lines: [Aside] Doctors doubt that: if Anne Page be my
daughter, she is, by this, Doctor Caius' wife.

speeches.speech: Whoa ho! ho, father Page!
lines: Whoa ho! ho, father Page!

speeches.speech: Son, how now! how now, son! have you dispatched?
lines: Son, how now! how now, son! have you dispatched?

speeches.speech: Dispatched! I'll make the best in Gloucestershire know on't; would I were hanged, la, else.
lines: Dispatched! I'll make the best in Gloucestershire
know on't; would I were hanged, la, else.

speeches.speech: Of what, son?
lines: Of what, son?

speeches.speech: I came yonder at Eton to marry Mistress Anne Page, and she's a great lubberly boy. If it had not been i' the church, I would have swinged him, or he should have swinged me. If I did not think it had been Anne Page, would I might never stir!--and 'tis a postmaster's boy.
lines: I came yonder at Eton to marry Mistress Anne Page,
and she's a great lubberly boy. If it had not been
i' the church, I would have swinged him, or he
should have swinged me. If I did not think it had
been Anne Page, would I might never stir!--and 'tis
a postmaster's boy.

speeches.speech: Upon my life, then, you took the wrong.
lines: Upon my life, then, you took the wrong.

speeches.speech: What need you tell me that? I think so, when I took a boy for a girl. If I had been married to him, for all he was in woman's apparel, I would not have had him.
lines: What need you tell me that? I think so, when I took
a boy for a girl. If I had been married to him, for
all he was in woman's apparel, I would not have had
him.

speeches.speech: Why, this is your own folly. Did not I tell you how you should know my daughter by her garments?
lines: Why, this is your own folly. Did not I tell you how
you should know my daughter by her garments?

speeches.speech: I went to her in white, and cried 'mum,' and she cried 'budget,' as Anne and I had appointed; and yet it was not Anne, but a postmaster's boy.
lines: I went to her in white, and cried 'mum,' and she
cried 'budget,' as Anne and I had appointed; and yet
it was not Anne, but a postmaster's boy.

speeches.speech: Good George, be not angry: I knew of your purpose; turned my daughter into green; and, indeed, she is now with the doctor at the deanery, and there married.
lines: Good George, be not angry: I knew of your purpose;
turned my daughter into green; and, indeed, she is
now with the doctor at the deanery, and there married.

speeches.speech: Vere is Mistress Page? By gar, I am cozened: I ha' married un garcon, a boy; un paysan, by gar, a boy; it is not Anne Page: by gar, I am cozened.
lines: Vere is Mistress Page? By gar, I am cozened: I ha'
married un garcon, a boy; un paysan, by gar, a boy;
it is not Anne Page: by gar, I am cozened.

speeches.speech: Why, did you take her in green?
lines: Why, did you take her in green?

speeches.speech: Ay, by gar, and 'tis a boy: by gar, I'll raise all Windsor.
lines: Ay, by gar, and 'tis a boy: by gar, I'll raise all Windsor.

speeches.speech: This is strange. Who hath got the right Anne?
lines: This is strange. Who hath got the right Anne?

speeches.speech: My heart misgives me: here comes Master Fenton. [Enter FENTON and ANNE PAGE] How now, Master Fenton!
lines: My heart misgives me: here comes Master Fenton.
[Enter FENTON and ANNE PAGE]
How now, Master Fenton!

speeches.speech: Pardon, good father! good my mother, pardon!
lines: Pardon, good father! good my mother, pardon!

speeches.speech: Now, mistress, how chance you went not with Master Slender?
lines: Now, mistress, how chance you went not with Master Slender?

speeches.speech: Why went you not with master doctor, maid?
lines: Why went you not with master doctor, maid?

speeches.speech: You do amaze her: hear the truth of it. You would have married her most shamefully, Where there was no proportion held in love. The truth is, she and I, long since contracted, Are now so sure that nothing can dissolve us. The offence is holy that she hath committed; And this deceit loses the name of craft, Of disobedience, or unduteous title, Since therein she doth evitate and shun A thousand irreligious cursed hours, Which forced marriage would have brought upon her.
lines: You do amaze her: hear the truth of it.
You would have married her most shamefully,
Where there was no proportion held in love.
The truth is, she and I, long since contracted,
Are now so sure that nothing can dissolve us.
The offence is holy that she hath committed;
And this deceit loses the name of craft,
Of disobedience, or unduteous title,
Since therein she doth evitate and shun
A thousand irreligious cursed hours,
Which forced marriage would have brought upon her.

speeches.speech: Stand not amazed; here is no remedy: In love the heavens themselves do guide the state; Money buys lands, and wives are sold by fate.
lines: Stand not amazed; here is no remedy:
In love the heavens themselves do guide the state;
Money buys lands, and wives are sold by fate.

speeches.speech: I am glad, though you have ta'en a special stand to strike at me, that your arrow hath glanced.
lines: I am glad, though you have ta'en a special stand to
strike at me, that your arrow hath glanced.

speeches.speech: Well, what remedy? Fenton, heaven give thee joy! What cannot be eschew'd must be embraced.
lines: Well, what remedy? Fenton, heaven give thee joy!
What cannot be eschew'd must be embraced.

speeches.speech: When night-dogs run, all sorts of deer are chased.
lines: When night-dogs run, all sorts of deer are chased.

speeches.speech: Well, I will muse no further. Master Fenton, Heaven give you many, many merry days! Good husband, let us every one go home, And laugh this sport o'er by a country fire; Sir John and all.
lines: Well, I will muse no further. Master Fenton,
Heaven give you many, many merry days!
Good husband, let us every one go home,
And laugh this sport o'er by a country fire;
Sir John and all.

speeches.speech: Let it be so. Sir John, To Master Brook you yet shall hold your word For he tonight shall lie with Mistress Ford.
lines: Let it be so. Sir John,
To Master Brook you yet shall hold your word
For he tonight shall lie with Mistress Ford.
